At $(1,2)$ the velocity is $(1)\hat{i} + (-2)\hat{j}$, and the tangent direction of the curve $xy=2$ there is indeed proportional to this vector. **Examiner credit:** Full 7 marks if you show all six steps explicitly. ## 3.2 Derivation B — Velocity Components from the Stream Function **Given:** $\psi(x,y) = x^2 - y^2$ in a 2-D incompressible flow. **Find:** the velocity field, the vorticity, and verify continuity. ### Step 1 — Apply the stream function definition For 2-D incompressible flow: $$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute the partial derivatives $$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(x^2 - y^2) = -2y$$ $$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(x^2 - y^2) = 2x$$ ### Step 3 — Write the velocity field $$u = -2y, \qquad v = -2x$$ $$\vec{V} = -2y\,\hat{i} - 2x\,\hat{j}$$ ### Step 4 — Verify continuity (mass conservation) For incompressible 2-D flow, continuity requires $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y = 0$. $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(-2y) = 0$$ $$\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(-2x) = 0$$ Sum: $0 + 0 = 0$. **Continuity satisfied.** ✓ ### Step 5 — Compute the vorticity In 2-D, the only non-zero component of $\vec{\omega} = \nabla\times\vec{V}$ is the $z$-component: $$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$ $$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(-2x) = -2$$ $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(-2y) = -2$$ $$\omega_z = -2 - (-2) = 0$$ ### Step 6 — Physical interpretation Since vorticity is zero everywhere, the flow is **irrotational**, and a velocity potential $\phi$ exists. This is the classic *stagnation-point flow* or *hyperbolic flow* pattern, which appears at the bifurcation of large arteries where flow decelerates and diverges. ## 3.3 Derivation C — Substantial Derivative of a Scalar Field Carried by the Velocity Field **Setup:** A scalar concentration $c(x,y,z,t)$ (e.g., oxygen concentration in blood plasma) moves with the fluid. Show that the rate of change *following the fluid* is the material derivative. ### Step 1 — Total differential of $c$ as the fluid element moves If a fluid particle moves from $(x,y,z)$ at time $t$ to $(x+dx, y+dy, z+dz)$ at time $t+dt$, the change in $c$ is: $$dc = \frac{\partial c}{\partial t}\,dt + \frac{\partial c}{\partial x}\,dx + \frac{\partial c}{\partial y}\,dy + \frac{\partial c}{\partial z}\,dz$$ ### Step 2 — Divide through by $dt$ $$\frac{dc}{dt} = \frac{\partial c}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial c}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial c}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial c}{\partial z}\frac{dz}{dt}$$ ### Step 3 — Recognise the kinematic relations $$\frac{dx}{dt} = u, \quad \frac{dy}{dt} = v, \quad \frac{dz}{dt} = w$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute and recognise the operator $$\frac{dc}{dt} = \frac{\partial c}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial c}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial c}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial c}{\partial z}$$ In compact notation: $$\boxed{\,\frac{Dc}{Dt} = \frac{\partial c}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)c\,}$$ This is the *convective (or material) derivative* that reappears in every transport equation of this course. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Velocity-Field Problem Types in KTU | # | Problem Type | Given | Asked | Key Equation to Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Find streamline | $u(x,y), v(x,y)$ | Equation of streamline | $dy/dx = v/u$ | | 2 | Find pathline | $u, v$ time-dependent | Trajectory $x_p(t), y_p(t)$ | $dx_p/dt = u$ | | 3 | Verify incompressibility | $\vec{V}(x,y,z)$ | Is $\nabla\cdot\vec{V}=0$? | Sum partials | | 4 | Find stream function | Velocity field | $\psi(x,y)$ | $u=\partial\psi/\partial y$, $v=-\partial\psi/\partial x$ | | 5 | Find vorticity | $\vec{V}$ | $\vec{\omega}$ | $\vec{\omega}=\nabla\times\vec{V}$ | | 6 | Check if irrotational | $\vec{V}$ | Does $\phi$ exist? | $\nabla\times\vec{V}=0$ | | 7 | Material acceleration | $\vec{V}(x,y,z,t)$ | $D\vec{V}/Dt$ | $D/Dt = \partial/\partial t + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)$ | | 8 | Steady vs unsteady | $\vec{V}$ | Identify type | Check $\partial/\partial t$ | # Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Velocity Field ```mermaid flowchart TD A["DOMAIN Omega"] --> B["Eulerian Frame"] A --> C["Lagrangian Frame"] B --> D["Velocity Field V x y z t"] D --> D1["Scalar Components u v w"] D --> D2["Magnitude abs V"] D --> D3["Direction unit Vector"] D --> E["Time Behaviour"] D --> F["Spatial Behaviour"] E --> E1["Steady"] E --> E2["Unsteady"] F --> F1["Uniform"] F --> F2["Non Uniform"] D --> G["Geometric Lines"] G --> G1["Streamlines at fixed t"] G --> G2["Pathlines of single particle"] G --> G3["Streaklines past fixed point"] D --> H["Derived Quantities"] H --> H1["Vorticity curl V"] H --> H2["Strain Rate tensor"] H --> H3["Material Derivative D V over D t"] H --> H4["Stream Function Psi 2D"] H --> H5["Velocity Potential Phi irrotational"] ``` ## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Streamline vs Pathline vs Streakline ```mermaid flowchart TD S0["Start: Given Velocity Field V"] --> S1{"Is flow steady?
partial V over partial t equals 0"} S1 -- Yes --> S2["All three lines COINCIDE"] S1 -- No --> S3["Lines DIVERGE"] S3 --> Q1{"What do you need?"} Q1 -- "Snapshot at fixed t" --> A1["Compute STREAMLINE using dy over dx equals v over u at constant t"] Q1 -- "Trajectory of one particle" --> A2["Solve ODE dx_p over dt equals u x_p t for PATHLINE"] Q1 -- "Past particles through a point" --> A3["Integrate pathlines from earlier times to get STREAKLINE"] S2 --> A4["Choose any of the three methods; results identical"] ``` ## 4.3 Coordinate-System Mapping for Velocity Components ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Velocity Field V"] --> B["Cartesian x y z"] A --> C["Cylindrical r theta z"] A --> D["Spherical r theta phi"] B --> B1["u hat i plus v hat j plus w hat k"] C --> C1["v_r hat e_r plus v_theta hat e_theta plus v_z hat e_z"] D --> D1["v_r hat e_r plus v_theta hat e_theta plus v_phi hat e_phi"] B1 --> E["Use for: vessels along z-axis approximations"] C1 --> F["Use for: curved vessels, swirling ventricular flow"] D1 --> G["Use for: aerosol droplets, spherical organ models"] ``` ## 4.4 Block Architecture — How the Velocity Field Connects to All Later Modules ```mermaid flowchart TD M1["Module 1: Velocity Field V"] --> M2["Module 2: Conservation of Mass - Continuity Equation"] M1 --> M3["Module 3: Conservation of Momentum - Navier Stokes"] M1 --> M4["Module 4: Energy Transport - Heat Equation"] M1 --> M5["Module 5: Mass Transport - Diffusion Convection"] M2 --> M2a["Used in: Blood volume balance, lung ventilation"] M3 --> M3a["Used in: Wall shear stress, stent design"] M4 --> M4a["Used in: Tissue heating by implants, cryopreservation"] M5 --> M5a["Used in: Oxygen delivery, drug diffusion through arterial wall"] ``` > [!TIP] > **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** In Part-B questions, drawing a small labelled block diagram of *how* your derived velocity field feeds into the next equation earns you easy "presentation marks" — examiners explicitly award 1–2 marks for neat, relevant diagrams. # Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **"Define a velocity field. Distinguish clearly between a streamline and a pathline, stating the condition under which they coincide."** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** A *velocity field* is a vector function $\vec{V}(x,y,z,t)$ that gives the velocity of the fluid at every spatial point and at every instant of time. - A **streamline** is a curve drawn at a *fixed instant* such that its tangent at every point is parallel to the local velocity vector. - A **pathline** is the *actual trajectory* traced by an individual fluid particle over a time interval. **Condition of coincidence:** When the flow is *steady* ($\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$), streamlines, pathlines, and streaklines are all identical. In unsteady flow they generally differ. *(Examiner credit: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for coincidence condition.)* ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **"For a 2-D incompressible flow, define the stream function $\psi(x,y)$ and show that its existence automatically guarantees mass conservation."** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **Definition:** The stream function $\psi(x,y)$ is a scalar function such that $$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$ **Proof of automatic mass conservation:** $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\partial^2\psi}{\partial x\,\partial y} - \frac{\partial^2\psi}{\partial y\,\partial x} = 0$$ by equality of mixed partials (Clairaut's theorem, valid for smooth $\psi$). This is the continuity equation for incompressible 2-D flow, hence mass conservation is guaranteed. ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT mix streamline and pathline equations.** Streamlines use $dy/dx = v/u$ *at fixed $t$*; pathlines use $dx_p/dt = u(t)$ — different mathematics, different answers. > 2. **Always state the steady-flow assumption** when invoking the coincidence of lines, or you lose 1 mark. > 3. **In stream-function problems, write the defining equations** $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ — examiners *expect* them on the first line. > 4. **Units must appear** in any numerical answer — examiners actively deduct for missing units in the final box. > 5. **Show all intermediate derivatives** — partial-credit marks are awarded for each visible step. ### Question A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 pattern] **(a)** Given the 2-D velocity field $\vec{V} = (3 + 2x)\,\hat{i} + (1 - 2y)\,\hat{j}$ m·s⁻¹: - (i) Determine whether the flow is steady or unsteady. *(2 marks)* - (ii) Test for incompressibility. *(2 marks)* - (iii) Derive the equation of the streamline passing through the point $(1, 1)$. *(3 marks)* **(b)** A catheter delivers a fluid whose velocity field inside the lumen is approximated as $\vec{V} = (0.5\,\sin(\pi x/L))\,\hat{i}$ for $0 \le x \le L$, with $L = 0.10$ m. The fluid carries a drug of concentration $c(x,t)$. Find the material derivative $Dc/Dt$ at $x = L/4$ and $t = 2$ s, given that $\partial c/\partial t = 0.1$ mol·m⁻³·s⁻¹, $\partial c/\partial x = -2.0$ mol·m⁻⁴ at that instant. *(7 marks)* --- ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a)(i): Steady or Unsteady? The velocity components $u = 3 + 2x$ and $v = 1 - 2y$ contain **no explicit time dependence**. Therefore: $$\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0$$ **Answer: The flow is steady.** *(2 marks)* #### Part (a)(ii): Incompressibility Test For incompressibility, compute the divergence: $$\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(3 + 2x) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(1 - 2y) = 2 + (-2) = 0$$ **Answer: The flow is incompressible.** *(2 marks)* #### Part (a)(iii): Streamline Through (1, 1) Apply the streamline relation: $$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{v}{u} = \frac{1 - 2y}{3 + 2x}$$ Separate variables: $$(1 - 2y)\,dx = (3 + 2x)\,dy$$ Better — rewrite as a proper separable form: $$\frac{dy}{1 - 2y} = \frac{dx}{3 + 2x}$$ Integrate both sides: $$\int \frac{dy}{1 - 2y} = \int \frac{dx}{3 + 2x}$$ $$-\tfrac{1}{2}\ln\mid 1 - 2y \mid = \tfrac{1}{2}\ln\mid 3 + 2x \mid + C_1$$ Multiply by 2: $$\ln\mid 1 - 2y \mid + \ln\mid 3 + 2x \mid = C_2$$ $$\ln\bigl(\mid (3 + 2x)(1 - 2y) \mid\bigr) = C_2$$ $$(3 + 2x)(1 - 2y) = K$$ Apply $(x,y) = (1,1)$: $(3 + 2)(1 - 2) = (5)(-1) = -5$, so $K = -5$. $$\boxed{\,(3 + 2x)(1 - 2y) = -5\,}$$ or equivalently $6y - 4xy = 8$, i.e. $y(3 - 2x) = 4$. *(3 marks)* #### Part (b): Material Derivative of Drug Concentration The material derivative for a scalar field is: $$\frac{Dc}{Dt} = \frac{\partial c}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial c}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial c}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial c}{\partial z}$$ **Given data at $x = L/4 = 0.025$ m, $t = 2$ s:** - $u = 0.5 \sin(\pi x / L) = 0.5 \sin(\pi \cdot 0.025/0.10) = 0.5 \sin(\pi/4) = 0.5 \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 0.354$ m·s⁻¹ - $v = 0$, $w = 0$ (1-D flow) - $\partial c/\partial t = 0.1$ mol·m⁻³·s⁻¹ - $\partial c/\partial x = -2.0$ mol·m⁻⁴ - No $y$ or $z$ dependence given, so $\partial c/\partial y = \partial c/\partial z = 0$. **Substitute:** $$\frac{Dc}{Dt} = 0.1 + (0.354)(-2.0) + 0 + 0$$ $$\frac{Dc}{Dt} = 0.1 - 0.707 = -0.607 \;\text{mol·m}^{-3}\text{·s}^{-1}$$ $$\boxed{\,\dfrac{Dc}{Dt} \approx -0.61\ \text{mol·m}^{-3}\text{·s}^{-1}\,}$$ *(7 marks: 2 for operator, 2 for velocity evaluation, 2 for substitution, 1 for final numerical answer with units.)* --- ### Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 pattern] **(a)** For the velocity field $\vec{V} = -2xy\,\hat{i} + (y^2 - x^2)\,\hat{j}$ m·s⁻¹: - (i) Show that the flow is incompressible. *(2 marks)* - (ii) Compute the vorticity $\vec{\omega}$. *(2 marks)* - (iii) Find the stream function $\psi(x,y)$. *(3 marks)* **(b)** Blood flowing in a long artery has a velocity profile approximated by the steady field $\vec{V} = U_{\max}(1 - (r/R)^2)\,\hat{e}_z$, where $r$ is the radial coordinate, $R$ is the vessel radius, and $U_{\max} = 0.4$ m·s⁻¹. A red blood cell is at $r = 0.5R$ at $t = 0$. - (i) Show that the cell's axial position evolves as $z_p(t) = U_{\max}(1 - (r_0/R)^2)\,t$ (assume radial position remains constant for this idealised profile). *(3 marks)* - (ii) Calculate the axial displacement after $t = 0.5$ s. *(2 marks)* - (iii) Calculate the wall shear rate and comment on its physiological relevance. *(2 marks)* --- ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a)(i): Incompressibility $$\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(-2xy) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(y^2 - x^2) = -2y + 2y = 0$$ **Flow is incompressible.** ✓ *(2 marks)* #### Part (a)(ii): Vorticity Only $z$-component in 2-D: $$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(y^2 - x^2) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(-2xy)$$ $$= -2x - (-2x) = 0$$ **Vorticity is zero everywhere; the flow is irrotational.** *(2 marks)* #### Part (a)(iii): Stream Function Use $u = \partial\psi/\partial y = -2xy$: $$\psi = \int -2xy\,dy = -xy^2 + f(x)$$ Apply $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x = y^2 - x^2$: $$-\frac{\partial\psi}{\partial x} = y^2 - x^2 \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{\partial\psi}{\partial x} = x^2 - y^2$$ From $\psi = -xy^2 + f(x)$: $$\frac{\partial\psi}{\partial x} = -y^2 + f'(x) = x^2 - y^2 \;\Longrightarrow\; f'(x) = x^2$$ $$f(x) = \frac{x^3}{3} + C$$ Take $C = 0$: $$\boxed{\,\psi(x,y) = \frac{x^3}{3} - xy^2\,}$$ **Verification:** $u = \partial\psi/\partial y = -2xy$ ✓ ; $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x = -(x^2 - y^2) = y^2 - x^2$ ✓ *(3 marks)* #### Part (b)(i): Pathline of Red Blood Cell Since the velocity field is purely axial and the cell remains at $r_0 = 0.5R$: $$\frac{dz_p}{dt} = U_{\max}\!\left(1 - \left(\frac{r_0}{R}\right)^{\!2}\right) = U_{\max}\!\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{4}\right) = 0.75\,U_{\max}$$ Integrating with $z_p(0) = 0$: $$z_p(t) = 0.75\,U_{\max}\,t$$ ✓ *(3 marks)* #### Part (b)(ii): Displacement at $t = 0.5$ s $$z_p(0.5) = 0.75 \times 0.4 \times 0.5 = 0.15\ \text{m}$$ $$\boxed{\,z_p = 0.15\ \text{m} = 15\ \text{cm}\,}$$ *(2 marks: 1 for substitution, 1 for numerical answer with units.)* #### Part (b)(iii): Wall Shear Rate The wall shear rate is $\dot\gamma_w = \vert\partial v_z/\partial r\vert_{r=R}$: $$\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} = U_{\max}\cdot\left(-\frac{2r}{R^2}\right) = -\frac{2U_{\max}\,r}{R^2}$$ At $r = R$: $$\dot\gamma_w = \frac{2U_{\max}}{R}$$ Taking $R = 2.5$ mm $= 2.5\times 10^{-3}$ m (typical carotid artery): $$\dot\gamma_w = \frac{2 \times 0.4}{2.5\times 10^{-3}} = 320\ \text{s}^{-1}$$ **Physiological relevance:** This wall shear rate corresponds to a wall shear stress of $\tau_w = \mu\dot\gamma_w \approx 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 320 \approx 1.1$ Pa (using blood viscosity $\mu \approx 3.5$ mPa·s). This is within the physiological range (1–2 Pa) that maintains endothelial cell health; sustained deviations are linked to atherosclerosis. *(2 marks)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Before you walk into the exam, tick every box below:** - [ ] **Definition mastered:** $\vec{V}(x,y,z,t) = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$ — write it cold in 10 seconds. - [ ] **Eulerian vs Lagrangian:** Eulerian = fixed spatial frame → velocity field; Lagrangian = tagged particle → pathline. - [ ] **Steady flow = streamline = pathline = streakline.** Remember to *state* $\partial\vec{V}/\partial t = 0$ before invoking this. - [ ] **Streamline formula (2-D):** $dy/dx = v/u$ at fixed $t$. Apply initial condition to find integration constant. - [ ] **Stream function:** $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$; auto-satisfies 2-D incompressible continuity. - [ ] **Velocity potential:** $\vec{V} = \nabla\phi$ exists iff $\nabla\times\vec{V} = 0$. - [ ] **Vorticity:** $\vec{\omega} = \nabla\times\vec{V}$; zero vorticity ≠ zero velocity; the field can still be moving but irrotational. - [ ] **Material derivative:** $D/Dt = \partial/\partial t + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)$; appears in every transport equation. - [ ] **Continuity (incompressible):** $\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = 0$; in Cartesian: $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y + \partial w/\partial z = 0$. - [ ] **Coordinate systems:** Cartesian, cylindrical, spherical — know when to use each (vessels → cylindrical, droplets → spherical). - [ ] **Physiological velocity benchmarks:** aorta ≈ 1 m·s⁻¹, trachea ≈ 0.2 m·s⁻¹, capillary ≈ 0.0005 m·s⁻¹. - [ ] **Pitfall guard:** Never confuse streamlines with pathlines in unsteady flow. Always state the assumption. - [ ] **Pitfall guard:** Never omit units in the final numerical box — automatic ½–1 mark deduction per KTU valuation key. - [ ] **Pitfall guard:** In stream-function derivations, show the *integration constant* explicitly and apply boundary conditions; never just write the answer.

topics.title: Flow Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT832 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flow Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Flow Rate

In **Biomedical Transport Phenomena**, the term **Flow Rate** (also called **Volumetric Flow Rate** or **Discharge**) is formally defined as the **volume of fluid (gas or liquid) that passes through a given cross-sectional area per unit time**. It is a fundamental macroscopic descriptor of the bulk transport of momentum (and incidentally, of mass and energy) in a fluid continuum.

Mathematically, the **volumetric flow rate** $Q$ is expressed as:

$$Q = \frac{V}{t} = A \cdot \bar{v}$$

where:
- $V$ is the fluid volume (in **m³** or **cm³**)
- $t$ is the time interval (in **seconds**)
- $A$ is the cross-sectional area perpendicular to the flow direction (in **m²** or **cm²**)
- $\bar{v}$ is the **mean (average) velocity** of the fluid across that area (in **m/s** or **cm/s**)

A closely related quantity is the **Mass Flow Rate** ($\dot{m}$), which measures the mass transported per unit time:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = \rho \cdot A \cdot \bar{v}$$

where $\rho$ is the fluid density (in **kg/m³**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The distinction between **Volumetric Flow Rate** ($Q$) and **Mass Flow Rate** ($\dot{m}$) is a frequently tested concept. The conversion between them hinges on the fluid density $\rho$. For incompressible fluids (like water and most physiological liquids in slow flow), $\rho$ is constant, making $Q$ a conserved quantity in a streamline.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on the bank of a river, watching water flow past you. The "flow rate" of the river is essentially the question: *"How much water passes by me in one second?"* It is not just about how fast the top layer moves; it also depends on the **width and depth** of the river. A wide, slow river can deliver just as much water (have the same flow rate) as a narrow, fast stream.

Now, replace the river with a **human blood vessel (artery, vein, or capillary)**. The "flow rate" tells you how much blood (in milliliters) is being delivered by your heart to your organs every minute. This is clinically critical because **cardiac output** is literally the volumetric flow rate of blood pumped by the left ventricle per minute (typically **5 L/min** at rest).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Flow rate is the *product* of two things — *how wide the pipe is* ($A$) and *how fast the fluid is moving* ($\bar{v}$). Change either one, and the flow rate changes.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | CGS Unit | Typical Biomedical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q$ | **m³/s** | **cm³/s** | Blood: $\approx 83$ cm³/s (cardiac output) |
| Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m}$ | **kg/s** | **g/s** | Aorta blood: $\approx 0.1$ kg/s |
| Fluid Density | $\rho$ | **kg/m³** | **g/cm³** | Blood plasma: $\approx 1060$ kg/m³ |
| Mean Velocity | $\bar{v}$ | **m/s** | **cm/s** | Aorta peak: $\approx 0.5$ m/s |
| Cross-sectional Area | $A$ | **m²** | **cm²** | Aorta: $\approx 4.5$ cm² |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Universal Gas Constant** ($R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)) and **Standard Gravity** ($g = 9.81$ m/s²) are often used in flow energy and manometry calculations, although the primary constants in the Flow Rate topic are density and viscosity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Differential Form of Flow Rate

For a fluid whose velocity varies across the cross-section (which is *always* the case in viscous flow), the volumetric flow rate is computed by integrating the local velocity $v(r)$ over the cross-sectional area:

$$Q = \int_{A} \vec{v} \cdot d\vec{A}$$

This is the **most general definition** of $Q$ in continuum mechanics, derived from the Reynolds Transport Theorem.

### Key Steps in the Logic:
1. **Local velocity** $v(r)$ at a point on the cross-section may differ from the centerline velocity.
2. To get total volume, sum (integrate) the contribution from every differential patch $dA$.
3. The **dot product** $\vec{v} \cdot d\vec{A}$ ensures that only the component of velocity *normal* to the surface contributes.
4. The result, when divided by the total area, gives the **mean velocity** $\bar{v}$.

## 2.2 The Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)

For a **steady-state, incompressible flow** through a control volume, the mass entering must equal the mass leaving:

$$Q_1 = Q_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_1 \bar{v}_1 = A_2 \bar{v}_2$$

This is the **Continuity Equation** — the most fundamental principle in fluid transport.

### Why does it matter?
- In a **narrowing artery** (e.g., a stenosis), the area $A$ decreases, so velocity $\bar{v}$ must *increase*.
- This is why **blood jets** out of a cut finger — the constriction of the capillary accelerates the flow.
- This is also the basis of the **Venturi effect** used in medical devices (e.g., oxygen masks).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "derive or state the continuity equation," always specify the underlying assumptions: **steady state, incompressible flow, single inlet, single outlet, no accumulation in the control volume.**

## 2.3 Types of Flow Regimes

The character of the flow rate depends on the **Reynolds Number** ($Re$), a dimensionless group:

$$Re = \frac{\rho \bar{v} D}{\mu} = \frac{\bar{v} D}{\nu}$$

where:
- $D$ = characteristic length (pipe diameter)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $\nu = \mu / \rho$ = kinematic viscosity (m²/s)

| Flow Regime | Reynolds Number Range | Velocity Profile | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Laminar** | $Re < 2100$ | Parabolic (smooth layers) | Blood in small arterioles |
| **Transitional** | $2100 \le Re \le 4000$ | Unstable, intermittent | Disturbed flow at bifurcations |
| **Turbulent** | $Re > 4000$ | Chaotic, mixing | Aortic flow during peak systole |

> [!NOTE]
> For blood flow in vessels, the critical Reynolds number is often taken lower (**$\approx 2000$**) because of plasma skimming, red blood cell aggregation, and vessel curvature. In *large vessels* (aorta, vena cava), turbulent flow can occur briefly during exercise.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables / Units | Boundary Conditions / Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q = A \bar{v}$ | $Q$ in m³/s, $A$ in m², $\bar{v}$ in m/s | Steady, uniform, fully-developed flow |
| 2 | Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m} = \rho Q = \rho A \bar{v}$ | $\dot{m}$ in kg/s, $\rho$ in kg/m³ | Any steady flow |
| 3 | General Integral Form | $Q = \int_A \vec{v} \cdot d\vec{A}$ | Vector form | All flow regimes |
| 4 | Continuity Equation | $A_1 \bar{v}_1 = A_2 \bar{v}_2$ | Incompressible, steady | No mass accumulation |
| 5 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \bar{v} D}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless | L, T, and DT independent of system size |
| 6 | Hagen-Poiseuille Law | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P \, r^4}{8 \mu L}$ | $\Delta P$ in Pa, $r$ in m, $L$ in m | Laminar, Newtonian, fully developed, rigid tube |
| 7 | Mean Velocity (Hagen-Poiseuille) | $\bar{v} = \dfrac{\Delta P \, r^2}{8 \mu L}$ | m/s | Same as above |
| 8 | Shear Stress (Newtonian) | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{dv}{dy}$ | Pa | Newtonian fluid only |
| 9 | Wall Shear Stress (Poiseuille) | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \, r}{2 L}$ | Pa | Laminar pipe flow |
| 10 | Volumetric Flow per Unit Length | $Q' = \dfrac{Q}{L}$ | m²/s | Useful for flow through porous media |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Hemodynamics:** Calculating cardiac output, perfusion rates, drug delivery via IV.
- **Respiratory Therapy:** Oxygen flow rates through ventilators and nasal cannulae.
- **Dialysis Machines:** Flow rate of dialysate through the extracorporeal circuit.
- **Pharmaceutical Manufacturing:** Ensuring correct dosing rates through microfluidic channels.
- **BioMEMS / Lab-on-Chip:** Designing microchannels where $Q$ controls reaction kinetics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Continuity Equation (Integral Form → Differential Form)

**Goal:** Derive the mass conservation relation for a control volume.

### Step 1: Define the control volume
Let $CV$ be a fixed region in space bounded by a control surface $CS$. The fluid mass inside is:

$$m_{CV} = \int_{CV} \rho \, dV$$

### Step 2: Mass balance (Reynolds Transport Theorem)
The rate of accumulation of mass equals the net inflow:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV = -\int_{CS} \rho \vec{v} \cdot \hat{n} \, dA$$

where $\hat{n}$ is the outward normal.

### Step 3: Apply the Divergence Theorem
Convert the surface integral to a volume integral:

$$\int_{CS} \rho \vec{v} \cdot \hat{n} \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \, dV$$

### Step 4: Combine the results
Substituting back:

$$\int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \right] dV = 0$$

### Step 5: Apply Locality (arbitrary CV)
Since $CV$ is arbitrary, the integrand itself must vanish:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$$

This is the **continuity equation** in differential form.

### Step 6: Specialize to Incompressible, Steady Flow
For $\rho = $ constant and $\partial / \partial t = 0$:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$$

For a 1-D pipe:

$$A_1 \bar{v}_1 = A_2 \bar{v}_2 = Q = \text{constant}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance of the $r^4$ dependence:** Because flow rate in a pipe varies as the **fourth power of the radius**, even a tiny change in vessel radius has an enormous effect on blood flow. This is the foundation of the **resistance-vessel** concept in medicine: arterioles control blood flow by vasoconstriction (small reduction in $r$ → large drop in $Q$).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hagen-Poiseuille Equation (Flow Rate through a Cylindrical Tube)

**Goal:** Derive an expression for $Q$ in fully-developed, laminar, Newtonian, steady flow through a straight, horizontal, rigid cylindrical tube of radius $R$ and length $L$, under a pressure drop $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$.

### Step 1: Governing differential equation
The Navier–Stokes equation for fully-developed axial flow $v_z(r)$ reduces to a balance between the axial pressure gradient and the radial gradient of viscous stress:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{dP}{dz}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the constant pressure gradient
Since $P$ varies linearly in $z$ for fully-developed flow, let $dP/dz = -\Delta P / L$:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) = -\frac{\Delta P}{\mu L}$$

### Step 3: First integration
Multiply both sides by $r$ and integrate:

$$\int \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) dr = -\int \frac{\Delta P}{\mu L} r \, dr$$

$$r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} r^2 + C_1$$

### Step 4: Apply the axisymmetry boundary condition
The velocity profile must be finite at $r = 0$. For the equation to remain finite, $C_1 = 0$:

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} r$$

### Step 5: Second integration

$$\int dv_z = -\int \frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} r \, dr$$

$$v_z(r) = -\frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} r^2 + C_2$$

### Step 6: Apply the no-slip boundary condition
At the tube wall, $r = R$ and $v_z = 0$:

$$0 = -\frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} R^2 + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{4 \mu L}$$

### Step 7: Final velocity profile

$$v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (R^2 - r^2)$$

This is a **parabolic (Poiseuille) profile**, with maximum velocity $v_{max} = \Delta P R^2 / (4 \mu L)$ at the centerline.

### Step 8: Integrate to get the volumetric flow rate

$$Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \, (2 \pi r) \, dr = 2 \pi \int_0^R \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (R^2 - r^2) \, r \, dr$$

### Step 9: Pull out the constants

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) \, dr$$

### Step 10: Evaluate the integral

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[ \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left( \frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4} \right)$$

### Step 11: Simplify

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8 \mu L}$$

### Step 12: Mean velocity

$$\bar{v} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2} = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{8 \mu L} = \frac{v_{max}}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Takeaway:** The mean velocity in Poiseuille flow is exactly **half** the centerline maximum velocity — a direct consequence of the parabolic shape. This is a favorite short-answer question.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Derivation of Hydraulic Resistance

**Goal:** Express the Hagen-Poiseuille law in the form of an Ohm's-law analog.

Starting from:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8 \mu L}$$

Rearrange to identify a "resistance" $R_h$:

$$\Delta P = Q \cdot R_h \quad \text{with} \quad R_h = \frac{8 \mu L}{\pi R^4}$$

This is the **hydraulic (flow) resistance** of a cylindrical tube.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomedical Analogy:** Vascular resistance is the **hydraulic analog of electrical resistance** (Ohm's law $\Delta V = I \cdot R$). Just as resistors in parallel reduce total resistance, **parallel capillary beds** in the circulatory system dramatically lower the total vascular resistance, enabling high total flow rates.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Forms of Flow Rate

| Aspect | Integral Form | Differential Form | Poiseuille Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Expression** | $Q = \int_A \vec{v} \cdot d\vec{A}$ | $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$ (incompressible) | $Q = \pi \Delta P R^4 / (8 \mu L)$ |
| **Best used for** | Computing $Q$ from a known velocity field | Governing field equations | Quick engineering estimates |
| **Assumptions** | None (general) | Steady, incompressible | Laminar, Newtonian, rigid tube |
| **Includes viscosity?** | No (only geometry + velocity) | No (continuity is kinematic) | **Yes** — explicitly $\mu$ |
| **KTU level** | Conceptual | Full derivation expected | Numerical problems expected |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Flow Rate Analysis

The following diagram maps the logical workflow a Biomedical Engineer follows when computing and analyzing a flow rate problem — from raw physical setup to diagnostic interpretation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Setup Identification] --> B[Fluid Type Classification]
    B --> C{Newtonian?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Apply Newtonian Constitutive Law]
    C -- No --> E[Use Non-Newtonian Viscosity Model]
    B --> F[Flow Regime Classification]
    F --> G{Compute Reynolds Number}
    G --> H{Re less than 2100}
    H -- Yes --> I[Laminar Regime]
    H -- No --> J[Turbulent Regime]
    I --> K[Use Hagen-Poiseuille Equation]
    J --> L[Use Empirical Friction Factor Correlations]
    E --> M[Apply Power Law or Carreau Model]
    D --> K
    K --> N[Compute Volumetric Flow Rate Q]
    M --> N
    L --> N
    N --> O[Validate via Continuity Equation]
    O --> P[Biomedical Interpretation Output]
    P --> Q[Cardiac Output]
    P --> R[Perfusion Rate]
    P --> S[Drug Delivery Rate]
    P --> T[Dialysate Flow]
```

## 4.2 Concept Map: Flow Rate and Its Governing Relations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Cross Sectional Area A] --> C1[Volumetric Flow Rate Q]
    A2[Mean Velocity v] --> C1
    A3[Fluid Density rho] --> M1[Mass Flow Rate mdot]
    C1 --> M1
    B1[Pressure Drop deltaP] --> C1
    B2[Tube Radius R] --> C1
    B3[Viscosity mu] --> C1
    B4[Tube Length L] --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Continuity Equation A1v1 equals A2v2]
    C1 --> D2[Reynolds Number Re]
    C1 --> D3[Hydraulic Resistance Rh]
    D2 --> E1{Laminar or Turbulent}
    D3 --> E2[Ohms Law Analog]
    E1 --> F1[Poiseuille or Darcy Weisbach]
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Clinical / Engineering Decision]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Velocity Profile Evolution in a Pipe

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Uniform Inlet Profile] --> P2[Entry Region Developing Flow]
    P2 --> P3[Fully Developed Parabolic Profile]
    P3 --> P4[Maximum Velocity at Centerline]
    P3 --> P5[Zero Velocity at Wall No Slip]
    P4 --> Q1[Mean Velocity equals half of Vmax]
    P5 --> R1[Wall Shear Stress tau_w]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Note:** In the **entry region** of a tube, the flow profile is flat (plug-like) at the inlet and gradually develops into the parabolic Poiseuille shape after a distance called the **hydrodynamic entry length** ($L_e \approx 0.06 \cdot Re \cdot D$ for laminar flow). In short vessels (e.g., capillaries) where $L < L_e$, the flow never becomes fully developed, and Poiseuille's law is a poor approximation.

## 4.4 Decision Tree: When to Use Which Flow Rate Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Given Flow Problem] --> Q1{Is the fluid Newtonian?}
    Q1 -- No --> Branch1[Use Power Law or Apparent Viscosity]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the flow laminar?}
    Q2 -- No --> Branch2[Use Fanning or Darcy Friction Factor]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is geometry a straight tube?}
    Q3 -- No --> Branch3[Use empirical correlations or CFD]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Is the tube rigid?}
    Q4 -- No --> Branch4[Account for compliance and pulsatility]
    Q4 -- Yes --> Final[Apply Hagen-Poiseuille Q equals pi deltaP R4 over 8 mu L]
    Branch1 --> Final2[Numerical or iterative solution]
    Branch2 --> Final2
    Branch3 --> Final2
    Branch4 --> Final2
    Final --> End[Compute Q and Interpret]
    Final2 --> End
```
