# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define volumetric flow rate and mass flow rate. How are they related for an incompressible fluid?

**Model Answer:**
The **volumetric flow rate** $Q$ is defined as the volume of fluid passing through a given cross-section per unit time, expressed in **m³/s** (SI) or **cm³/s** (CGS):

$$Q = \frac{V}{t} = A \cdot \bar{v}$$

The **mass flow rate** $\dot{m}$ is the mass of fluid passing through a given cross-section per unit time, in **kg/s**:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{m}{t} = \rho A \bar{v} = \rho Q$$

**Relationship for an incompressible fluid:** Since $\rho$ is constant,

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q \quad \text{and} \quad Q = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho}$$

For example, blood in a healthy aorta with $\rho = 1060$ kg/m³ and cardiac output $Q = 5 \text{ L/min} = 8.33 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s gives $\dot{m} \approx 0.088$ kg/s.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** State the continuity equation for steady, incompressible flow. Mention its physical significance.

**Model Answer:**
The **continuity equation** for steady, incompressible, single-stream flow is:

$$A_1 \bar{v}_1 = A_2 \bar{v}_2 = Q = \text{constant}$$

**Physical Significance:**
1. It expresses the **conservation of mass** (and, for incompressible fluids, conservation of volume).
2. It indicates that **velocity is inversely proportional to area**: fluid speeds up in constricted regions and slows in expanded ones.
3. **Biomedical Examples:** (i) Blood accelerates through a stenotic artery; (ii) Air velocity increases during inhalation through the trachea as the airway branches narrow in total area; (iii) Venous blood slows as it enters the larger right atrium.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Hagen-Poiseuille equation for the volumetric flow rate of a Newtonian fluid through a cylindrical tube. Clearly state the assumptions.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A biomedical device delivers a saline solution ($\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) through a rigid tube of length $L = 0.5$ m and inner radius $R = 1.5$ mm. The pressure drop across the tube is $\Delta P = 8000$ Pa. Calculate (i) the volumetric flow rate $Q$, (ii) the mean velocity $\bar{v}$, (iii) the Reynolds number, and (iv) comment on the flow regime.

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Derivation

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state flow.
2. Incompressible, Newtonian fluid (constant $\mu$).
3. Fully-developed, laminar, axisymmetric flow.
4. Horizontal rigid tube (no gravity component along axis).
5. No-slip at the wall.
6. Negligible entrance effects.

**Governing equation** — from the $z$-component of Navier-Stokes for fully-developed flow:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{dP}{dz}$$

Since the pressure drops linearly along the tube:

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) = -\frac{\Delta P}{\mu L}$$

**First integration** (multiply by $r$, integrate):

$$r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} r^2 + C_1$$

By **axisymmetry** (finite velocity at $r = 0$): $C_1 = 0$.

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} r$$

**Second integration:**

$$v_z(r) = -\frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} r^2 + C_2$$

By **no-slip** at $r = R$: $v_z(R) = 0$:

$$C_2 = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{4 \mu L}$$

**Velocity profile:**

$$v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (R^2 - r^2)$$

**Volumetric flow rate** by integration:

$$Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \, 2 \pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8 \mu L}$$

This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Numerical Calculation

**Given:** $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $L = 0.5$ m, $R = 1.5$ mm $= 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\Delta P = 8000$ Pa.

**(i) Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8 \mu L}$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \times 8000 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^4}{8 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.5}$$

Compute numerator:

$$(1.5 \times 10^{-3})^4 = 5.0625 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^4$$

$$\text{Numerator} = \pi \times 8000 \times 5.0625 \times 10^{-12} = 1.272 \times 10^{-7}$$

Compute denominator:

$$8 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.5 = 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

Therefore:

$$Q = \frac{1.272 \times 10^{-7}}{4.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.18 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 31.8 \text{ mL/s}$$

**(ii) Mean velocity:**

$$\bar{v} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2} = \frac{3.18 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2} = \frac{3.18 \times 10^{-5}}{7.069 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\bar{v} \approx 4.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho \bar{v} D}{\mu} = \frac{\rho \bar{v} (2R)}{\mu}$$

$$Re = \frac{1000 \times 4.5 \times (2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3})}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \frac{1000 \times 4.5 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 13500$$

**(iv) Flow regime comment:**

Since $Re = 13{,}500 > 4000$, the flow is **turbulent**. Therefore, **Hagen-Poiseuille is not strictly valid** for this case — the actual flow rate would be lower than predicted by the Poiseuille formula. This is a common clinical scenario in high-flow IV lines and extracorporeal circuits.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Module Choice — **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **continuity equation** in differential form from a mass balance on a fixed control volume. Reduce it to the form $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ for 1-D, incompressible, steady flow.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A horizontal artery bifurcates into two identical branches. The main artery has diameter $D_1 = 1.2$ cm with mean velocity $v_1 = 0.4$ m/s. Each branch has diameter $D_2 = 0.8$ cm. Assuming steady, incompressible flow and no leakage, calculate (i) the total cross-sectional area of the two branches combined, (ii) the mean velocity in each branch, and (iii) verify continuity.

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Derivation of Differential Continuity

**Step 1: Mass inside control volume:**

$$m_{CV} = \int_{CV} \rho \, dV$$

**Step 2: Mass balance:**

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV = -\oint_{CS} \rho \vec{v} \cdot \hat{n} \, dA$$

**Step 3: Apply Divergence Theorem:**

$$-\oint_{CS} \rho \vec{v} \cdot \hat{n} \, dA = -\int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \, dV$$

**Step 4: Combine and simplify:**

$$\int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) \right] dV = 0$$

**Step 5: Arbitrary CV implies:**

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$$

**Step 6: For incompressible, steady flow** ($\rho = $ const, $\partial / \partial t = 0$):

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$$

**Step 7: For 1-D flow** (single velocity component $v_z$ depending only on $z$ and on transverse coordinates integrated out):

$$A \bar{v} = \text{constant}$$

$$\boxed{A_1 \bar{v}_1 = A_2 \bar{v}_2}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Numerical Calculation

**Given:** $D_1 = 1.2$ cm $= 0.012$ m, $\bar{v}_1 = 0.4$ m/s, $D_2 = 0.8$ cm $= 0.008$ m, two identical branches.

**(i) Total cross-sectional area of branches:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi D_1^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.012)^2}{4} = 1.131 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{\text{single branch}} = \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.008)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{2,\text{total}} = 2 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-5} = 1.005 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**(ii) Mean velocity in each branch:**

By continuity: $A_1 \bar{v}_1 = A_{2,\text{total}} \bar{v}_2$:

$$\bar{v}_2 = \frac{A_1 \bar{v}_1}{A_{2,\text{total}}} = \frac{1.131 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4}{1.005 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\bar{v}_2 \approx 0.45 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Verify continuity:**

- Main artery: $Q_1 = A_1 \bar{v}_1 = 1.131 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4 = 4.524 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s
- Both branches: $Q_2 = A_{2,\text{total}} \bar{v}_2 = 1.005 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.45 = 4.524 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s
- $Q_1 = Q_2$ ✓ — Continuity verified.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to double** the branch area in part (b)(i) of Question B. Many students compute the velocity in a *single* branch, then forget that the total downstream area is $2 A_2$.
> 2. **Unit errors:** $R$ in Hagen-Poiseuille must be in **meters**, not cm or mm. A 1000× error is the most common cause of wrong numerical answers.
> 3. **Neglecting the Reynolds number check:** If $Re > 2100$, students must explicitly say that the Poiseuille equation is not valid. Marks are deducted for blindly applying the formula to turbulent flow.
> 4. **No-slip boundary condition** ($v = 0$ at the wall) must be stated explicitly during derivations.
> 5. **Density is required** for Reynolds number, not for Poiseuille's law — students sometimes invert these.
> 6. **In Part (a) of Question A**, do not skip the **assumptions** list; the examiner expects at least 5–6 explicit assumptions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Revision Checklist — Flow Rate (Module 1)**

- [x] **Volumetric Flow Rate** $Q = A \bar{v}$ (SI: m³/s).
- [x] **Mass Flow Rate** $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ (SI: kg/s).
- [x] **General integral form**: $Q = \int_A \vec{v} \cdot d\vec{A}$.
- [x] **Continuity equation** (incompressible, steady, 1-D): $A_1 \bar{v}_1 = A_2 \bar{v}_2$.
- [x] **Differential continuity**: $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{v}) = 0$.
- [x] For **incompressible, steady** flow: $\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0$.
- [x] **Reynolds Number**: $Re = \dfrac{\rho \bar{v} D}{\mu}$.
- [x] **Laminar regime**: $Re < 2100$; **Turbulent**: $Re > 4000$.
- [x] **Hagen-Poiseuille Law**: $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8 \mu L}$.
- [x] **Mean velocity = half of centerline velocity** in Poiseuille flow.
- [x] **Hydraulic resistance**: $R_h = \dfrac{8 \mu L}{\pi R^4}$ (Ohm's-law analog).
- [x] Flow rate depends on $R^4$ — physiologically critical for vasoconstriction/vasodilation.
- [x] **No-slip BC** at walls; **axisymmetry BC** at the centerline.
- [x] Always **check Re** before applying Poiseuille's law.
- [x] **Newtonian fluid assumption** is required for Hagen-Poiseuille.
- [x] **Units of $R$** must be meters, not mm or cm.
- [x] **Parabolic velocity profile** is the hallmark of fully-developed laminar flow.
- [x] **Cardiac output** (≈ 5 L/min) is a direct application of $Q$ in hemodynamics.
- [x] **Continuity is the *first* equation to invoke** in any flow problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acceleration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT832 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Acceleration ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the rigorous framework of **Biomedical Transport Phenomena**, **acceleration** is the time rate of change of the velocity field of a fluid (or continuum) element. Because the field is generally both spatially non-uniform and time-dependent, acceleration is mathematically captured by the **material (or substantial) derivative** of the velocity vector: $$\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt}$$ For an arbitrary scalar or vector field, the material derivative in Cartesian coordinates expands to: $$\frac{D}{Dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} + v_x \frac{\partial}{\partial x} + v_y \frac{\partial}{\partial y} + v_z \frac{\partial}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} + (\vec{v} \cdot \nabla)$$ Therefore, the **acceleration field** of a biofluid (e.g., blood in an artery, air in the pulmonary tract, or interstitial fluid in tissue) is: $$\vec{a} = \frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + (\vec{v} \cdot \nabla)\vec{v}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1 of OEBRT832, acceleration is the foundational building block preceding the continuity equation, momentum equation (Navier–Stokes), and energy equation. A strong command of the material derivative is **non-negotiable** for subsequent derivations. ### 1.2 Two Fundamental Components of Acceleration The total acceleration of a fluid parcel contains two physically distinct parts: | Component | Mathematical Form | Physical Name | |---|---|---| | Local (Temporal) | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t}$ | Unsteady acceleration | | Convective (Advective) | $(\vec{v} \cdot \nabla)\vec{v}$ | Spatial / Streamline acceleration | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — A Leaf in a River:** > Imagine standing on a bridge watching a **leaf** floating in a fast stream. The leaf accelerates for two possible reasons: > 1. **Wind picks up** → the water at that *fixed point* speeds up with time. This is the **local acceleration** $\partial \vec{v}/\partial t$ — the velocity at your observation post changes. > 2. **The leaf moves into faster water** further downstream, even though the velocity at each point in the river is steady. This is the **convective acceleration** $(\vec{v} \cdot \nabla)\vec{v}$ — the fluid element is *carried* into regions of different velocity. > > In **pulsatile blood flow** through the aorta, both effects occur simultaneously: the heart's rhythmic ejection creates a time-dependent (local) component, and the tapering geometry of the artery forces red blood cells to accelerate (convective) as they move into narrower cross-sections. This dual nature is the essence of the material derivative. ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - The standard unit of acceleration in the SI system is **m·s⁻²** (metres per second squared). - For **biomedical applications**, typical accelerations encountered are: - **Blood in aorta (systolic peak):** ~ **10–20 m·s⁻²** - **Air in trachea (cough):** ~ **100 m·s⁻²** - **Cerebrospinal fluid pulsations:** ~ **0.01 m·s⁻²** - The gravitational acceleration constant used in body-force terms is **g = 9.81 m·s⁻²** (magnitude), with vector $\vec{g} = -g\hat{k}$ when z points upward. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Logic Behind the Two-Term Decomposition The decomposition of acceleration into a local and a convective piece is not arbitrary — it is a direct consequence of choosing an **Eulerian** (field) description over a **Lagrangian** (particle-tracking) description. **Step-by-step reasoning:** - **Lagrangian view:** We tag a specific fluid particle (e.g., a labelled red blood cell) and follow it. Its position is $\vec{r}_p(t)$, and its acceleration is simply $d^2\vec{r}_p/dt^2$. - **Eulerian view:** We sit at a *fixed* point in space $\vec{r}$ and watch the velocity field $\vec{v}(\vec{r}, t)$ pass by. We never "follow" a particle; we observe the *field*. - **Bridging the two views:** When a particle moves from $\vec{r}$ to $\vec{r} + d\vec{r}$ in time $dt$, the velocity it experiences changes because (a) time advanced AND (b) it moved to a new spatial location. Applying the chain rule of multivariable calculus yields the material derivative — a purely mathematical consequence of the duality. ### 2.2 Why the Convective Term Is Nonlinear Notice that $(\vec{v} \cdot \nabla)\vec{v}$ is **quadratic in velocity**. This nonlinearity is the *mathematical origin* of: - Turbulence in physiological flows (e.g., post-stenotic jets). - The inertial (non-Newtonian-looking) behaviour of blood at high shear rates. - The complexity that prevents closed-form solutions of the Navier–Stokes equations except in idealized geometries. ### 2.3 Acceleration in Different Coordinate Systems KTU 2024 expects fluency in both Cartesian and curvilinear forms. In **cylindrical coordinates** $(r, \theta, z)$, used heavily for arterial and airway flows: $$a_r = \frac{\partial v_r}{\partial t} + v_r \frac{\partial v_r}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta} - \frac{v_\theta^2}{r} + v_z \frac{\partial v_r}{\partial z}$$ $$a_\theta = \frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial t} + v_r \frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \frac{v_r v_\theta}{r} + v_z \frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial z}$$ $$a_z = \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} + v_r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial \theta} + v_z \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z}$$ The terms $v_\theta^2/r$ and $v_r v_\theta/r$ are the **centripetal** and **Coriolis-type** accelerations, essential when analysing swirling or helical blood flow (e.g., in the aortic arch or ventricular vortex rings). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this table — it covers ~70% of numericals on acceleration in Module 1.** | # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | SI Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Material derivative operator | $\dfrac{D}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} + (\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)$ | Time-rate following a fluid element | s⁻¹ | | 2 | Acceleration field (Cartesian) | $\vec{a} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + (\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v}$ | $\vec{v}$: velocity; $\vec{a}$: acceleration | m·s⁻² | | 3 | Local acceleration | $\vec{a}_{\text{local}} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t}$ | Unsteady term | m·s⁻² | | 4 | Convective acceleration | $\vec{a}_{\text{conv}} = (\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v}$ | Spatial advection term | m·s⁻² | | 5 | Cartesian x-component | $a_x = \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} + v_x\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + v_y\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + v_z\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial z}$ | x-direction | m·s⁻² | | 6 | Streamwise normal acceleration | $a_n = \dfrac{v^2}{R}$ | v: speed; R: radius of curvature of streamline | m·s⁻² | | 7 | Streamwise tangential acceleration | $a_t = \dfrac{\partial v_s}{\partial t} + v_s\dfrac{\partial v_s}{\partial s}$ | s: arc length along streamline | m·s⁻² | | 8 | Steady-flow special case | $\vec{a} = (\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v}$ | Local term vanishes | m·s⁻² | | 9 | Uniform-flow special case | $\vec{a} = 0$ | Both local and convective vanish | m·s⁻² | | 10 | Reynolds number (context) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | Inertial vs viscous ratio (uses acceleration) | dimensionless | ### 2.5 Engineering & Biomedical Utility - **Diagnostic imaging (Doppler ultrasound, MRI velocimetry):** The convective acceleration term determines how the velocity profile deforms spatially; mapping it allows estimation of pressure gradients via Euler's equation. - **Cardiovascular device design:** Prosthetic heart valves and stents must be designed so that peak convective acceleration does not exceed thresholds that would damage red blood cells (haemolysis). - **Drug delivery & microfluidics:** In lab-on-chip devices, understanding acceleration is essential for predicting particle trajectories in inertial focusing. - **Respiratory mechanics:** Acceleration of gas in the upper airway during a cough determines aerosol dispersion — a topic relevant to infectious disease transmission. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Material Derivative from First Principles (Chain Rule) **Starting premise:** A fluid particle's position $\vec{r}_p(t)$ is a function of time. Its velocity is: $$\vec{v}_p(t) = \frac{d\vec{r}_p}{dt}$$ We want the *acceleration* of this particle, observed from the Eulerian field $\vec{v}(\vec{r}, t)$. **Step 1.** Because the particle's position changes with time, the velocity it samples at time $t$ is the *field* evaluated along its trajectory: $$\vec{v}_p(t) = \vec{v}\big(\vec{r}_p(t),\, t\big)$$ **Step 2.** Differentiate with respect to $t$ using the **multivariable chain rule** (the field depends on $t$ both explicitly and *through* $\vec{r}_p$): $$\vec{a}_p = \frac{d\vec{v}_p}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\,\vec{v}\big(\vec{r}_p(t),\, t\big)$$ **Step 3.** Apply the chain rule component by component. For the x-component ($v_x = v_x(x,y,z,t)$): $$\frac{dv_x}{dt} = \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z}\frac{dz}{dt} + \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial t}$$ **Step 4.** Recognise that $dx/dt = v_x$, $dy/dt = v_y$, $dz/dt = v_z$ (the particle moves with the local fluid velocity). Substitute: $$\frac{dv_x}{dt} = v_x \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + v_y \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + v_z \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial t}$$ **Step 5.** Rearrange (group the local term last for convention): $$\frac{dv_x}{dt} = \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} + \left(v_x \frac{\partial}{\partial x} + v_y \frac{\partial}{\partial y} + v_z \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\right) v_x$$ **Step 6.** Identify the operator in parentheses as $(\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)$ and repeat for the y and z components, then assemble vectorially: $$\boxed{\;\vec{a} = \frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + (\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v} = \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt}\;}$$ This is the **defining equation** of acceleration in a continuum. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Streamline Decomposition (Normal + Tangential) Consider a fluid particle moving along a curved streamline. Decompose its velocity into a tangential unit vector $\hat{t}$ and magnitude $v_s$: $$\vec{v} = v_s\,\hat{t}$$ Differentiate with respect to time (using the Frenet–Serret identity $d\hat{t}/ds = \hat{n}/R$, where $R$ is the local radius of curvature and $\hat{n}$ is the principal normal): $$\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = \frac{D v_s}{Dt}\hat{t} + v_s \frac{D\hat{t}}{Dt}$$ Expand $D\hat{t}/Dt$ using the chain rule $D\hat{t}/Dt = (d\hat{t}/ds)(ds/dt) = (\hat{n}/R)\,v_s$: $$\vec{a} = \left(\frac{\partial v_s}{\partial t} + v_s\frac{\partial v_s}{\partial s}\right)\hat{t} + \frac{v_s^2}{R}\hat{n}$$ **Interpretation:** - Tangential component: $a_t = \dfrac{\partial v_s}{\partial t} + v_s\dfrac{\partial v_s}{\partial s}$ — change of *speed* along the streamline. - Normal component: $a_n = \dfrac{v_s^2}{R}$ — centripetal acceleration due to streamline curvature. This decomposition is extensively used in **biomedical applications** such as tracing particle paths through the curved aortic arch. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix) **Problem:** Blood flows in the carotid artery with a velocity field given by: $$v_x(x,y,z,t) = 2t + x^2, \quad v_y = 0, \quad v_z = 0$$ Find the acceleration of a fluid particle at the point $(1, 0, 0)$ m at time $t = 1$ s. **Solution Matrix:** | Step | Computation | Result | |---|---|---| | 1. Compute partial derivatives of $v_x$ | $\partial v_x/\partial t = 2$; $\partial v_x/\partial x = 2x$ | At (1,0,0), t=1: $\partial v_x/\partial t = 2$, $\partial v_x/\partial x = 2$ | | 2. Evaluate velocity at the point | $v_x(1,0,0,1) = 2(1) + 1^2 = 3$ m/s | $v_x = 3$ m/s | | 3. Compute local acceleration | $\partial v_x/\partial t = 2$ | $a_{\text{local}} = 2$ m·s⁻² | | 4. Compute convective acceleration | $v_x (\partial v_x/\partial x) = 3 \times 2 = 6$ | $a_{\text{conv}} = 6$ m·s⁻² | | 5. Sum for total acceleration | $a_x = 2 + 6$ | $a_x = 8$ m·s⁻² | | 6. y and z components | $v_y = v_z = 0$ ⇒ all derivatives zero | $a_y = a_z = 0$ | **Final Answer:** $\vec{a} = 8\,\hat{i}$ m·s⁻². --- ### 3.4 Vector Identity: Convective Term as a Curl-like Form Using the vector identity $(\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v} = \nabla(v^2/2) - \vec{v}\times(\nabla\times\vec{v})$: $$\vec{a} = \frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + \nabla\!\left(\frac{v^2}{2}\right) - \vec{v}\times(\nabla\times\vec{v})$$ This form (the **Lamb form**) is the version that appears in **Bernoulli's equation** for inviscid flow — and is the bridge to deriving Euler's equation of motion in subsequent modules. KTU frequently tests this identity. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Particle Motion to Material Derivative ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Fluid Particle Tracked
Lagrangian View"] --> B["Position r subscript p of t"] B --> C["Velocity equals d r subscript p over d t"] C --> D["Acceleration equals d squared r subscript p over d t squared"] D --> E["Apply Chain Rule
to Eulerian field v of r and t"] E --> F["Local Term
partial v over partial t"] E --> G["Convective Term
v dot nabla times v"] F --> H["Material Derivative
D v over D t"] G --> H H --> I["Apply to Navier Stokes
Momentum Equation"] H --> J["Apply to Continuity
Mass Conservation"] H --> K["Apply to Energy
Equation"] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Components of Acceleration in a Pulsatile Artery ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Heart["Heart Ejection Pulse"] H1["Systolic Contraction"] end Heart --> LA["Local Acceleration
partial v over partial t
Time dependent ejection"] subgraph Geometry["Arterial Geometry"] G1["Tapering Cross Section"] G2["Curved Aortic Arch"] end Geometry --> CA["Convective Acceleration
v dot nabla v
Spatial velocity gradients"] LA --> Total["Total Acceleration
D v over D t"] CA --> Total Total --> Output1["Euler Equation
Pressure gradient"] Total --> Output2["WSS Calculation
Wall shear stress"] Total --> Output3["Haemolysis Risk
Index"] ``` ### 4.3 Decision Logic: Choosing the Right Acceleration Term ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Given Flow Problem"] --> Q1{"Is the flow
steady?"} Q1 -- Yes --> S1["Local term equals 0
Only convective remains"] Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Is the flow
spatially uniform?"} Q2 -- Yes --> S2["Convective term equals 0
Only local remains"] Q2 -- No --> S3["Both terms nonzero
Use full material derivative"] S1 --> End["Acceleration = v dot nabla v"] S2 --> End2["Acceleration = partial v over partial t"] S3 --> End3["Acceleration = D v over D t"] ``` ### 4.4 Coordinate-System Selection Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P["Problem Setup"] --> Q{"Geometry type?"} Q -- "Straight pipe / channel" --> C1["Cartesian x y z"] Q -- "Circular artery / airway" --> C2["Cylindrical r theta z"] Q -- "Spherical droplet / cell" --> C3["Spherical r theta phi"] C1 --> A1["Use 3 Cartesian components
of material derivative"] C2 --> A2["Include centripetal term
v subscript theta squared over r"] C3 --> A3["Include all 3 spherical
Christoffel terms"] ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the *material derivative* of a fluid property. Why is it called the *substantial* derivative? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The material derivative is the time rate of change of a property as measured by an observer moving with the fluid particle. $$\frac{D}{Dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} + (\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)$$ It is called the *substantial* (or *total*) derivative because it captures the **substantial change** experienced by the substance (the fluid element) — combining the explicit time variation (local) and the change due to motion through a spatially varying field (convective). It represents the derivative following a *substantial* parcel of fluid, not a fixed point in space. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for operator form, 1 for naming rationale.)** **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Distinguish between *local* and *convective* acceleration with one biomedical example each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Local acceleration** $\partial \vec{v}/\partial t$ arises when the velocity at a fixed spatial point changes with time. *Biomedical example:* Pulsatile blood flow in the aorta — the velocity at the centreline oscillates between systole and diastole. - **Convective acceleration** $(\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v}$ arises when a fluid element moves into a region of different velocity, even under steady conditions. *Biomedical example:* Steady laminar flow through a tapering artery — a red blood cell accelerates as the cross-section narrows, although the flow at any fixed point is time-independent. **(3 marks: 1 each for definitions, 1 for examples.)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer either full **Question A** OR full **Question B** within the allotted module. --- #### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style] **(a)** Starting from the chain rule of multivariable calculus, derive the expression for the acceleration of a fluid particle in Cartesian coordinates. Clearly identify the local and convective components. (7 marks) **(b)** A two-dimensional biomedical flow has the velocity field $\vec{v} = (3x + t)\,\hat{i} + (2y)\,\hat{j}$ m/s. Determine the acceleration of a fluid particle at the point $(1, 2)$ m at time $t = 0$. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** - Identify the velocity of a particle as the field evaluated at the particle's position: $\vec{v}_p(t) = \vec{v}(\vec{r}_p(t), t)$. **(1 mark)** - Differentiate using the chain rule. For x-component: $dv_x/dt = (\partial v_x/\partial t) + (\partial v_x/\partial x)(dx/dt) + (\partial v_x/\partial y)(dy/dt) + (\partial v_x/\partial z)(dz/dt)$. **(2 marks)** - Substitute $dx/dt = v_x$, $dy/dt = v_y$, $dz/dt = v_z$. **(1 mark)** - Generalise to all three components and write in operator form: $D/Dt = \partial/\partial t + \vec{v}\cdot\nabla$. **(1 mark)** - Final boxed expression $\vec{a} = \partial\vec{v}/\partial t + (\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v}$, identifying each term. **(2 marks)** **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** - Compute partial derivatives at $(x,y) = (1,2)$, $t=0$: - $\partial v_x/\partial t = 1$; $\partial v_x/\partial x = 3$; $\partial v_x/\partial y = 0$. - $\partial v_y/\partial t = 0$; $\partial v_y/\partial x = 0$; $\partial v_y/\partial y = 2$. **(2 marks)** - Compute velocity components at the point: $v_x = 3(1) + 0 = 3$ m/s; $v_y = 2(2) = 4$ m/s. **(1 mark)** - Local acceleration: $a_{x,\text{local}} = 1$ m·s⁻², $a_{y,\text{local}} = 0$. **(1 mark)** - Convective acceleration: $a_{x,\text{conv}} = v_x(\partial v_x/\partial x) + v_y(\partial v_x/\partial y) = 3(3) + 4(0) = 9$ m·s⁻². $a_{y,\text{conv}} = v_x(\partial v_y/\partial x) + v_y(\partial v_y/\partial y) = 3(0) + 4(2) = 8$ m·s⁻². **(2 marks)** - Total: $a_x = 1 + 9 = 10$ m·s⁻², $a_y = 0 + 8 = 8$ m·s⁻². **(1 mark)** **Final Answer:** $\vec{a} = 10\,\hat{i} + 8\,\hat{j}$ m·s⁻², with magnitude $\vert\vec{a}\vert = \sqrt{100 + 64} = \sqrt{164} \approx 12.81$ m·s⁻². --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style] **(a)** Using the vector identity $(\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v} = \nabla(v^2/2) - \vec{v}\times(\nabla\times\vec{v})$, derive the **Lamb form** of the material derivative and state one physical interpretation of each term. (7 marks) **(b)** Air flows in a curved bronchial tube modelled as a 2-D channel with velocity $\vec{v} = (5y)\,\hat{i} + (0)\,\hat{j}$ m/s. Determine: (i) the local acceleration, (ii) the convective acceleration at $(x,y) = (0.5, 0.2)$ m, and (iii) comment on the nature of the flow (steady/uniform?). (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Lamb Form Derivation (7 marks):** - Start with $\vec{a} = \partial\vec{v}/\partial t + (\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)\vec{v}$. **(1 mark)** - Apply the stated vector identity to the second term. **(2 marks)** - Result: $\vec{a} = \partial\vec{v}/\partial t + \nabla(v^2/2) - \vec{v}\times(\nabla\times\vec{v})$. **(2 marks)** - Interpretation (2 marks, 1 each): - $\nabla(v^2/2)$ — gradient of kinetic-energy density; produces a force-like term in Euler's equation. - $\vec{v}\times(\nabla\times\vec{v})$ — represents the rotational (vortical) contribution; vanishes in irrotational flow, enabling Bernoulli integration. **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** - $v_x = 5y$, $v_y = 0$; $v_x$ and $v_y$ have *no* explicit $t$ dependence. - (i) Local acceleration: $\partial v_x/\partial t = 0$, $\partial v_y/\partial t = 0$ ⇒ $\vec{a}_{\text{local}} = 0$. **(2 marks)** - (ii) Convective: $a_x = v_x(\partial v_x/\partial x) + v_y(\partial v_x/\partial y) = 5y(0) + 0(5) = 0$. $a_y = v_x(\partial v_y/\partial x) + v_y(\partial v_y/\partial y) = 0$. Total convective = 0 at *all* points. **(3 marks)** - (iii) The flow is **steady** (no $t$ in field) AND **uniform along x** (no x-dependence). In fact $\vec{v} = 5y\,\hat{i}$ is a simple shear flow, and its material derivative is identically zero — the flow is **acceleration-free**. **(2 marks)** **Final Answer:** (i) $\vec{a}_{\text{local}} = 0$; (ii) $\vec{a}_{\text{conv}} = 0$; (iii) Total $\vec{a} = 0$ — acceleration-free simple shear. --- ### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Valuation:** > 1. **Forgetting the convective term** when the field is steady but spatially varying — it costs **up to 4 marks**. The phrase "steady" only kills the *local* term. > 2. **Sign errors in the chain rule** — KTU examiners award zero credit for a final boxed answer whose sign contradicts the intermediate steps. > 3. **Mixing up Eulerian and Lagrangian acceleration** in Part A definitions — explicitly state *which observer* is measuring. > 4. **Ignoring cross-derivative terms** in cylindrical coordinates (especially $v_\theta^2/r$) — this is a 2-mark deduction each time. > 5. **Not writing units** in numerical answers — KTU 2024 scheme *mandates* SI units; missing units can cost **0.5–1 mark per sub-part**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Acceleration in a continuum ≠ acceleration of a rigid body.** It must be expressed using the **material derivative** because the field is observed from a fixed (Eulerian) frame, while the *physics* follows a moving particle. - The material derivative operator is $D/Dt = \partial/\partial t + (\vec{v}\cdot\nabla)$. Memorize the form, not the derivation word-for-word. - **Two-term structure:** *local* (temporal, $\partial/\partial t$) + *convective* (spatial, $\vec{v}\cdot\nabla$). - **Streamline decomposition** gives $a_t$ (tangential, change of speed) and $a_n = v^2/R$ (normal, centripetal). - **Lamb form** $\vec{a} = \partial\vec{v}/\partial t + \nabla(v^2/2) - \vec{v}\times(\nabla\times\vec{v})$ is the gateway to Euler's and Bernoulli's equations. - In **cylindrical coordinates**, the centripetal term $v_\theta^2/r$ must be **subtracted** in $a_r$ and **added** in $a_\theta$ (with the Coriolis-like term $v_r v_\theta/r$). - **Steady flow** ⇒ local term vanishes; **uniform flow** ⇒ convective term vanishes. A flow must be **both** for total acceleration to be zero. - **Units check:** Acceleration = m·s⁻²; density × acceleration = pressure gradient (N·m⁻³) — this is the cornerstone of the Navier–Stokes derivation in later modules. - **Biomedical relevance:** Pulsatile blood flow, respiratory airflow, intracardiac vortices, and microfluidic cell sorting all hinge on the proper evaluation of $\vec{a}$. ---

topics.title: Streamlines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT832 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Streamlines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **streamline** is an imaginary curve drawn through a flowing fluid such that the tangent to the curve at any point is parallel to the **instantaneous velocity vector** of the fluid at that point. In the context of *Biomedical Transport Phenomena*, streamlines describe the spatial trajectory followed by an infinitesimally small fluid element (such as blood plasma, oxygen, or an intravenous drug bolus) as it moves through a biological conduit (such as an artery, vein, capillary bed, or even an artificial organ like a dialysis cartridge).

Mathematically, for a three-dimensional velocity field $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$, a streamline is defined by the vector differential relation:

$$\frac{dx}{u(x,y,z,t)} = \frac{dy}{v(x,y,z,t)} = \frac{dz}{w(x,y,z,t)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Streamlines vs. Pathlines vs. Streaklines**
> 
> | Feature | Streamline | Pathline | Streakline |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Definition** | Tangent to instantaneous velocity | Actual trajectory of a single fluid particle | Locus of all particles that previously passed through a given point |
> | **Time dependence** | Instantaneous snapshot (can be unsteady) | Traces history of one particle | Traces history of a marked fluid line |
> | **Steady flow** | Streamline = Pathline = Streakline | Coincide | Coincide |

In **steady flow**, streamlines remain *fixed in space* and do not change with time. In **unsteady flow**, the streamline pattern itself evolves temporally.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Leaf on the River"

Imagine floating a small, perfectly buoyant leaf on the surface of a gently flowing river. The instantaneous path the leaf *appears* to be taking at a frozen instant of time (a photograph) is essentially its **streamline** at that moment. Now imagine photographing the leaf one second later — in steady flow, the direction it is moving at every point in space looks identical to the previous photo. The river's surface is paved with invisible "lanes" (streamlines) that guide the leaf, swirling around rocks and accelerating through narrow gorges — exactly mimicking how blood streamlines deform around atherosclerotic plaques in human arteries.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Dynamic viscosity of blood (at 37 °C):** $\mu_{blood} \approx 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- **Density of blood:** $\rho_{blood} \approx 1060 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Kinematic viscosity of blood:** $\nu \approx 3.3 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Reynolds number threshold for streamline breakdown (transition to turbulence in aorta):** $Re_{crit} \approx 2300$ (pipes); $\sim 200$–$300$ in large vessels (aorta, due to pulsatility)

> [!NOTE]
> In **laminar flow** (low Reynolds number), streamlines are smooth, parallel, and well-defined. In **turbulent flow**, streamlines become chaotic, interweaving, and stochastic — this is a *very common viva question* in KTU biomedical exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Anatomy of a Streamline

A streamline is constructed using the following logical chain:

1. **Specify the velocity field** $\vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$ for the biological fluid (e.g., blood, air, cerebrospinal fluid).
2. **At a chosen instant** $t = t_0$, evaluate velocity components $(u, v, w)$ at point $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$.
3. **The direction of motion** of the fluid at that instant is along the vector $(u, v, w)$.
4. **Draw a continuous curve** such that the unit tangent vector $\hat{T}$ at every point satisfies $\hat{T} \parallel \vec{V}$.
5. **Streamlines never cross** (except at stagnation points or singular points) — crossing would imply two different velocity directions at the same point, a physical impossibility in classical continuum mechanics.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Streamline Equation

The defining equation arises directly from the requirement that the **displacement vector** $d\vec{r} = (dx, dy, dz)$ along a streamline must be **parallel** to the local velocity vector $\vec{V} = (u, v, w)$. Two vectors are parallel if and only if their cross product vanishes, or equivalently, if their components are proportional:

$$d\vec{r} \times \vec{V} = \vec{0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}$$

### 2.3 Stream Function $\psi$ (2D, Incompressible Flow)

For **two-dimensional, incompressible** flow (a very common scenario in KTU problems involving cross-sections of arteries, airways, or the bladder), we introduce a scalar function $\psi(x, y)$ such that:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Lines of constant $\psi$ are exactly the **streamlines**! The stream function automatically enforces mass conservation ($\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$) for incompressible flow.

The volumetric flow rate $Q$ between two streamlines $\psi_1$ and $\psi_2$ is simply:

$$Q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$$

This elegant property makes the stream function extremely useful in analyzing **blood flow through bifurcations** and **airflow in the bronchial tree**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S. No.** | **Concept** | **Formula / Relation** | **Units / Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Streamline definition (3D) | $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$ | Scalar parametric identity |
| 2 | Streamline in 2D | $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v}$ | $(u, v)$ velocity components |
| 3 | Stream function relation | $u = \partial \psi / \partial y$, $v = -\partial \psi / \partial x$ | $u, v$ in m/s; $\psi$ in m²/s |
| 4 | Continuity (incompressible) | $\partial u / \partial x + \partial v / \partial y = 0$ | Automatically satisfied by $\psi$ |
| 5 | Flow rate between streamlines | $Q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$ | m³/s |
| 6 | Stagnation point | $u = 0, \ v = 0$ | Streamlines converge/diverge |
| 7 | Cylindrical coordinates | $u_r \, dr = u_\theta \, r \, d\theta = u_z \, dz$ | Radial, azimuthal, axial |
| 8 | Reynolds number | $Re = \rho V D / \mu$ | Dimensionless; governs streamline stability |
| 9 | Critical Re (turbulence onset, pipe) | $Re_{crit} \approx 2300$ | Empirical |
| 10 | Streamline density | $\propto \vert \vec{V} \vert$ | Closeness of streamlines → flow speed |

### 2.5 Real-World Biomedical Engineering Utility

- **Cardiovascular stents:** Designing stent geometries that preserve smooth, non-recirculating streamlines prevents **intimal hyperplasia** and **thrombosis**.
- **Hemodialysis:** Streamline analysis ensures uniform dialysate flow over hollow fibers, maximizing solute clearance.
- **Ventilator design:** Streamline analysis of airflow in endotracheal tubes reduces **turbulence** and patient work of breathing.
- **Drug delivery microfluidics:** Lab-on-chip devices for targeted drug delivery rely on precise **streamline splitting** at Y-junctions.
- **Heart valve prosthetics:** Leaflet design aims to minimize flow separation (loss of streamline coherence), reducing hemolysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Velocity Field to Streamline Equation

**Step 1 — Start with the velocity vector.**
The fluid velocity at a spatial point $(x, y, z)$ and time $t$ is:

$$\vec{V} = u(x, y, z, t) \, \hat{i} + v(x, y, z, t) \, \hat{j} + w(x, y, z, t) \, \hat{k}$$

**Step 2 — Define an elemental arc length along the streamline.**
A small movement along the streamline corresponds to a displacement vector:

$$d\vec{r} = dx \, \hat{i} + dy \, \hat{j} + dz \, \hat{k}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the parallel condition.**
Since the streamline is everywhere tangent to $\vec{V}$, the displacement $d\vec{r}$ must be parallel to $\vec{V}$. Two parallel vectors satisfy:

$$d\vec{r} = \lambda \, \vec{V} \quad \text{for some scalar } \lambda$$

**Step 4 — Component-wise decomposition.**
Equating components of $d\vec{r}$ and $\lambda \vec{V}$:

$$dx = \lambda u, \quad dy = \lambda v, \quad dz = \lambda w$$

**Step 5 — Eliminate the parameter $\lambda$.**
Dividing pairwise:

$$\frac{dx}{u} = \lambda = \frac{dy}{v} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v}$$

$$\frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}$$

**Step 6 — Combine to form the unified streamline identity.**

$$\boxed{\frac{dx}{u(x, y, z, t)} = \frac{dy}{v(x, y, z, t)} = \frac{dz}{w(x, y, z, t)}}$$

> **Conversion logic:** We have eliminated the proportionality constant $\lambda$ by taking ratios, yielding a parameter-free intrinsic definition of the streamline. This is the *only* formula KTU expects in Part A derivations on streamlines.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Stream Function from Continuity

**Step 1 — State the continuity equation for 2D incompressible flow.**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Propose a stream function $\psi(x, y)$.**
Since the divergence is zero, there exists a scalar field $\psi$ such that:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3 — Verify satisfaction of continuity.**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \, \partial y}, \qquad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \, \partial y}$$

Adding:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \, \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \, \partial y} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Show that constant-$\psi$ curves are streamlines.**
The differential change in $\psi$ along an arbitrary curve is:

$$d\psi = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} dy$$

Substituting the stream function relations:

$$d\psi = -v \, dx + u \, dy$$

Along a streamline, $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v}$, which gives $v \, dx = u \, dy$. Hence:

$$d\psi = 0 \quad \text{along a streamline} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \psi = \text{constant} \ \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Problem (KTU-style)

**Problem:** Blood flows through a small artery segment modeled by the 2D velocity field $u = 2x$ m/s, $v = -2y$ m/s. Find the equation of the streamline passing through the point $(1, 1)$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the 2D streamline equation.**

$$\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dx}{2x} = \frac{dy}{-2y}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate both sides.**

$$\int \frac{dx}{x} = - \int \frac{dy}{y}$$

$$\ln x = -\ln y + C$$

**Step 3 — Combine logarithms.**

$$\ln x + \ln y = C \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln(xy) = C$$

$$xy = K \quad \text{(where } K = e^C \text{)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the boundary condition** — streamline passes through $(1, 1)$:

$$K = (1)(1) = 1$$

**Step 5 — Final streamline equation.**

$$\boxed{xy = 1}$$

> **Biomedical interpretation:** This family of hyperbolic streamlines ($xy = K$) resembles the flow pattern observed near a **bifurcation apex** in arterial networks, where streamlines diverge from a stagnation point — a configuration prone to plaque deposition at low-shear regions.

---

### 3.4 Alternative Analytical Form — Streamlines in Polar Coordinates

For flows with cylindrical symmetry (e.g., flow around a catheter, or in an artificial blood vessel):

$$\frac{dr}{u_r} = \frac{r \, d\theta}{u_\theta} = \frac{dz}{u_z}$$

This form is essential for problems involving **stents**, **catheters**, and **vascular grafts**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Flow — Streamline Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT: Velocity Field Vxyz t"] --> nodeB["Instantaneous Snapshot at t0"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Evaluate u v w at Point P"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Compute Tangent Direction"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Advance by Infinitesimal dr"]
    nodeE --> nodeF{"Reached Boundary or Domain Edge?"}
    nodeF -->|"No"| nodeC
    nodeF -->|"Yes"| nodeG["OUTPUT: Complete Streamline Curve"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Check Stream Function psi = constant"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Compute Volumetric Flow Rate Q"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Biomedical Application: Stent Valve Catheter Design"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Streamline Topology in Arterial Bifurcation

```mermaid

    nodeA["Inlet Artery: Laminar Parallel Streamlines"] -->|"Parent Vessel"| nodeB["Bifurcation Apex Stagnation Point"]
    nodeB -->|"Daughter Vessel 1"| nodeC["Branch 1: Streamlines Curve Outward"]
    nodeB -->|"Daughter Vessel 2"| nodeD["Branch 2: Streamlines Curve Outward"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Recirculation Zone Low Shear: Plaque Risk"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Restored Laminar Flow"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Clinical Pathology: Atherosclerosis Site"]
```

### 4.3 Visual Conceptual Map — Streamlines vs. Pathlines vs. Streaklines

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Fluid Flow Visualization Techniques"] --> nodeB["Streamline"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Pathline"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Streakline"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Instantaneous Tangent to Velocity"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Trajectory of Single Fluid Particle Over Time"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["Locus of All Particles Through Fixed Point"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["In Steady Flow: All Three Coincide"]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

### 4.4 Block Architecture — Stream Function Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["2D Velocity Field Input ux y vx y"] --> nodeB["Verify Incompressibility: du_dx plus dv_dy equals 0"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Integrate u with respect to y to get psi"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Determine Integration Function fx from v"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Final Stream Function psi x y"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Plot Constant psi Contours: Streamlines"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Compute Q equals psi 2 minus psi 1"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Define a streamline. Why do two streamlines never intersect each other?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A streamline is an imaginary curve in a flowing fluid such that the tangent drawn at any point on this curve is parallel to the **instantaneous velocity vector** of the fluid at that point. Mathematically, it is defined by:

$$\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}$$

Two streamlines cannot intersect each other because at the point of intersection, there would be two distinct tangent directions, implying **two different velocity vectors at the same point at the same instant** — a physical impossibility in classical continuum mechanics. The only exception is a **stagnation point**, where velocity vanishes and streamlines may converge or branch.

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — equation, 1 mark — non-intersection explanation)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
> **Differentiate between streamlines, pathlines, and streaklines. When do they coincide?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Streamline | Pathline | Streakline |
|---|---|---|---|
| Definition | Curve tangent to instantaneous velocity | Actual trajectory of a single fluid particle | Locus of all particles that passed a fixed point earlier |
| Nature | Instantaneous geometric locus | Historical trajectory of a tagged particle | Continuum of tagged particles |

**All three coincide in steady (time-independent) flow**, where $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$, because the velocity field at any fixed point does not change with time — making the *snapshot* streamline identical to the *actual path* a particle traces.

*(1.5 marks — distinction, 1.5 marks — coincidence condition)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, ESE Module 1, Part B Choice Q1]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the general mathematical equation defining a streamline for a 3D, unsteady velocity field. State clearly the physical interpretation of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2D steady velocity field representing blood flow near an arterial bifurcation is given by $u = 3x^2$, $v = -6xy$. Show that the flow is incompressible and hence find the stream function $\psi(x, y)$ and the equation of the streamline passing through the point $(1, 2)$. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the streamline equation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Let the velocity field be $\vec{V}(x, y, z, t) = u \hat{i} + v \hat{j} + w \hat{k}$.

**Step 2:** An infinitesimal displacement along a streamline is $d\vec{r} = dx \hat{i} + dy \hat{j} + dz \hat{k}$.

**Step 3:** Tangency condition: $d\vec{r} \parallel \vec{V}$, hence $d\vec{r} \times \vec{V} = 0$.

**Step 4:** Component-wise: $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$.

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation:**
- $u, v, w$ are instantaneous velocity components (m/s).
- $dx, dy, dz$ are infinitesimal displacements along the streamline.
- The ratio represents the time parameter $\lambda$ such that $\lambda = dt$ in steady flow.

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\frac{dx}{u(x,y,z,t)} = \frac{dy}{v(x,y,z,t)} = \frac{dz}{w(x,y,z,t)}}$$

---

**(b) Stream function and streamline equation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Verify incompressibility:**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(3x^2) = 6x$$

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(-6xy) = -6x$$

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 6x - 6x = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute stream function using $u = \partial \psi / \partial y$:**

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = 3x^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \psi = 3x^2 y + f(x)$$

**Step 3 — Use $v = -\partial \psi / \partial x$ to find $f(x)$:**

$$-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -6xy - f'(x) = -6xy$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad f'(x) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f(x) = C_1$$

**Step 4 — Final stream function:**

$$\boxed{\psi(x, y) = 3x^2 y + C_1}$$

**Step 5 — Streamline equation is $\psi = $ constant:**

$$3x^2 y = K$$

**Step 6 — Apply condition $(1, 2)$:**

$$K = 3(1)^2(2) = 6$$

**Final streamline equation:**

$$\boxed{3x^2 y = 6 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad x^2 y = 2}$$

**Biomedical Note:** Streamlines of the form $x^2 y = $ const are typical of **Stokes flow** past a stagnation point, mimicking flow patterns near arterial bifurcations where endothelial cells experience altered shear stress.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the **stream function** $\psi$ for a 2D incompressible flow. Prove that lines of constant $\psi$ are streamlines, and derive the expression for volumetric flow rate between two streamlines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Air flows steadily through the trachea modeled by a 2D velocity field $u = Ay$, $v = -Ax$, where $A = 5 \ \text{s}^{-1}$. Find: (i) the streamline passing through $(2, 3)$, and (ii) the volumetric flow rate per unit depth between this streamline and the $x$-axis. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Stream function concept and proof (7 marks):**

**Definition:** The stream function $\psi(x, y)$ is a scalar field defined for 2D incompressible flow such that:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

**Proof that $\psi = $ const defines a streamline:**

Along a curve, the change in $\psi$ is:

$$d\psi = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} dy = -v \, dx + u \, dy$$

Along a streamline, $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v}$, i.e., $u \, dy - v \, dx = 0$, so $d\psi = 0$, confirming $\psi = $ const. $\blacksquare$

**Volumetric flow rate between streamlines $\psi_1$ and $\psi_2$:**

$$Q = \int_{path} \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{A} = \psi_2 - \psi_1$$

---

**(b) Application to tracheal airflow (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Verify incompressibility:**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0 + 0 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Integrate to find $\psi$:**

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = Ay \quad \Rightarrow \quad \psi = \frac{Ay^2}{2} + g(x)$$

$$-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -g'(x) = -Ax \quad \Rightarrow \quad g(x) = \frac{Ax^2}{2} + C$$

**Step 3 — Stream function:**

$$\psi(x, y) = \frac{A}{2}(x^2 + y^2) = \frac{5}{2}(x^2 + y^2) \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Streamline through $(2, 3)$:**

$$x^2 + y^2 = 4 + 9 = 13 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{x^2 + y^2 = 13}$$

This is a circle of radius $\sqrt{13}$ — geometrically representing a *radial* symmetric flow pattern, characteristic of **tracheal airflow during quiet breathing**.

**Step 5 — Volumetric flow rate per unit depth between this streamline and the $x$-axis (where $\psi = 0$ at $y = 0$ for $x = 0$):**

$$Q = \psi_{streamline} - \psi_{x-axis} = \frac{5}{2}(13) - 0 = 32.5 \ \text{m}^2/\text{s per unit depth}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 32.5 \ \text{m}^2/\text{s} \ \text{per unit depth}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the non-intersection property** — Students often define a streamline correctly but omit the *crucial* second part: streamlines cannot cross (except at stagnation points). KTU examiners allocate **at least 1 mark** for this.
> 
> 2. **Confusing steady-flow streamlines with pathlines** — In *unsteady* flow, streamlines are *snapshots*, not trajectories. A common error: writing "the streamline is the path of a particle" — this is only true in steady flow.
> 
> 3. **Stream function sign error** — The stream function relation is $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$. The **negative sign on $v$ is frequently dropped** by students, leading to wrong streamline orientations. This single sign error can cost **2–3 marks** in Part B.
> 
> 4. **Missing units in the final answer** — KTU strictly requires units (m²/s for $\psi$, m³/s for $Q$). Unanswered unit deductions are a frequent cause of losing 0.5–1 mark.
> 
> 5. **Failing to verify incompressibility** — Before defining a stream function, students must verify $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$. Skipping this validation costs **1 mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Streamline** = curve everywhere tangent to **instantaneous velocity vector**.
- **Master equation (3D):** $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$.
- **Streamlines NEVER cross** (except at stagnation points where $u = v = w = 0$).
- In **steady flow**, streamlines, pathlines, and streaklines **all coincide**.
- In **laminar flow**, streamlines are smooth, parallel, and well-ordered.
- In **turbulent flow**, streamlines become chaotic and stochastic — no two snapshots look alike.
- **Stream function** $\psi(x, y)$ exists for 2D incompressible flow and satisfies $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$.
- **Lines of constant $\psi$ = streamlines** — a fundamental identity.
- **Volumetric flow rate** between two streamlines = $\psi_2 - \psi_1$.
- **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho V D / \mu$ governs streamline stability; $Re > 2300$ in pipes marks turbulent breakdown.
- **Biomedical relevance:** Stent design, prosthetic heart valves, hemodialyzer cartridges, ventilator tubing, and microfluidic drug-delivery chips all rely on streamline analysis to minimize flow separation, recirculation, and hemolysis.
- **Polar/cylindrical form** of streamline equation: $\dfrac{dr}{u_r} = \dfrac{r \, d\theta}{u_\theta} = \dfrac{dz}{u_z}$.
- **Stagnation points** (where $\vec{V} = \vec{0}$) are clinically significant — they correspond to regions of low wall shear stress and are preferred sites for **atherosclerotic plaque deposition** in arteries.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stream Tubes and Streak Lines - Conservation Relations and Boundary Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT832 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stream Tubes and Streak Lines - Conservation Relations and Boundary Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Kinematic Descriptions of Fluid Motion

In the study of fluid mechanics and transport phenomena, three primary kinematic lines are used to describe fluid motion. Understanding the distinction between them is fundamental before tackling conservation relations.

### Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Pathline (Path of a Particle)**
The trajectory traced by a single fluid particle as it moves through space over a time interval. Mathematically, if a particle is at position $\mathbf{r}_0$ at time $t_0$, the pathline is $\mathbf{r}_p(t)$ satisfying:
$$\mathbf{r}_p(t) = \mathbf{r}_0 + \int_{t_0}^{t} \mathbf{v}(\mathbf{r}_p(\tau), \tau)\, d\tau$$

**Streamline**
An instantaneous curve that is everywhere tangent to the velocity vector field at a given fixed instant of time. At every point along a streamline, the velocity vector is tangent to the curve.

**Streakline (Filament Line)**
The locus of all fluid particles that have previously passed through a prescribed point in the flow field. It is generated by continuously injecting a marker (dye or smoke) at a fixed point and observing the resulting curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Examinations:**
> - For **steady flow** ($\partial \mathbf{v}/\partial t = 0$): Pathlines, streamlines, and streaklines **coincide**.
> - For **unsteady flow**: All three are distinct curves.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a bridge over a river:

- **Pathline** = The actual route a single leaf takes as it floats downstream. If you could attach a tiny GPS to the leaf, its recorded trajectory is the pathline.
- **Streamline** = A snapshot photograph of the river showing arrows (velocity vectors) at every point. If you connect these arrows smoothly, you get a streamline at that instant.
- **Streakline** = You continuously drop green dye from the bridge. The green trail of dye observed at any instant is the streakline.

For steady river flow, these three look identical. For unsteady flow (e.g., a wave passing or a paddle stirring), they become noticeably different.

### Stream Tube and Stream Function

**Stream Tube**
A stream tube is the surface formed by a continuous family of streamlines passing through a closed curve in the flow field. A critical property is that **no fluid crosses the surface of a stream tube**, because by definition the velocity vector is tangent to the surface.

**Stream Function $\psi$ (2D Incompressible Flow)**
For 2D incompressible flow, a scalar function $\psi(x, y, t)$ can be defined such that the velocity components are obtained by:
$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

The stream function is **constant along a streamline** ($d\psi = 0$), which is why lines of constant $\psi$ are streamlines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Definition — Stream Tube:** A stream tube is the tubular surface generated by the streamlines passing through every point of an arbitrary closed curve drawn in the flow. The boundary of a stream tube acts as an impermeable membrane: there is no mass flux across it.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value (in SI) |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ | Density of blood (plasma) | **$\approx 1060 \text{ kg/m}^3$** |
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity of blood | **$\approx 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$** |
| $\nu$ | Kinematic viscosity | **$\nu = \mu/\rho$** |
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **$9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$** |
| $\gamma$ | Surface tension (blood-air) | **$\approx 0.045 \text{ N/m}$** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For biomedical applications (e.g., blood flow in vessels $\sim 1$ mm diameter), the Reynolds number is often low, leading to **laminar**, **creeping** (Stokes) flow — a recurring theme in KTU numericals.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Differential Analysis of Fluid Motion

### Material Derivative (Substantial Derivative)

The rate of change of any property following a fluid particle is the material derivative:
$$\frac{D}{Dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} + \mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla$$

This is essential because it connects the **Eulerian** (fixed-point) and **Lagrangian** (following-the-particle) viewpoints.

### The Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)

For any extensive property $B$ with intensive counterpart $\beta = dB/dm$:
$$\frac{D}{Dt} \int_{CV} \rho \beta \, dV = \int_{CV} \rho \frac{D\beta}{Dt}\, dV = \int_{CV} \frac{\partial (\rho\beta)}{\partial t}\, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho \beta (\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n})\, dA$$

RTT is the **bridging theorem** that converts system-based (Lagrangian) conservation laws into control-volume (Eulerian) forms.

## 2.2 Conservation of Mass — The Continuity Equation

### Integral Form
$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho\, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n})\, dA = 0$$

### Differential Form
Applying the divergence theorem to the surface integral and localizing:
$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0$$

### Incompressible Continuity
For constant density fluids (e.g., water, blood plasma under normal physiological conditions):
$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$$

In Cartesian coordinates:
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **stream tube of constant cross-section in steady, incompressible flow**, mass conservation directly yields the relation $Q = A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ (continuity equation in 1D form). This is a frequently tested KTU result.

## 2.3 Conservation of Momentum — Navier-Stokes Equation

For a Newtonian fluid with constant viscosity:
$$\rho \frac{D\mathbf{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} + \rho \mathbf{g}$$

In expanded Cartesian form (x-component):
$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} \right) + \rho g_x$$

## 2.4 Conservation of Energy — Heat Equation

For a fluid with thermal conductivity $k$ and viscous dissipation $\Phi$:
$$\rho c_p \frac{DT}{Dt} = k \nabla^2 T + \Phi + \dot{q}$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering / Biomedical Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Material derivative | $D/Dt = \partial/\partial t + \mathbf{v}\cdot\nabla$ | Converts Eulerian to Lagrangian rate |
| Continuity (general) | $\partial \rho/\partial t + \nabla\cdot(\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0$ | Mass conservation, blood flow analysis |
| Continuity (incompressible) | $\nabla\cdot\mathbf{v} = 0$ | Water, plasma flow simplification |
| 1D stream tube continuity | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | Vessel constriction, Venturi flowmeters |
| Stream function (2D) | $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ | Visualization of 2D flows |
| Navier-Stokes (Newtonian) | $\rho D\mathbf{v}/Dt = -\nabla p + \mu\nabla^2\mathbf{v} + \rho\mathbf{g}$ | Pulsatile blood flow, drug transport |
| Stokes (creeping) flow | $0 = -\nabla p + \mu\nabla^2\mathbf{v} + \rho\mathbf{g}$ | Microcirculation, capillary flow |
| Energy equation | $\rho c_p DT/Dt = k\nabla^2 T + \Phi + \dot{q}$ | Tissue heat transfer, hyperthermia |
| Reynolds Transport Theorem | $D/Dt \int \rho\beta dV = \int \partial(\rho\beta)/\partial t dV + \oint \rho\beta(\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{n})dA$ | Control-volume analysis |
| No-slip condition | $\mathbf{v}\big\vert_{\text{wall}} = 0$ | Vascular wall, catheter surfaces |
| No-penetration | $\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{n}\big\vert_{\text{wall}} = 0$ | Impermeable boundaries |
| Wall temperature (Dirichlet) | $T\big\vert_{\text{wall}} = T_w$ | Tissue at constant temperature |
| Heat flux (Neumann) | $-k \partial T/\partial n\big\vert_{\text{wall}} = q_w$ | Constant heat-flux boundary |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vascular stents and grafts**: Continuity and Navier-Stokes govern the redesign of vessel geometry to avoid flow separation and thrombus formation.
- **Dialysis membranes**: The jump boundary conditions across a membrane drive solute flux, quantified by Kedem-Katchalsky equations.
- **Oxygenators (heart-lung machines)**: Energy and mass transport through hollow fibers use the conservation relations derived here.
- **Insulin pumps and IV cannulae**: 1D stream tube continuity in narrow cannulae prevents pressure drop and flow instabilities.
- **Hyperthermia cancer treatment**: Bioheat equation (energy conservation) with perfusion term estimates tissue temperature.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Continuity Equation (Differential Form)

### Step 1: Begin with Integral Conservation of Mass

For a fixed control volume $CV$ with control surface $CS$, mass is conserved:
$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho\, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n})\, dA = 0$$

**Reasoning:** The rate of mass accumulation inside the CV plus the net efflux of mass through the CS must equal zero (no sources or sinks of mass).

### Step 2: Bring the Time Derivative Inside the Integral

Since the CV is fixed in space, Leibniz's rule for moving control volumes (with fixed $V$) gives:
$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho\, dV = \int_{CV} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\, dV$$

**Reasoning:** The integrand $\rho(\mathbf{r}, t)$ depends on time only, not on the moving boundary, so the time derivative passes inside the integral.

### Step 3: Apply the Divergence Theorem to the Surface Integral

The divergence theorem converts a surface integral of a vector field to a volume integral of its divergence:
$$\oint_{CS} \rho (\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n})\, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v})\, dV$$

**Reasoning:** $\rho \mathbf{v}$ is the mass flux vector, and the divergence theorem applies to any continuously differentiable vector field.

### Step 4: Combine into a Single Volume Integral

$$\int_{CV} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\, dV + \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v})\, dV = 0$$

$$\int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) \right] dV = 0$$

**Reasoning:** Sum of two integrals over the same volume equals a single integral of the sum.

### Step 5: Localize the Equation

Since the control volume $CV$ is **arbitrary**, the integrand itself must vanish at every point in the flow field:
$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0$$

**Reasoning:** This is the standard localization argument. If the integral of a continuous function vanishes over an arbitrary volume, the function is identically zero everywhere inside it.

### Step 6: Expand the Divergence in Cartesian Form

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$$

Expanding each product:
$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial z} + \rho\left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} \right) = 0$$

**Reasoning:** Using the product rule on $\partial(\rho u)/\partial x = u\partial \rho/\partial x + \rho \partial u/\partial x$.

### Step 7: Recognize the Material Derivative of Density

The first four terms form the material derivative:
$$\frac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) = 0$$

**Final Compact Form (Continuity Equation):**
$$\boxed{\frac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v}) = 0}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: 1D Continuity Through a Stream Tube

### Step 1: Define the Stream Tube

Let $S$ be a stream tube bounded by streamlines along its lateral surface, with two cross-sections $A_1$ and $A_2$ at the ends.

### Step 2: Apply Mass Conservation to the Stream Tube

Because no mass crosses the lateral surface (streamlines are tangent to velocity), mass only enters through $A_1$ and exits through $A_2$:
$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho\, dV = \dot{m}_1 - \dot{m}_2$$

### Step 3: Evaluate Mass Flow Rates

$$\dot{m}_1 = \int_{A_1} \rho_1 \mathbf{v}_1 \cdot \mathbf{n}_1\, dA, \qquad \dot{m}_2 = \int_{A_2} \rho_2 \mathbf{v}_2 \cdot \mathbf{n}_2\, dA$$

### Step 4: Steady, Incompressible Assumption

For steady flow ($\partial/\partial t = 0$) and incompressible fluid ($\rho = $ const):
$$0 = \rho \int_{A_1} \mathbf{v}_1 \cdot \mathbf{n}_1\, dA - \rho \int_{A_2} \mathbf{v}_2 \cdot \mathbf{n}_2\, dA$$

### Step 5: Assume Uniform Velocity at Cross-Sections

For a 1D averaged treatment, $V_1 A_1 = V_2 A_2$:
$$\boxed{Q = A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = \text{constant}}$$

**Reasoning:** This is the classical continuity equation for 1D flow in a stream tube.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Kinematic Lines

| Property | Pathline | Streamline | Streakline |
|---|---|---|---|
| Definition | Trajectory of one particle | Tangent to velocity at fixed $t$ | Locus of particles through fixed point |
| Mathematical form | $\mathbf{r}_p(t)$ | $d\mathbf{r} \times \mathbf{v} = 0$ | All particles tagged at $\mathbf{r}_0$ |
| Coincide in steady flow? | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Coincide in unsteady flow? | No (generally) | No | No |
| Visualization method | Track one particle | Snapshot of velocity field | Inject dye continuously |
| KTU exam importance | Definition + 1 derivation | Definition + stream function | Definition + dye analogy |

## 3.4 KTU Numerical Pattern: Continuity in a Bifurcating Vessel

**Problem Pattern:** Blood flow of $Q = 5 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$ in a parent artery of diameter $4 \text{ mm}$ splits into two identical daughter branches.

### Step 1: Total Flow Conservation
$$Q_{\text{parent}} = Q_1 + Q_2 = 2Q_{\text{daughter}}$$

### Step 2: Solve for Daughter Flow
$$Q_{\text{daughter}} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

### Step 3: Use 1D Continuity to Find Velocity
If daughter diameter $d_d = 3 \text{ mm}$:
$$A_d = \frac{\pi d_d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (3 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} = 7.07 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

$$V_d = \frac{Q_{\text{daughter}}}{A_d} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-6}}{7.07 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.354 \text{ m/s}$$

**Reasoning:** This type of problem is common in KTU Module 1 numericals and is the basis for clinical Doppler ultrasound velocity measurements.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stream Tube Kinematics

This diagram maps the hierarchical relationships between the three kinematic lines, the stream tube concept, and the conservation relations derived from them.

```mermaid
    C --> F[STREAKLINE]

    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[NO MASS FLUX ACROSS LATERAL SURFACE]

    I --> J[CONTINUITY EQUATION DELTA RHO BY DELTA T PLUS NABLA DOT RHO V EQUALS ZERO]

    K --> L[NAVIER STOKES EQUATION]
    H --> M[CONSERVATION OF ENERGY]

    N --> O

```
