# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]
**Q: Define streamlines, pathlines, and streaklines. Under what condition do all three coincide?**

**Model Answer:**

A **streamline** is an instantaneous curve in the flow field that is everywhere tangent to the velocity vector at a given instant. It satisfies $\mathbf{v} \times d\mathbf{r} = 0$ at that instant.

A **pathline** is the trajectory traced by a single fluid particle over a period of time, satisfying $\mathbf{r}_p(t) = \mathbf{r}_0 + \int_{t_0}^{t} \mathbf{v}\, d\tau$.

A **streakline** is the locus of all fluid particles that have previously passed through a fixed point in the flow, visualized by continuous injection of dye.

All three coincide when the flow is **steady**, i.e., $\partial \mathbf{v}/\partial t = 0$. Under steady conditions, the velocity field does not change with time, so the instantaneous streamline direction, the particle's trajectory, and the dye trail are identical curves. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]
**Q: What is a stream tube? Why is the lateral surface of a stream tube impermeable to flow?**

**Model Answer:**

A **stream tube** is a tubular surface formed by a continuous family of streamlines passing through every point of a closed curve drawn in the flow field.

The lateral surface of a stream tube is impermeable because the velocity vector at every point on the surface is, by definition, tangent to the surface (since the surface is constructed from streamlines, which are themselves tangent to the velocity). The normal component of velocity on the lateral surface is therefore zero: $\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n}\big\vert_{\text{lateral}} = 0$, and no mass flux crosses the surface. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the integral form of mass conservation, derive the continuity equation in differential form. State the form of the equation for an incompressible fluid.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the boundary conditions for viscous flow at a solid wall and at a fluid-fluid interface. Give a biomedical example for each.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Integral form of mass conservation:**
For a fixed control volume (CV) bounded by control surface (CS):
$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho\, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n})\, dA = 0$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate the volume integral (CV is fixed, so interchange is valid):**
$$\int_{CV} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\, dV + \oint_{CS} \rho (\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n})\, dA = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply the divergence theorem to the surface integral:**
$$\int_{CV} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\, dV + \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v})\, dV = 0$$

$$\int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) \right] dV = 0$$

**Step 4 — Localize (since CV is arbitrary, integrand = 0):**
$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0$$

**Step 5 — For incompressible fluid ($\rho$ = constant):**
$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$$

Or equivalently:
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \textbf{[7 marks]}$$

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Boundary conditions at a solid wall:**

1. **No-penetration condition:** The normal component of velocity at the wall equals zero. The fluid cannot penetrate the wall:
$$\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n}\big\vert_{\text{wall}} = 0$$

2. **No-slip condition:** The tangential component of velocity at the wall also equals zero. The fluid in direct contact with the wall moves with the wall:
$$\mathbf{v}\big\vert_{\text{wall}} = \mathbf{v}_{\text{wall}}$$

For a stationary wall, this reduces to $\mathbf{v}\big\vert_{\text{wall}} = 0$.

**Biomedical example:** Blood flow in an artery. The endothelial cells of the arterial wall are stationary, so the velocity of blood at the vessel wall is zero. This is the origin of the **Fåhræus-Lindqvist effect** in capillaries, where apparent blood viscosity decreases with vessel diameter.

**Boundary conditions at a fluid-fluid interface:**

1. **Continuity of velocity (kinematic condition):** The normal components of velocity must be equal on either side of the interface to prevent the interface from breaking or interpenetrating:
$$(\mathbf{v}_1 - \mathbf{v}_2) \cdot \mathbf{n} = 0$$

2. **Continuity of stress (dynamic condition):** The traction vector on either side of the interface must be balanced. Including surface tension $\gamma$, this gives:
$$\mathbf{n} \cdot (\mathbf{T}_2 - \mathbf{T}_1) = \gamma \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \right) \mathbf{n}$$

3. **Thermal condition:** If heat transfer occurs, temperature is continuous across the interface (assuming no phase change):
$$T_1 = T_2 \text{ at interface}$$

**Biomedical example:** Air-liquid interface in the alveoli of the lungs, where surface tension at the air-blood interface in the pulmonary capillaries plays a critical role in respiratory mechanics. The Laplace pressure $p = 2\gamma/R$ governs alveolar stability. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define a stream function $\psi$ for 2D incompressible flow. Show that the stream function satisfies the continuity equation automatically. Derive the velocity components in Cartesian and polar coordinates.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the principle of mass conservation in a stream tube, derive the 1D continuity equation $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$. Apply it to calculate the velocity in a 2 mm diameter artery that branches into eight identical 0.5 mm diameter capillaries, given the parent artery carries $4 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$ of blood.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Definition:** A stream function $\psi(x, y, t)$ is a scalar function such that, in 2D incompressible flow, the velocity components are derived as:
$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

**Proof that $\psi$ satisfies continuity automatically:**

The continuity equation for incompressible 2D flow is:
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

Substituting the stream function definitions:
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left( \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left( -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} \right) = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y \partial x} = 0$$

Since mixed partial derivatives are equal (assuming $\psi$ is smooth), the continuity equation is satisfied identically. **(3 marks)**

**Polar coordinates:** In 2D polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$, the velocity components are related to the stream function by:
$$v_r = \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta}, \qquad v_\theta = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}$$

Substituting into the polar form of continuity $\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial(r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} = 0$ also gives zero identically. **(4 marks)**

**Important property:** Streamlines correspond to lines of constant $\psi$. The volume flow rate between two streamlines $\psi_1$ and $\psi_2$ is $Q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Derivation of 1D continuity:**

Consider a stream tube of cross-sectional area $A$ with uniform velocity $V$ normal to the cross-section. Mass flow rate:
$$\dot{m} = \rho A V$$

For steady, incompressible flow, mass conservation requires that the mass flow rate entering section 1 equals the mass flow rate exiting section 2:
$$\rho A_1 V_1 = \rho A_2 V_2$$

Cancelling $\rho$ (incompressible):
$$\boxed{A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2}$$ **(3 marks)**

**Numerical Application:**

**Step 1 — Total flow into capillary bed:**
$$Q_{\text{parent}} = 4 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Flow per capillary (8 identical branches):**
$$Q_{\text{cap}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-6}}{8} = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-sectional area of each capillary:**
$$A_{\text{cap}} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Velocity in capillary:**
$$V_{\text{cap}} = \frac{Q_{\text{cap}}}{A_{\text{cap}}} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{1.963 \times 10^{-7}} = 2.547 \text{ m/s}$$ **(4 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Failing to convert cm³/s to m³/s before using SI units. Always convert ALL quantities to a single consistent unit system **before** substitution.
> 2. **Forgetting to divide by number of branches:** In bifurcation problems, total flow = $N \times$ flow per branch. Students often forget the factor of 8 in this type of problem.
> 3. **Diameter vs. radius confusion:** The area formula uses $d^2$, not $d$. A common error is using radius in the formula.
> 4. **No-slip boundary condition ignored:** The derived $V$ is the **average** velocity. The actual velocity profile is parabolic (Poiseuille flow), with $V_{\max} = 2 \bar{V}$.
> 5. **Stream function property forgotten:** In exam answers, students often fail to state that streamlines are lines of constant $\psi$, which is a 1-mark loss in Part (a).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Pathline** = trajectory of one particle; **Streamline** = tangent to velocity at fixed $t$; **Streakline** = locus of particles through a fixed point.
- [x] In **steady flow**, all three coincide; in unsteady flow, they are generally distinct.
- [x] **Stream tube** lateral surface has $\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n} = 0$ — no mass flux crosses it.
- [x] **Stream function** $\psi$ exists for 2D incompressible flow; $u = \partial \psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial \psi/\partial x$.
- [x] **Continuity (general):** $\partial \rho/\partial t + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{v}) = 0$.
- [x] **Continuity (incompressible):** $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0$.
- [x] **1D stream-tube continuity:** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ (for steady, incompressible flow).
- [x] **Material derivative:** $D/Dt = \partial/\partial t + \mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla$ (Eulerian to Lagrangian conversion).
- [x] **Reynolds Transport Theorem** bridges system and control-volume conservation laws.
- [x] **No-slip BC:** $\mathbf{v}\big\vert_{\text{wall}} = 0$ (for stationary wall); applies at vascular endothelium.
- [x] **No-penetration BC:** $\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n}\big\vert_{\text{wall}} = 0$ (valid for any solid surface).
- [x] **Fluid-fluid interface BCs:** Velocity continuity, stress continuity (with surface tension correction).
- [x] **Navier-Stokes equation** (Newtonian): $\rho D\mathbf{v}/Dt = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} + \rho \mathbf{g}$.
- [x] **Biomedical relevance:** Blood is non-Newtonian (shear-thinning), so the strict Navier-Stokes form is an approximation; corrections via the Casson or Carreau-Yasuda models are used in clinical research.
- [x] **Capillary blood flow** ($Re \ll 1$) is well described by **Stokes (creeping) flow**: $0 = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{v} + \rho \mathbf{g}$.
- [x] **High-Yield KTU Tip:** When asked for "differential form," always go through RTT → divergence theorem → localization. Examiners award marks for each of these conceptual steps.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Statics: Surface Tension
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT832 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fluid Statics: Surface Tension

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Surface Tension (γ)** is a fundamental physical property of the interface between two immiscible fluid phases (typically liquid-gas or liquid-liquid) that arises from the imbalance of **cohesive intermolecular forces** experienced by molecules located at the surface. Mathematically, it is defined as the **work (energy) required per unit area** to create a new surface, or equivalently, the **force per unit length** acting along a line on the surface, directed perpendicular to that line and tangential to the surface.

In SI units, surface tension is expressed in **N/m** (Newton per meter) or equivalently **J/m²** (Joule per square meter), reflecting its dual character as both an energy density and a tensile force per length.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Equivalent):**
> "Surface tension is the elastic tendency of a fluid surface which makes it acquire the least surface area possible. It is the force per unit length acting perpendicular to an imaginary line drawn on the liquid surface."

The **surface tension coefficient (γ)** is mathematically expressed as:

$$dW = \gamma \, dA$$

where $dW$ is the work done to increase the surface area by $dA$.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a tightly stretched trampoline (the surface) with a heavy ball (the liquid bulk) sitting on top. The molecules inside the liquid are pulled equally in **all directions** by neighboring molecules, so they experience a net force of **zero** — they are "comfortable." However, molecules at the surface have **no neighbors above them** (only air or vacuum). So they experience an **inward pull** that pulls the surface inward, much like the stretched trampoline fabric trying to contract to a smaller area.

**Key Intuition:** Surface tension is the surface trying to "shrink" itself to minimize area — this is why water droplets are spherical (a sphere has the **minimum surface area for a given volume**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Analogy — Lung Alveoli:**
> Think of millions of tiny air sacs in our lungs (alveoli) that must remain open for gas exchange. Surface tension at the air-liquid interface inside alveoli would normally cause them to collapse (Laplace's law). Our body produces a soap-like substance called **pulmonary surfactant** that *reduces* surface tension, preventing collapse. Premature babies lacking this surfactant suffer from **Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome (NRDS)** — a direct clinical consequence of surface tension physics.

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Molecular Origin

The origin of surface tension lies in **intermolecular forces**:

| Force Type | Origin | Examples |
|------------|--------|----------|
| **Cohesive forces** | Attraction between like molecules (within liquid) | Hydrogen bonds in water, Van der Waals in oils |
| **Adhesive forces** | Attraction between unlike molecules (liquid–solid) | Water–glass, Mercury–glass |

- Molecules in the **bulk** are pulled symmetrically (zero net force).
- Molecules at the **surface** experience a net **inward** cohesive pull.
- This creates a "tensioned skin" effect at the surface, characterized by γ.

> [!TIP]
> **Cohesive vs. Adhesive Memory Trick:**
> - **Co-hesion** = "**Co**-workers holding together" (same kind, same team).
> - **Ad-hesion** = "**Ad**vertising a different brand" (foreign, sticking to other surfaces).

---

## 1.4 Surface Tension in Biomedical Engineering — Why It Matters

Surface tension governs critical physiological phenomena:

1. **Alveolar mechanics** in the lungs (surfactant therapy).
2. **Capillary blood flow** in microcirculation.
3. **Tear film stability** in the eye (dry eye disease).
4. **Cell membrane bilayer mechanics** (lipid surface tension).
5. **Drug delivery** across liquid-liquid interfaces.
6. **Wound healing** and surface tension of biological fluids.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Value:** At **20 °C** and atmospheric pressure, the surface tension of **water against air is γ ≈ 0.0728 N/m** (or 72.8 dyne/cm). Blood plasma has γ ≈ 0.045–0.060 N/m due to dissolved proteins and lipids.

---

## 1.5 Surface Tension vs. Surface Energy — A Subtle Distinction

Although they share units and are often used interchangeably, there is a conceptual nuance:

- **Surface Energy (J/m²):** Energy per unit area required to form a new surface.
- **Surface Tension (N/m):** Force per unit length along the surface.

For a **single-component, pure interface**, these two quantities are **numerically equal** and represent the same molecular phenomenon expressed in different physical dimensions. This is because energy per area and force per length both have dimensions of **M·T⁻²** in SI base units.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phenomena Controlled by Surface Tension

Surface tension drives several observable macroscopic phenomena that students must recognize:

1. **Spherical droplets** — A free liquid droplet assumes spherical shape to minimize surface area for a given volume.
2. **Capillary rise / depression** — Liquids climb or fall in narrow tubes.
3. **Soap bubble formation** — Stable thin films require low surface tension.
4. **Beading of water on a waxy leaf** (lotus effect — superhydrophobicity).
5. **Emulsion stability** in pharmaceuticals (oil-in-water vs. water-in-oil).

---

## 2.2 Laplace Pressure (Young–Laplace Equation) — The Central Theorem

When a curved interface exists between two fluids, there is a **pressure difference** across the interface. This pressure difference is sustained by surface tension acting along the curved boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Young–Laplace Equation (General Form):**
> The pressure jump across a curved fluid interface equals the surface tension multiplied by the **mean curvature** of the interface.

$$\Delta P = \gamma \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \right)$$

where:
- $\Delta P$ = pressure difference (inside minus outside), **Pa**
- $\gamma$ = surface tension, **N/m**
- $R_1, R_2$ = principal radii of curvature, **m**

**Special Cases (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):**

| Geometry | Radii | Laplace Equation | Application |
|----------|-------|------------------|-------------|
| **Spherical droplet (single surface)** | $R_1 = R_2 = R$ | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma}{R}$ | Mist, aerosols |
| **Soap bubble (two surfaces)** | $R_1 = R_2 = R$ | $\Delta P = \dfrac{4\gamma}{R}$ | Lung alveoli, foam |
| **Cylindrical jet** | $R_1 = R, R_2 = \infty$ | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\gamma}{R}$ | Liquid columns |
| **Planar surface** | $R_1 = R_2 = \infty$ | $\Delta P = 0$ | Bulk flat liquid |

---

## 2.3 Capillarity and the Capillary Rise Equation

When a narrow tube (capillary) is placed in a liquid, the liquid either **rises** or **falls** within the tube depending on the balance between adhesive and cohesive forces.

**Jurin's Law (Capillary Rise Formula):**

$$h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g R}$$

where:
- $h$ = height of liquid column rise (m)
- $\gamma$ = surface tension (N/m)
- $\theta$ = contact angle (radians or degrees)
- $\rho$ = density of liquid (kg/m³)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $R$ = radius of capillary tube (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Wetting vs. Non-Wetting Behavior:**
> - If $\theta < 90°$ → $\cos\theta > 0$ → liquid **rises** (e.g., water in glass). Wetting case.
> - If $\theta > 90°$ → $\cos\theta < 0$ → liquid **falls** (e.g., mercury in glass). Non-wetting case.

---

## 2.4 Contact Angle (θ) — Wetting Characteristics

The **contact angle** is the angle formed at the three-phase contact line where liquid, solid, and gas meet. It is governed by **Young's Equation**:

$$\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG} \cos\theta$$

where:
- $\gamma_{SG}$ = solid–gas interfacial tension
- $\gamma_{SL}$ = solid–liquid interfacial tension
- $\gamma_{LG}$ = liquid–gas surface tension

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spreading Coefficient (S):**
> $$S = \gamma_{SG} - (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG})$$
> - If $S \geq 0$ → complete wetting (spreading film).
> - If $S < 0$ → partial wetting (discrete droplet forms).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master the following table — these equations appear in nearly every KTU ESE question on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | SI Units |
|---|---------|------------------|----------|
| 1 | $\gamma = \dfrac{F}{L}$ | Force per unit length on a surface line | N/m |
| 2 | $W = \gamma \cdot \Delta A$ | Work to create new surface | J |
| 3 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma}{R}$ | Laplace pressure (droplet) | Pa |
| 4 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{4\gamma}{R}$ | Laplace pressure (soap bubble) | Pa |
| 5 | $\Delta P = \gamma\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2}\right)$ | General Young–Laplace | Pa |
| 6 | $h = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g R}$ | Capillary rise (Jurin's law) | m |
| 7 | $\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG} \cos\theta$ | Young's contact angle equation | N/m |
| 8 | $S = \gamma_{SG} - (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG})$ | Spreading coefficient | N/m |
| 9 | $\Pi = \dfrac{RT}{A_m}\left[1 - \dfrac{x}{x_m}\right]$ | Surface pressure (Langmuir trough, biomembranes) | N/m |
| 10 | $\Delta P_{alv} = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r}$ | Alveolar pressure (surfactant critical) | Pa |

> Symbols: $F$ = force; $L$ = length; $R$ = radius; $\rho$ = density; $g$ = gravity; $r$ = alveolar radius; $A_m$ = area per molecule; $x$ = mole fraction; $T$ = temperature.

---

## 2.6 Surface Tension of Common Biomedical Fluids (Reference Data)

| Fluid | Surface Tension (N/m) at 20–25 °C |
|-------|-------------------------------------|
| Pure water | 0.0728 |
| Blood plasma | 0.045 – 0.060 |
| Whole blood | ~ 0.055 |
| Tears | ~ 0.040 – 0.050 |
| Lung surfactant film | ~ 0.005 – 0.030 (variable) |
| Mercury (Hg) | ~ 0.485 |
| Olive oil | ~ 0.032 |
| Ethanol | ~ 0.022 |

---

## 2.7 Why This Matters — Real Engineering Utility

- **Mechanical Engineering:** Design of inkjet print heads, microfluidic devices, fuel injectors.
- **Civil Engineering:** Capillary rise in concrete pores (dampness in buildings).
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Pulmonary surfactant dosing, ocular drug delivery, biosensor surface functionalization.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Emulsion polymerization, foams, distillation trays.
- **Materials Science:** Self-cleaning surfaces (lotus effect), hydrophobic coatings.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "explain the importance of surface tension in a biomedical context," always tie the answer to a **specific physiological function** (alveolar mechanics, tear film, capillary blood flow). Generic answers lose marks.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Young–Laplace Equation for a Spherical Droplet

### Objective
Derive an expression for the pressure difference between the inside and outside of a spherical liquid droplet of radius $R$ in terms of surface tension $\gamma$.

### Physical Setup
Consider a spherical droplet of liquid in equilibrium with its vapor. The droplet is in **mechanical and thermodynamic equilibrium** with its surroundings. The molecules at the surface experience a net inward pull (cohesion), which compresses the droplet slightly, raising the internal pressure above the external pressure by an amount $\Delta P = P_{inside} - P_{outside}$.

### Method — Force Balance Across a Hemispherical Cross-Section

We perform a **virtual displacement analysis** (energy method / force balance) to find the equilibrium condition.

**Step 1:** Imagine cutting the sphere into two equal hemispheres using a great circle.

**Step 2:** The two hemispheres are held together by surface tension acting along the circular cut of length $2\pi R$ on each face. Since a sphere has a single surface, the total surface tension force pulling the hemispheres together is:

$$F_{\gamma} = \gamma \cdot (2\pi R)$$

**Step 3:** The internal pressure $P_{in}$ pushes outward on the projected circular area $\pi R^2$, while the external pressure $P_{out}$ pushes inward on the same area. The net outward force from the pressure difference is:

$$F_P = \Delta P \cdot \pi R^2 = (P_{in} - P_{out}) \cdot \pi R^2$$

**Step 4:** At mechanical equilibrium, the pressure force pushing the hemispheres apart is balanced by the surface tension force pulling them together:

$$F_P = F_{\gamma}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting the expressions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(P_{in} - P_{out}) \cdot \pi R^2 &= \gamma \cdot 2\pi R \\
\Delta P \cdot \pi R^2 &= 2\pi R \gamma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $\Delta P$ by dividing both sides by $\pi R^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= \frac{2\pi R \gamma}{\pi R^2} \\
\Delta P &= \frac{2\gamma}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result (Spherical Droplet):**
> $$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{R}}$$

### Physical Interpretation
- The **smaller** the droplet, the **larger** the internal pressure. This is why very fine mist droplets evaporate rapidly (high internal pressure raises vapor pressure → Kelvin effect).
- For a **soap bubble** (which has **two** surfaces — inner and outer), the equation becomes:
  $$\Delta P_{bubble} = \frac{4\gamma}{R}$$
  because the surface tension force acts on **two** interfaces, doubling the tensile force.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capillary Rise (Jurin's Law)

### Objective
Derive the equilibrium height of a liquid column rising in a capillary tube of radius $R$ in terms of surface tension $\gamma$, contact angle $\theta$, liquid density $\rho$, and gravity $g$.

### Physical Setup
A glass capillary tube of inner radius $R$ is dipped vertically into a wetting liquid (e.g., water). The liquid rises up the tube to a height $h$ above the free external surface, forming a **concave meniscus** at the top.

### Method — Force Balance on the Liquid Column

Three forces act on the raised liquid column inside the tube:

**Step 1: Upward Force — Surface Tension Component**

Surface tension acts along the contact line at the meniscus, with total length $2\pi R$ (perimeter of the tube). The vertical (upward) component of the surface tension force is:

$$F_{up} = \gamma \cdot (2\pi R) \cdot \cos\theta$$

The factor $\cos\theta$ extracts the **vertical component** because surface tension is directed tangent to the meniscus, making an angle $\theta$ with the vertical tube wall.

**Step 2: Downward Force — Weight of the Liquid Column**

The column of raised liquid has volume $V = \pi R^2 h$ and mass $m = \rho \pi R^2 h$. Its weight is:

$$F_{down} = mg = \rho \pi R^2 h g$$

**Step 3: Downward Force — Atmospheric Pressure on Top of Column**

In standard problems, the pressure on top of the meniscus and the external free surface are both atmospheric, so these **cancel** and do not appear in the final equation.

**Step 4: Equilibrium Condition**

At equilibrium, upward force = downward force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{up} &= F_{down} \\
\gamma \cdot 2\pi R \cdot \cos\theta &= \rho \pi R^2 h g
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $h$ by dividing both sides by $\pi R g$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{R} &= \rho g h \\
h &= \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g R}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result (Jurin's Law — Capillary Rise):**
> $$\boxed{h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g R}}$$

### Engineering Insight
- Capillary rise is **inversely proportional** to tube radius — finer tubes produce higher rise.
- This explains water transport in **plant xylem** (very narrow vessels) and **soil moisture migration** in civil engineering.
- In **biomedical microcirculation**, capillary rise is critical for understanding interstitial fluid transport.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure Inside an Alveolus with Surfactant

### Objective
Determine the alveolar pressure in terms of alveolar radius $r$ and surface tension $\gamma$ in the surfactant lining. Show why surfactant is essential.

### Setup
Alveoli are tiny spherical air sacs in the lungs, lined with a thin liquid film containing **pulmonary surfactant**. Treat the alveolus as a sphere with a **single air-liquid interface** (since alveoli open to air, not closed bubbles — unlike soap bubbles which have two surfaces).

### Derivation

By the Young–Laplace equation for a spherical single surface:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{r}
$$

where:
- $\Delta P = P_{inside} - P_{outside}$ = alveolar pressure relative to pleural cavity.
- $\gamma$ = surface tension of the surfactant film.
- $r$ = alveolar radius.

Therefore:

$$
P_{alveolar} = P_{pleural} + \frac{2\gamma}{r}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
- Typical alveolar radius: $r = 100 \, \mu m = 100 \times 10^{-6} \, m$
- Without surfactant: $\gamma \approx 0.070 \, N/m$ (like water)
  $$\Delta P = \frac{2 \times 0.070}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 1400 \, Pa \approx 14 \, cmH_2O$$
  (This is dangerously high and would collapse the alveolus.)

- With surfactant: $\gamma \approx 0.005 \, N/m$ (15× reduction)
  $$\Delta P = \frac{2 \times 0.005}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 100 \, Pa \approx 1 \, cmH_2O$$
  (Manageable pressure — alveolus remains open.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Insight:** Surfactant **reduces surface tension dramatically**, lowering the pressure needed to keep alveoli open. **Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome (NRDS)** in premature infants occurs because surfactant production begins only after ~26 weeks of gestation. Treatment involves exogenous surfactant instillation into the trachea.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Problem Classification

| Problem Type | Given | To Find | Governing Equation |
|--------------|-------|---------|--------------------|
| Laplace pressure in droplet | $\gamma, R$ | $\Delta P$ | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma}{R}$ |
| Laplace pressure in bubble | $\gamma, R$ | $\Delta P$ | $\Delta P = \dfrac{4\gamma}{R}$ |
| Capillary rise | $\gamma, \theta, \rho, R$ | $h$ | $h = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g R}$ |
| Capillary tube radius | $h, \gamma, \theta, \rho$ | $R$ | $R = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g h}$ |
| Contact angle | $\gamma_{SG}, \gamma_{SL}, \gamma_{LG}$ | $\theta$ | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\gamma_{SG} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LG}}$ |
| Force on a contact line | $\gamma, L$ | $F$ | $F = \gamma L$ |
| Work to form a surface | $\gamma, A$ | $W$ | $W = \gamma A$ |

## 4.4 Verbal Schematic — The Young–Laplace Pressure Jump

```
  INSIDE (Higher Pressure P_in)
  ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
  \                           /
   \         _______         /
    \       /       \       /        ← Curved Interface
     \     /  γ film \     /            (Surface Tension γ)
      \   /  ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ \   /
       \_/_____________\_/
  -------------------------
  OUTSIDE (Lower Pressure P_out)

  ΔP = P_in − P_out = γ (1/R₁ + 1/R₂)
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Molecules inside are "pushed" outward by higher internal pressure.
- Surface tension acts tangentially along the curved film.
- The film is "stretched" — molecules at the curve experience this tension.
- Equilibrium between pressure force and surface tension force yields Young–Laplace.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters:** The Young–Laplace jump is the **conceptual bridge** between molecular surface tension and macroscopic pressure. Every lung alveolus, every soap bubble, and every water droplet in a cloud is governed by this same physical principle.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Surface Tension Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Laplace's law for bubbles** — Students often write $\Delta P = 2\gamma/R$ for both droplets AND bubbles, but soap bubbles have **two surfaces** (inner + outer) → use $4\gamma/R$.
> 2. **Sign error in capillary rise** — When $\theta > 90°$ (non-wetting, e.g., mercury), $h$ is **negative**, indicating depression, not rise. Always state the physical interpretation.
> 3. **Confusing surface tension (N/m) with surface energy (J/m²)** — They are numerically equal in pure interfaces but have different dimensional interpretations.
> 4. **Unit mismatch** — Always convert μm to m, dyne/cm to N/m, and cm H₂O to Pa before plugging into formulas.
> 5. **Forgetting Young's equation components** — In contact angle problems, students often write only the surface tension without the interfacial tensions.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Define surface tension. Mention its SI unit and one biomedical application.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Surface tension is the property of a liquid surface by which it behaves like a stretched elastic membrane and tends to contract to the minimum possible surface area. It is defined as the **force per unit length** acting on an imaginary line drawn on the liquid surface, directed perpendicular to the line and tangent to the surface.

**SI Unit (1 mark):** **N/m** (Newton per meter), equivalently J/m².

**Biomedical Application (1 mark):** Surface tension plays a vital role in the stability of pulmonary alveoli. The **pulmonary surfactant** lining the alveoli reduces surface tension, preventing alveolar collapse. A deficiency of surfactant in premature infants causes **Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome (NRDS)**.

---

### Question A2

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> *State the Young–Laplace equation and explain the significance of Laplace pressure in lung alveoli.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Equation (1.5 marks):** The Young–Laplace equation for a spherical interface is:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{R}$$

where $\Delta P$ is the pressure difference across the interface, $\gamma$ is the surface tension, and $R$ is the radius of curvature.

**Significance in Alveoli (1.5 marks):** Laplace pressure dictates the pressure required to keep an alveolus open. Since $\Delta P \propto \frac{1}{R}$, **smaller alveoli require higher internal pressure** to remain inflated. Pulmonary surfactant dynamically reduces $\gamma$ as the alveolar radius decreases (during expiration), preventing collapse. This is described by the **Law of LaPlace applied to respiration**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**
> **(a)** Derive the Young–Laplace equation for the pressure difference across a spherical liquid droplet. State how the equation changes for a soap bubble. (7 marks)
> **(b)** A spherical air bubble of radius **1 mm** is released from the bottom of a lake where the water temperature is uniform. The surface tension of water is **0.072 N/m** and the atmospheric pressure is **1.013 × 10⁵ Pa**. Calculate the **total pressure inside the bubble** when it is at a depth of **10 m** below the free surface. Take water density ρ = 1000 kg/m³ and g = 9.81 m/s². (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Derivation of Young–Laplace Equation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider a spherical liquid droplet of radius $R$ surrounded by its vapor. The internal pressure $P_{in}$ exceeds the external pressure $P_{out}$ due to surface tension.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Imagine the sphere divided into two hemispheres by a great circle. The surface tension acts along this circular contact line of length $2\pi R$, pulling the two hemispheres together with a force:
$$F_{\gamma} = \gamma \cdot 2\pi R$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The net outward force from the pressure difference acting on the projected circular area $\pi R^2$ is:
$$F_P = (P_{in} - P_{out}) \cdot \pi R^2 = \Delta P \cdot \pi R^2$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** At mechanical equilibrium:
$$F_P = F_{\gamma}$$
$$\Delta P \cdot \pi R^2 = 2\pi R \gamma$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Solving for $\Delta P$:
$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{R}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** A soap bubble has **two** air-liquid interfaces (inner and outer surfaces). The total surface tension force is therefore doubled:
$$F_{\gamma,bubble} = 2 \cdot 2\pi R \gamma = 4\pi R \gamma$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** The Laplace pressure for a soap bubble becomes:
$$\Delta P_{bubble} = \frac{4\gamma}{R}$$

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Pressure Inside Bubble at Depth (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Bubble radius: $R = 1 \, mm = 1 \times 10^{-3} \, m$
- Surface tension: $\gamma = 0.072 \, N/m$
- Atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5 \, Pa$
- Depth: $h = 10 \, m$
- $\rho = 1000 \, kg/m^3$, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Hydrostatic pressure due to water column at depth 10 m:
$$P_{hydro} = \rho g h = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 10 = 9.81 \times 10^4 \, Pa = 98100 \, Pa$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Pressure outside the bubble (hydrostatic + atmospheric):
$$P_{out} = P_{atm} + P_{hydro} = 1.013 \times 10^5 + 9.81 \times 10^4$$
$$P_{out} = 1.013 \times 10^5 + 0.981 \times 10^5 = 1.994 \times 10^5 \, Pa$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Laplace pressure (single surface, like droplet):
$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{R} = \frac{2 \times 0.072}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.144}{10^{-3}} = 144 \, Pa$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Total pressure inside the bubble:
$$P_{in} = P_{out} + \Delta P = 1.994 \times 10^5 + 144$$
$$P_{in} = 1.994 \times 10^5 + 1.44 \times 10^2$$
$$\boxed{P_{in} \approx 1.9954 \times 10^5 \, Pa \approx 199.54 \, kPa}$$

**Note (1 mark included):** The Laplace pressure contribution (144 Pa) is small compared to hydrostatic pressure (~98.1 kPa), but becomes dominant for very small bubbles (e.g., R = 1 μm → ΔP = 144 kPa).

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**
> **(a)** Explain the phenomenon of capillarity. Derive Jurin's law for capillary rise in a tube and state the assumptions made. (7 marks)
> **(b)** A capillary tube of inner radius **0.5 mm** is dipped into water at **25 °C**. The contact angle between water and glass is **30°**. Calculate the **height of water rise** in the tube. Given: surface tension of water = **0.072 N/m**, density of water = **1000 kg/m³**, g = **9.81 m/s²**. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Capillarity and Jurin's Law (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Phenomenon (1.5 marks):**
Capillarity is the rise or fall of a liquid in a narrow tube (capillary) due to the combined effects of **surface tension** and **wetting characteristics** of the liquid with the tube wall. If adhesive forces (liquid-solid) exceed cohesive forces (liquid-liquid), the liquid **rises** (wetting case, e.g., water-glass). If cohesive forces dominate, the liquid **falls** (non-wetting, e.g., mercury-glass).

**Step 2 — Setup (1 mark):**
Consider a capillary tube of inner radius $R$ dipped in a wetting liquid. The liquid rises to a height $h$ above the free surface, forming a concave meniscus with contact angle $\theta$.

**Step 3 — Force balance (2 marks):**
- Upward force from surface tension along the contact line (perimeter $2\pi R$):
$$F_{up} = \gamma \cdot 2\pi R \cdot \cos\theta$$
- Downward force from weight of the liquid column:
$$F_{down} = \rho \cdot \pi R^2 h \cdot g$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium (1 mark):**
$$\gamma \cdot 2\pi R \cos\theta = \rho \pi R^2 h g$$
$$h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g R}$$

**Step 5 — Assumptions (1.5 marks):**
1. The meniscus is spherical (valid when $R$ is very small, gravity effects negligible).
2. Contact angle $\theta$ is constant along the contact line.
3. Temperature is uniform.
4. The tube is vertical.
5. The liquid is pure and homogeneous.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Calculation of Capillary Rise (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $R = 0.5 \, mm = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \, m = 5 \times 10^{-4} \, m$
- $\theta = 30°$, so $\cos\theta = \cos 30° = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$
- $\gamma = 0.072 \, N/m$
- $\rho = 1000 \, kg/m^3$, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Plug into Jurin's law:
$$h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g R}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Substitute the values:
$$h = \frac{2 \times 0.072 \times 0.866}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the numerator:
$$\text{Numerator} = 2 \times 0.072 \times 0.866 = 0.144 \times 0.866 = 0.1247 \, N/m$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute the denominator:
$$\text{Denominator} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 9.81 \times 0.5 = 4.905 \, N/m^3$$

Wait — units check: $1000 \times 9.81 = 9810$; $\times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 4.905$. Units: $\frac{N/m}{N/m^3} = m$ ✓

**Step 5 (Bonus for clean result):** Final answer:
$$h = \frac{0.1247}{4.905} \approx 0.02543 \, m$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 2.54 \, cm}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Surface Tension (Module 1)**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ Surface tension = **force per unit length** OR **energy per unit area** at a fluid interface.
- ☐ Origin: **Imbalance of cohesive forces** at the surface (molecules experience net inward pull).
- ☐ SI Unit: **N/m**; CGS Unit: **dyne/cm**; Conversion: 1 N/m = 1000 dyne/cm.
- ☐ **Standard value for water at 20 °C**: γ ≈ **0.0728 N/m**.

**Key Equations (must memorize):**
- ☐ Laplace pressure (droplet): $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma}{R}$
- ☐ Laplace pressure (soap bubble): $\Delta P = \dfrac{4\gamma}{R}$
- ☐ General Laplace: $\Delta P = \gamma\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2}\right)$
- ☐ Capillary rise (Jurin's law): $h = \dfrac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g R}$
- ☐ Young's contact angle: $\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG} \cos\theta$
- ☐ Work to form a surface: $W = \gamma \cdot A$

**Phenomena & Distinctions:**
- ☐ **Cohesive** forces = like molecules; **Adhesive** forces = unlike molecules.
- ☐ Wetting case: $\theta < 90°$, liquid rises in capillary (e.g., water-glass).
- ☐ Non-wetting case: $\theta > 90°$, liquid falls in capillary (e.g., mercury-glass).
- ☐ **Smaller radius** → **larger Laplace pressure** → bubbles/droplets under more internal stress.

**Biomedical Applications (high-yield):**
- ☐ **Pulmonary surfactant** reduces surface tension in alveoli, preventing collapse.
- ☐ **Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome (NRDS)** = surfactant deficiency.
- ☐ **Capillary rise** explains water transport in plant xylem and soil.
- ☐ **Tear film stability** depends on lipid layer surface tension (dry eye disease).
- ☐ **Cell membrane mechanics** governed by lipid bilayer surface tension.

**Common Pitfalls in KTU Exams:**
- ☐ Forgetting the **factor of 2** in bubble vs. droplet Laplace equation.
- ☐ Mixing up **surface tension (N/m)** with **surface energy (J/m²)** dimensions.
- ☐ Sign error in capillary rise when $\theta > 90°$.
- ☐ Unit conversion mistakes (μm → m, dyne/cm → N/m).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Memory Anchor:** "**Two for one, four for bubble**" — Droplet = 2γ/R, Soap Bubble = 4γ/R. This is the single most commonly tested distinction in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Membrane and Cortical Tension - Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Fluids - Laminar and Turbulent Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT832 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Membrane and Cortical Tension - Newtonian and Non-Newtonian Fluids - Laminar and Turbulent Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Membrane Tension ($\sigma_m$)

**Membrane Tension** is the in-plane tensile stress (force per unit length) existing within the lipid bilayer of a biological cell membrane. It arises from the lateral interactions between phospholipid molecules and from the adhesion of the membrane to the underlying cytoskeleton.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Membrane tension is defined as the isotropic in-plane stress per unit length, $\sigma_m = \Delta F / \Delta L$, measured in **N/m** (equivalent to J/m²). It is a 2D analogue of 3D pressure, but acting tangentially rather than normally to the membrane surface.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a fully inflated balloon. The stretched rubber skin tries to shrink, generating a tension along its surface. A cell membrane behaves similarly, but the tension is set by the lipid packing density, membrane area, and cortical attachment. When you poke a cell with an atomic force microscopy (AFM) tip or a micropipette, you are directly measuring this tension.

### 1.2 Cortical Tension ($\sigma_c$)

**Cortical Tension** is the active contractile stress generated by the **actomyosin cortex**, a thin meshwork of actin filaments and myosin II motors located just beneath the plasma membrane (~100–500 nm thick).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** Membrane tension is **passive** (set by lipid bilayer stretching). Cortical tension is **active** (driven by ATP-dependent myosin motors and regulated by Rho-GTPase signaling). Together, they govern cell shape, rounding during mitosis, and tissue morphogenesis.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of the cell membrane as a thin plastic sheet (the lipid bilayer) glued to a stretchy contractile sleeve (the cortex). When the sleeve tightens (myosin pulls actin), the sheet wrinkles, the cell rounds up, and the apparent surface tension rises — analogous to tightening the drawstring of a trash bag.

### 1.3 Newtonian Fluids

A **Newtonian fluid** obeys Newton's law of viscosity exactly: the shear stress is linearly proportional to the shear rate, and the constant of proportionality (the dynamic viscosity $\mu$) is independent of the shear rate.

$$\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples (Biomedical):** Water, saline, plasma (approximately), cerebrospinal fluid, synovial fluid (at high shear), air, and most low-molecular-weight liquids.

### 1.4 Non-Newtonian Fluids

A **Non-Newtonian fluid** is one in which the apparent viscosity $\eta(\dot{\gamma})$ depends on the shear rate (or shear history). The constitutive relation $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ with a constant $\mu$ does **not** hold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biological Example (Most Important):** **Whole blood** is the canonical non-Newtonian fluid in biomedical engineering. It exhibits **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** behavior because red blood cells (RBCs) aggregate at low shear (forming rouleaux) and deform/align at high shear, reducing apparent viscosity.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Compare **ketchup** (non-Newtonian, shear-thinning) with **water** (Newtonian). Ketchup resists flow when at rest in the bottle, but flows easily when you shake or squeeze it. Blood behaves the same way: it is "thick" in stagnant veins but becomes "thin" when squeezed through arterioles.

### 1.5 Laminar Flow

**Laminar flow** is a regime in which the fluid moves in smooth, parallel, well-defined streamlines with no macroscopic mixing between adjacent layers. Momentum and mass transport occur primarily by molecular diffusion across the streamlines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Governing Number:** $\text{Re} < \text{Re}_{crit} \approx 2300$ for flow in a straight circular pipe.

### 1.6 Turbulent Flow

**Turbulent flow** is characterized by chaotic, random fluctuations in velocity and pressure, with vigorous eddies and macroscopic mixing across streamlines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Governing Number:** $\text{Re} > 4000$ for fully turbulent flow in a pipe. The transition zone lies between 2300 and 4000.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Picture a wide, slow river (laminar) where you can clearly see the water gliding in layers. Now imagine rapids after heavy rain (turbulent) — water churns unpredictably, with vortices and splashes. The Reynolds number is the "rapidity index": low Re means calm, high Re means chaos.

| Phenomenon | Physical Origin | Biomedical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Membrane Tension | Lipid packing, bilayer stretching | Cell shape, endocytosis, exocytosis |
| Cortical Tension | Actomyosin contractility | Cell rounding, division, migration |
| Newtonian Fluid | Constant viscosity | Plasma, CSF — simple modeling |
| Non-Newtonian Fluid | Shear-dependent viscosity | Whole blood, mucus, synovial fluid |
| Laminar Flow | Low inertia | Venous flow, microcirculation |
| Turbulent Flow | High inertia | Aortic flow at peak systole, post-stenotic jets |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Membrane Tension — Theoretical Framework

The total apparent surface tension of a cell ($\sigma_{app}$) is the sum of the two contributions:

$$\sigma_{app} = \sigma_m + \sigma_c$$

where:
- $\sigma_m$ is the **membrane (bilayer) tension** — typically **$\sim 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-4}$ N/m**
- $\sigma_c$ is the **cortical tension** — typically **$\sim 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ N/m**

**Step-by-step logic of how each arises:**

1. **Lipid bilayer elasticity:** When the membrane area is stretched beyond its equilibrium value $A_0$, the lipids are pulled apart. The work required per unit area defines a stretching modulus $K_A$ (typically $K_A \approx 0.2$ N/m for DOPC bilayers).
2. **Membrane-cytoskeleton adhesion:** The membrane is tethered to the cortex via linker proteins (ezrin, ankyrin). The density of these linkers sets a baseline $\sigma_m$.
3. **Cortex contractility:** Myosin II hydrolyzes ATP and pulls antiparallel actin filaments together, generating an isotropic contractile stress in the cortical layer. The resulting surface tension scales as $\sigma_c \sim \zeta \cdot G$, where $G$ is the cortical shear modulus and $\zeta$ is the cortex thickness.

**Real-world engineering utility:** Micropipette aspiration, AFM indentation, and optical tweezers use these tension values to infer cell mechanical phenotypes — critical in cancer diagnostics (cancer cells are softer), drug screening, and stem-cell differentiation studies.

### 2.2 Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian — Constitutive Theory

**Newtonian Constitutive Equation (1D shear):**

$$\tau_{yx} = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where $\tau_{yx}$ is the shear stress [Pa], $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity [Pa·s], and $du/dy$ is the velocity gradient [s⁻¹].

**Generalized Newtonian (Non-Newtonian) — Ostwald–de Waele Power-Law Model:**

$$\tau = K \left( \dot{\gamma} \right)^n$$

where $K$ is the consistency index [Pa·sⁿ] and $n$ is the power-law index [dimensionless].

| Behavior | Value of $n$ | Apparent Viscosity Trend |
|---|---|---|
| Newtonian | $n = 1$ | $\eta = K$ (constant) |
| Shear-thinning (Pseudoplastic) | $n < 1$ | $\eta$ decreases with $\dot{\gamma}$ |
| Shear-thickening (Dilatant) | $n > 1$ | $\eta$ increases with $\dot{\gamma}$ |
| Bingham Plastic | $\tau = \tau_y + \mu_p \dot{\gamma}$ | Yield stress $\tau_y$ must be exceeded |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blood is shear-thinning:** Casson's model $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\mu_p \dot{\gamma}}$ is more accurate at very low shear, capturing the yield stress caused by RBC aggregation.

### 2.3 Laminar vs. Turbulent Flow — Reynolds Number

The dimensionless **Reynolds number** characterizes the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces:

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D_h}{\mu} = \frac{\text{Inertial forces}}{\text{Viscous forces}}$$

For a circular pipe, $D_h = D$ (diameter). For non-circular conduits, $D_h = 4A/P$ (hydraulic diameter).

**Physical interpretation:**
- $\text{Re} \ll 1$: creeping (Stokes) flow — viscous forces dominate
- $\text{Re} \sim 1$ to $2300$: laminar — inertial and viscous comparable
- $\text{Re} > 4000$: turbulent — inertia dominates

**Why transition occurs:** Small disturbances in the flow are damped by viscosity at low Re but amplified by inertia at high Re, until nonlinear coupling produces chaotic eddies.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{app} = \sigma_m + \sigma_c$ | Total cell surface tension | N/m |
| 2 | $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ | Newton's law of viscosity | Pa |
| 3 | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Ostwald–de Waele power law | Pa |
| 4 | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\mu_p \dot{\gamma}}$ | Casson model (blood) | Pa$^{1/2}$ |
| 5 | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | Kinematic viscosity | m²/s |
| 6 | $\text{Re} = \rho v D / \mu$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 7 | $D_h = 4A / P$ | Hydraulic diameter | m |
| 8 | $v_{avg} = Q / A$ | Average velocity from flow rate | m/s |
| 9 | $\Delta P = 32 \mu L v / D^2$ | Hagen–Poiseuille pressure drop | Pa |
| 10 | $Q = \pi D^4 \Delta P / 128 \mu L$ | Hagen–Poiseuille flow rate | m³/s |
| 11 | $f = 64 / \text{Re}$ | Friction factor (laminar pipe) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\tau_w = \Delta P D / 4 L$ | Wall shear stress | Pa |
| 13 | $v_{max} = 2 v_{avg}$ | Centerline velocity (laminar) | m/s |
| 14 | $\dot{\gamma}_w = 8 v_{avg} / D$ | Wall shear rate (laminar pipe) | s⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value bars in the table have been replaced with slashes or omitted to preserve markdown table integrity. For example, $\vert v \vert$ is rendered as plain $v$ when context is unambiguous.

### 2.5 Engineering / Clinical Utility

- **Drug delivery design:** Non-Newtonian models predict pressure–flow relationships for viscous injectables.
- **Stent & graft design:** Reynolds number helps predict whether aortic flow is laminar (healthy) or turbulent (pathological).
- **Dialysis & IV lines:** Hagen–Poiseuille predicts flow resistance in tubing.
- **Rheology of mucus:** Critical for cystic fibrosis, pulmonary drug delivery.
- **Cell mechanics assays:** Quantify malignant transformation via tension changes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Wall Shear Stress and Shear Rate in Laminar Pipe Flow

We begin with the Navier–Stokes equation for steady, fully-developed, incompressible laminar flow in a horizontal pipe (no body forces, axial direction $z$):

$$0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left( r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} \right) \right]$$

**Step 1 — Recognize that the pressure gradient is constant:**

Because the flow is fully developed, $\partial v_z / \partial z = 0$. The only variable is $r$, so $\partial P / \partial z = \Delta P / L = $ constant (negative for flow in $+z$).

$$-\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{\mu}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $r / \mu$ and integrate once with respect to $r$:**

$$-\frac{\Delta P}{\mu L} r = \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right)$$

Integrating:

$$r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} r^2 + C_1$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $r$ and integrate a second time:**

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} r + \frac{C_1}{r}$$

Integrating:

$$v_z(r) = -\frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} r^2 + C_1 \ln r + C_2$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions:**

- BC1 (axisymmetry): velocity finite at $r = 0$ ⟹ $C_1 = 0$
- BC2 (no-slip at wall): $v_z(R) = 0$ ⟹ $C_2 = \dfrac{\Delta P \, R^2}{4 \mu L}$

Substituting:

$$v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \left( R^2 - r^2 \right)$$

This is the **parabolic (Poiseuille) velocity profile**.

**Step 5 — Derive the wall shear stress:**

$$\tau_{rz}\big\vert_{r=R} = \mu \left. \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right\vert_{r=R} = -\frac{\Delta P \, R}{2 L}$$

The magnitude of the wall shear stress is:

$$\boxed{\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \, D}{4 L}}$$

**Step 6 — Derive the wall shear rate:**

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \left\vert \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right\vert_{r=R} = \frac{\Delta P \, R}{2 \mu L}$$

Using the relation $\Delta P = 32 \mu L v_{avg} / D^2$:

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{32 \mu L v_{avg} / D^2 \cdot D/2}{2 \mu L} = \frac{8 v_{avg}}{D}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{8 v_{avg}}{D}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Reynolds Number from Force Ratio

The Navier–Stokes equation can be non-dimensionalized to extract dimensionless groups. Starting with the $x$-momentum equation:

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} + v_x \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + v_y \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + v_z \frac{\partial v_x}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 v_x + \rho g_x$$

**Step 1 — Introduce dimensionless variables:**

Let $x^* = x / L$, $v^* = v / V$, $P^* = P / (\rho V^2)$, $t^* = t V / L$.

**Step 2 — Substitute and divide by $\rho V^2 / L$:**

$$\frac{\partial v_x^*}{\partial t^*} + v_x^* \frac{\partial v_x^*}{\partial x^*} + v_y^* \frac{\partial v_x^*}{\partial y^*} + v_z^* \frac{\partial v_x^*}{\partial z^*} = -\frac{\partial P^*}{\partial x^*} + \frac{\mu}{\rho V L} \nabla^{*2} v_x^* + \frac{g L}{V^2}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the dimensionless groups:**

- Coefficient of inertial term: $\mathcal{O}(1)$
- Coefficient of viscous term: $\dfrac{\mu}{\rho V L} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Re}}$
- Coefficient of gravity term: $\dfrac{g L}{V^2} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Fr}^2}$

**Step 4 — Define the Reynolds number:**

$$\boxed{\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}}$$

For a pipe of diameter $D$, $L \to D$ and $V$ is the average velocity.

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Problem — Non-Newtonian Blood Flow

A patient has a partially stenosed carotid artery modeled as a circular tube of diameter $D = 4$ mm, where the average blood velocity is $v_{avg} = 0.5$ m/s. Blood density $\rho = 1060$ kg/m³, and at this shear rate the apparent viscosity is $\eta_{app} = 0.004$ Pa·s. Determine (a) the Reynolds number, (b) the flow regime, and (c) the wall shear stress assuming Hagen–Poiseuille flow.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Compute Reynolds number:**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\eta_{app}}$$

$$\text{Re} = \frac{1060 \times 0.5 \times 0.004}{0.004} = \frac{2.12}{0.004} = 530$$

**Part (b) — Identify flow regime:**

Since $\text{Re} = 530 \ll 2300$, the flow is **laminar**.

**Part (c) — Compute wall shear stress (Poiseuille):**

$$\Delta P / L = 32 \eta_{app} v_{avg} / D^2 = 32 \times 0.004 \times 0.5 / (0.004)^2 = 0.064 / 0.000016 = 4000 \text{ Pa/m}$$

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \, D}{4 L} = \frac{4000 \times 0.004}{4} = 4 \text{ Pa}$$

This physiological wall shear stress (~4 Pa) is within the healthy arterial range of 1–7 Pa that promotes endothelial quiescence and atheroprotection.

### 3.4 Verification Matrix for Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| Problem Type | Key Inputs | Key Output | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compute Re in vessel | $D$, $v$, $\rho$, $\mu$ | Re | $\text{Re} = \rho v D / \mu$ |
| Compute flow rate | $\Delta P$, $D$, $L$, $\mu$ | $Q$ | $Q = \pi D^4 \Delta P / 128 \mu L$ |
| Compute wall shear | $\Delta P$, $D$, $L$ | $\tau_w$ | $\tau_w = \Delta P D / 4 L$ |
| Identify non-Newtonian type | $n$ value | Fluid type | $n<1$ shear-thinning, etc. |
| Cell tension contribution | $\sigma_m$, $\sigma_c$ | $\sigma_{app}$ | $\sigma_{app} = \sigma_m + \sigma_c$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Architecture of Cell Surface Tension

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cell Surface Apparent Tension sigma_app] --> B[sigma_m Membrane Tension]
    A --> C[sigma_c Cortical Tension]
    B --> B1[Lipid Bilayer Stretching Modulus K_A]
    B --> B2[Membrane Cytoskeleton Linker Density]
    B --> B3[Spontaneous Curvature Effects]
    C --> C1[Actin Cortex Thickness zeta]
    C --> C2[Myosin II Contractility]
    C --> C3[Cortex Shear Modulus G]
    C2 --> C2a[ATP Hydrolysis]
    C2 --> C2b[RhoA ROCK Signaling]
    A --> D[Functional Outcomes]
    D --> D1[Cell Shape Changes]
    D --> D2[Mitosis Cell Rounding]
    D --> D3[Endocytosis Exocytosis Regulation]
    D --> D4[Mechanosensing]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Constitutive Fluid Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Fluid Sample] --> Q1{Linear stress vs shear rate}
    Q1 -- Yes --> N[Newtonian Fluid]
    N --> N1[Water]
    N --> N2[Plasma approximately]
    N --> N3[CSF]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Does yield stress exist}
    Q2 -- Yes --> BP[Bingham Plastic]
    BP --> BP1[Toothpaste]
    BP --> BP2[Drilling Mud]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Power law index n}
    Q3 -- n less than 1 --> ST[Shear Thinning Pseudoplastic]
    ST --> ST1[Blood]
    ST --> ST2[Ketchup]
    ST --> ST3[Mucus]
    Q3 -- n greater than 1 --> TH[Shear Thickening Dilatant]
```
