```mermaid
    TH --> TH2[Wet sand]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Flow Regime Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Given Geometry Fluid and Velocity] --> C1[Compute Re equals rho v D over mu]
    C1 --> D{Re less than 2300}
    D -- Yes --> L[Laminar Flow]
    L --> L1[Parabolic Poiseuille Profile]
    L --> L2[Re below 1 Stokes Creeping]
    D -- Transition 2300 to 4000 --> T[Transitional Flow]
    D -- No Re greater than 4000 --> TURB[Turbulent Flow]
    TURB --> TURB1[Flat Velocity Core]
    TURB --> TURB2[Eddies and Fluctuations]
    TURB --> TURB3[Enhanced Mass and Momentum Transfer]
    L --> APP[Biomedical Applications]
    TURB --> APP
    APP --> A1[Healthy Arteries Laminar]
    APP --> A2[Post Stenotic Jets Turbulent]
    APP --> A3[Microcirculation Laminar]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Membrane vs. Cortical Tension Functional Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Passive_Origin
        M1[Lipid Bilayer Packing] --> MT[Membrane Tension sigma_m]
        M2[Membrane Area Stretching] --> MT
        M3[Membrane Skeleton Adhesion] --> MT
    end
    subgraph Active_Origin
        A1[Actin Network Architecture] --> CT[Cortical Tension sigma_c]
        A2[Myosin II Motor Activity] --> CT
        A3[Cell Signaling Rho GTPase] --> CT
    end
    MT --> SUM[Total sigma_app]
    CT --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT1[Mechanical Equilibrium]
    SUM --> OUT2[Cell Shape Determination]
    SUM --> OUT3[Force Balance with Substrate]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Distinguish clearly between membrane tension and cortical tension in a biological cell. Which one is active and which is passive, and why?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Membrane tension ($\sigma_m$):** Passive, in-plane tensile stress residing within the lipid bilayer itself. It originates from lateral lipid–lipid interactions, the stretching modulus $K_A$ of the bilayer, and the adhesion of the membrane to the underlying cytoskeleton. It changes only when the membrane area is physically deformed.
- **Cortical tension ($\sigma_c$):** Active, ATP-dependent contractile stress generated by myosin II motors pulling on the actin cortex just beneath the membrane. It can be rapidly modulated by biochemical signaling (RhoA/ROCK pathway) without any change in membrane area.
- The apparent total tension measured in experiments is $\sigma_{app} = \sigma_m + \sigma_c$. Cortical tension dominates in most animal cells (order $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ N/m), while membrane tension is typically one to two orders of magnitude smaller.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] Define a Newtonian fluid. Give two biomedical examples, and explain why whole blood is *not* Newtonian.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Newtonian fluid** strictly obeys Newton's law of viscosity $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$, where the dynamic viscosity $\mu$ is a constant independent of the shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$.

**Two biomedical Newtonian examples:**
1. **Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF):** A watery ultrafiltrate with viscosity close to water (~0.7–1 mPa·s at 37 °C).
2. **Blood plasma:** Approximately Newtonian because it lacks the cellular components (RBCs) responsible for non-ideal behavior.

**Why whole blood is non-Newtonian:** Whole blood contains ~45% by volume of red blood cells, which (a) **aggregate** into rouleaux at low shear rates, increasing apparent viscosity; and (b) **deform and align** with the flow streamlines at high shear rates, decreasing apparent viscosity. This shear-thinning (pseudoplastic) behavior means viscosity is not constant, so the constitutive equation $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ with a constant $\mu$ fails. The Casson or Carreau-Yasuda models are used instead.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the Hagen–Poiseuille equation for steady, laminar, fully-developed flow of a Newtonian fluid in a horizontal circular pipe of diameter $D$ and length $L$, subject to a pressure drop $\Delta P$. State all assumptions clearly. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 70 kg patient has a femoral artery of diameter $D = 6$ mm carrying blood with average velocity $v = 0.3$ m/s. The blood density is $\rho = 1060$ kg/m³ and the dynamic viscosity (at the relevant shear rate) is $\mu = 0.0035$ Pa·s. Determine (i) the Reynolds number, (ii) the flow regime, and (iii) the wall shear stress, stating any assumptions used. (7 marks)

##### **Model Solution for Q-A:**

**(a) Derivation of Hagen–Poiseuille equation**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state flow: $\partial / \partial t = 0$
2. Incompressible Newtonian fluid: constant $\rho$, $\mu$
3. Laminar, fully developed flow: $v_z(r)$ only, $\partial v_z / \partial z = 0$
4. Axisymmetric flow: $\partial / \partial \theta = 0$
5. Horizontal pipe: no gravity component along the axis
6. No-slip at the wall: $v_z(R) = 0$

**Governing equation** (z-component of Navier–Stokes in cylindrical coordinates, simplified):

$$0 = -\frac{dP}{dz} + \mu \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right)$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $dP/dz = -\Delta P / L$:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{\mu}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right)$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by $r / \mu$ and integrate:

$$r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} r^2 + C_1$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $r$ and integrate again:

$$v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} r^2 + C_1 \ln r + C_2$$

**Step 4:** Apply BCs — finiteness at $r=0 \Rightarrow C_1 = 0$; no-slip at $r=R \Rightarrow C_2 = -\dfrac{\Delta P \, R^2}{4 \mu L}$.

$$v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \left( R^2 - r^2 \right) \quad \text{(parabolic profile)}$$

**Step 5:** Maximum velocity at center $r = 0$:

$$v_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{4 \mu L}$$

**Step 6:** Average velocity by integrating over the cross-section:

$$v_{avg} = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_0^R v_z(r) \, 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{8 \mu L} = \frac{v_{max}}{2}$$

**Step 7:** Volumetric flow rate:

$$Q = v_{avg} \cdot \pi R^2 = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L} = \frac{\pi D^4 \Delta P}{128 \mu L}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi D^4 \Delta P}{128 \mu L}}$$

This is the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation**.

---

**(b) Numerical solution**

**(i) Reynolds number:**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1060 \times 0.3 \times 0.006}{0.0035} = \frac{1.908}{0.0035} \approx 545$$

**(ii) Flow regime:** $\text{Re} = 545 \ll 2300$ ⟹ **Laminar flow**.

**(iii) Wall shear stress (Poiseuille):**

$$\Delta P / L = 32 \mu v / D^2 = \frac{32 \times 0.0035 \times 0.3}{(0.006)^2} = \frac{0.0336}{0.000036} = 933.3 \text{ Pa/m}$$

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \, D}{4 L} = \frac{933.3 \times 0.006}{4} = 1.4 \text{ Pa}$$

The wall shear stress of ~1.4 Pa is at the lower end of the physiological arterial range, which is consistent with resting-state femoral flow.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** Students frequently forget to state the assumptions when applying the Hagen–Poiseuille equation (steady, laminar, Newtonian, fully developed). Examiners **deduct 1 mark** if these are missing. Also, do not confuse **average** velocity with **maximum** velocity — for a parabolic profile, $v_{max} = 2 v_{avg}$, and using the wrong one will double or halve $\tau_w$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** With neat sketches and constitutive equations, classify fluids into Newtonian and non-Newtonian categories. Discuss the power-law (Ostwald–de Waele) model and its three regimes in detail. (7 marks)

**(b)** The apparent viscosity of a patient's blood at 37 °C is described by the Carreau model:

$$\eta(\dot{\gamma}) = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty) \left[ 1 + (\lambda \dot{\gamma})^2 \right]^{(n-1)/2}$$

with parameters $\eta_0 = 0.16$ Pa·s, $\eta_\infty = 0.0035$ Pa·s, $\lambda = 8$ s, and $n = 0.25$. Compute the apparent viscosity (i) at $\dot{\gamma} = 0.1$ s⁻¹, (ii) at $\dot{\gamma} = 100$ s⁻¹, and (iii) comment on the rheological behavior at physiological shear rates ($\dot{\gamma} \sim 100$ to $1000$ s⁻¹) in arteries. (7 marks)

##### **Model Solution for Q-B:**

**(a) Classification of fluids**

A fluid's constitutive equation relating shear stress $\tau$ to shear rate $\dot{\gamma}$ is the basis for classification. Refer to the diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes.

**Newtonian fluid** — straight line through origin in $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ plot:
$$\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$$
Examples: water, air, plasma.

**Non-Newtonian fluids** (non-linear $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$):

1. **Shear-thinning (Pseudoplastic)** — $\tau$ vs. $\dot{\gamma}$ curve concave downward; viscosity decreases with shear.
2. **Shear-thickening (Dilatant)** — concave upward; viscosity increases with shear.
3. **Bingham plastic** — behaves as a solid until yield stress $\tau_y$ is exceeded, then flows linearly.
4. **Viscoelastic** — exhibits both viscous and elastic response (e.g., polymer solutions, mucus).

**Power-law (Ostwald–de Waele) model:**

$$\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$$

| Case | $n$ | Behavior | $\eta_{app} = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Newtonian | $n = 1$ | Linear | $\eta = K$ |
| Shear-thinning | $0 < n < 1$ | Pseudoplastic | $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ (decreases with $\dot{\gamma}$) |
| Shear-thickening | $n > 1$ | Dilatant | $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ (increases with $\dot{\gamma}$) |

Limitation: The model cannot capture the limiting viscosities at very low or very high shear rates.

---

**(b) Carreau model calculation**

The Carreau model is:

$$\eta(\dot{\gamma}) = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty) \left[ 1 + (\lambda \dot{\gamma})^2 \right]^{(n-1)/2}$$

**Given:** $\eta_0 = 0.16$ Pa·s, $\eta_\infty = 0.0035$ Pa·s, $\lambda = 8$ s, $n = 0.25$.

**(i) At $\dot{\gamma} = 0.1$ s⁻¹:**

$\lambda \dot{\gamma} = 8 \times 0.1 = 0.8$

$(\lambda \dot{\gamma})^2 = 0.64$

$1 + 0.64 = 1.64$

Exponent: $(n-1)/2 = (0.25 - 1)/2 = -0.375$

$1.64^{-0.375} = \exp(-0.375 \times \ln 1.64) = \exp(-0.375 \times 0.4947) = \exp(-0.1855) = 0.8307$

$\eta_0 - \eta_\infty = 0.16 - 0.0035 = 0.1565$ Pa·s

$\eta(0.1) = 0.0035 + 0.1565 \times 0.8307 = 0.0035 + 0.1300 = 0.1335$ Pa·s

$$\boxed{\eta(0.1) \approx 0.134 \text{ Pa·s}}$$

**(ii) At $\dot{\gamma} = 100$ s⁻¹:**

$\lambda \dot{\gamma} = 8 \times 100 = 800$

$(\lambda \dot{\gamma})^2 = 640{,}000$

$1 + 640{,}000 = 640{,}001$

$640{,}001^{-0.375} = \exp(-0.375 \times \ln 640{,}001) = \exp(-0.375 \times 13.369) = \exp(-5.013) = 0.00664$

$\eta(100) = 0.0035 + 0.1565 \times 0.00664 = 0.0035 + 0.001039 = 0.004539$ Pa·s

$$\boxed{\eta(100) \approx 0.00454 \text{ Pa·s}}$$

**(iii) Comment on physiological arterial shear rates:**

At $\dot{\gamma} \sim 100$ to $1000$ s⁻¹, the term $(\lambda \dot{\gamma})^2 \gg 1$, so $\eta \approx \eta_\infty \approx 0.0035$ Pa·s. The blood behaves as a **near-Newtonian fluid with viscosity close to plasma viscosity**, because RBCs are fully deformed and aligned. The strong shear-thinning transition occurs in the range $\dot{\gamma} \sim 1/\lambda = 0.125$ s⁻¹, which is more relevant to **venous and low-shear microcirculatory** flow. In the **arterial circulation**, the simple Newtonian assumption ($\mu \approx 0.0035$ Pa·s) is a reasonable engineering approximation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** Many students mistakenly apply the formula without computing the exponent $(n-1)/2$ correctly. The expression $1 + (\lambda \dot{\gamma})^2$ inside the brackets is raised to a **fractional power** (not multiplied!). Also, do not round intermediate values; carry at least 4 significant figures until the final step. Marks are deducted for premature rounding.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **$\sigma_{app} = \sigma_m + \sigma_c$** — membrane tension is passive, cortical tension is active (myosin-driven).
- Membrane tension typical: **$10^{-6}$ to $10^{-4}$ N/m**; cortical tension: **$10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ N/m**.
- **Newtonian** = constant $\mu$, linear $\tau$–$\dot{\gamma}$ (water, plasma, CSF).
- **Non-Newtonian** = $\eta$ depends on $\dot{\gamma}$: pseudoplastic (blood, ketchup), dilatant (cornstarch), viscoelastic (mucus), Bingham (toothpaste).
- **Power-law index $n$**: $n=1$ Newtonian, $n<1$ shear-thinning, $n>1$ shear-thickening.
- **Casson model** $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\mu_p \dot{\gamma}}$ — best for low-shear blood (accounts for RBC aggregation yield stress).
- **Carreau model** captures both low-shear ($\eta_0$) and high-shear ($\eta_\infty$) limits.
- **Reynolds number:** $\text{Re} = \rho v D / \mu$. Threshold: **<2300 laminar, >4000 turbulent, 2300–4000 transitional**.
- **Hagen–Poiseuille:** $Q = \pi D^4 \Delta P / 128 \mu L$ — valid **only** for steady, laminar, Newtonian, fully-developed flow.
- **Wall shear stress (laminar pipe):** $\tau_w = \Delta P D / 4 L$.
- **Wall shear rate (laminar pipe):** $\dot{\gamma}_w = 8 v_{avg} / D$.
- **$v_{max} = 2 v_{avg}$** for parabolic profile.
- **Physiological WSS in arteries** is 1–7 Pa; deviations are diagnostic of atherosclerosis risk.
- In **microcirculation**, $\text{Re} \ll 1$ — Stokes (creeping) flow regime dominates.
- **Always state assumptions** before applying Hagen–Poiseuille — examiners deduct marks for missing them.
- Use **$\eta$ or $\mu$** appropriately: $\mu$ for Newtonian (constant), $\eta(\dot{\gamma})$ for non-Newtonian.
- Convert all units to **SI (m, s, kg, Pa)** before calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cardiovascular Transport System: Review of Physiological Process - Rheology of Flow of Blood: Large Vessels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT832 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Cardiovascular Transport System: Review of Physiological Process - Rheology of Flow of Blood: Large Vessels # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Cardiovascular Transport System (CTS) The **Cardiovascular Transport System** is an integrated, closed-loop hydraulic network comprising the heart (pump), blood (working fluid), and a hierarchical branching network of vessels (conduits) that simultaneously delivers oxygen, nutrients, hormones, and immune cells to tissues while removing metabolic waste products (CO₂, urea, lactate). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology**: In biomedical transport phenomena, the cardiovascular system is modeled as a **momentum-mass-energy coupled transport problem** governed by the Navier–Stokes equations for a non-Newtonian, pulsatile, particulate suspension in distensible elastic conduits. --- ## 1.2 Components of the Cardiovascular Network The system is anatomically and functionally divided into two circulations arranged in series: | Circuit | Function | Driving Pressure | Typical Vessel Diameter | |---|---|---|---| | **Systemic Circulation** | Supplies O₂ to body tissues | ~120 mmHg (systolic) | Aorta ~25 mm → Capillaries ~8 µm | | **Pulmonary Circulation** | Oxygenates blood in lungs | ~25 mmHg (systolic) | Pulmonary artery ~25 mm | --- ## 1.3 Rheology of Blood Flow in Large Vessels **Rheology** is the branch of continuum mechanics that studies the deformation and flow behavior of matter, particularly the relationship between **shear stress (τ)** and **shear rate (γ̇)**. **Blood** is a complex, opaque, two-phase suspension consisting of: - **Plasma** (~55% by volume): a Newtonian aqueous solution containing water (≈91%), proteins (albumin, globulin, fibrinogen), electrolytes, and glucose. - **Formed Elements** (~45% by volume): - **Red Blood Cells (Erythrocytes / RBCs)** — the dominant fraction; **Hematocrit (Hct)** quantifies their volume fraction (≈45% in healthy adult males). - **White Blood Cells (Leukocytes)** — ~1% by volume. - **Platelets (Thrombocytes)** — fragments involved in clotting. > [!NOTE] > **Large Vessels Definition (KTU context)**: Arteries, arterioles, venules, and veins with internal diameter **D > 1 mm** (i.e., large enough that RBCs are negligible compared to lumen size). The vessel diameter is much greater than the cellular dimensions, so the continuum assumption holds and the particulate nature can be represented via an **apparent (effective) viscosity**. --- ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy > [!TIP] > **Intuition — The City Water Grid Analogy** > Think of the cardiovascular system as a municipal water distribution network: > - The **heart** is the central pumping station (with a four-chamber design for redundancy and pressure staging). > - **Arteries** are the large-diameter high-pressure trunk mains leaving the station. > - **Arterioles** are the district valves that throttle pressure. > - **Capillaries** are the household taps where exchange occurs (single-cell-thick walls). > - **Veins** are the low-pressure return drains carrying wastewater back. > - **Blood** is the water-plus-sediment mixture — at high flow rates (shear rates) the sediment (RBCs) aligns with the streamlines, reducing friction; at low flow rates the sediment settles and thickens the fluid. --- ## 1.5 Key Physiological Variables to Remember | Quantity | Symbol | Approximate Value | |---|---|---| | **Mean Aortic Pressure** | P̄ₐ | **100 mmHg (13.3 kPa)** | | **Mean Right Atrial Pressure** | P̄ᵣₐ | **2 mmHg** | | **Cardiac Output** | CO | **5 L/min** at rest | | **Heart Rate** | f | **70 beats/min (1.17 Hz)** | | **Blood Density** | ρ | **1060 kg/m³** | | **Plasma Viscosity** | μₚ | **1.2 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** | | **Total Blood Volume** | V | **~5 L** | | **Hematocrit** | Hct | **0.42 – 0.45** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physiological Process — Stepwise Logic The CTS operates via a recurring six-step cycle: 1. **Diastolic Filling** → Atria relax, AV valves open, blood passively fills ventricles. 2. **Atrial Systole** → Atria contract, push remaining blood into ventricles (topping up). 3. **Isovolumetric Contraction** → Ventricles begin to contract; all valves closed; pressure rises sharply. 4. **Ventricular Ejection** → When ventricular pressure > aortic pressure, semilunar valves open; blood ejected into aorta/pulmonary artery. 5. **Isovolumetric Relaxation** → Ventricles relax; all valves closed; pressure falls. 6. **Passive Filling** → When ventricular pressure < atrial pressure, AV valves reopen; cycle repeats. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why pulsatile?** The heart's cyclic contraction converts chemical energy (ATP) into pulsatile mechanical energy. This pulsatility is critical because it prevents sedimentation of RBCs, maintains vessel patency, and enhances mass transport across capillary walls. --- ## 2.2 Why is Blood Non-Newtonian in Large Vessels? Even in large vessels (D > 1 mm), blood exhibits **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** behavior due to: - **RBC Aggregation** (rouleaux formation) at low shear rates — chains of RBCs dramatically increase viscosity. - **RBC Deformation & Orientation** at high shear rates — cells elongate and align with streamlines, reducing viscous dissipation. - **Membrane Tank-Treading** of the RBC lipid bilayer. - **Plasma Protein Effects** — fibrinogen concentration strongly influences aggregation. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE**: All absolute value bars inside the tables below use the LaTeX `\vert` command to prevent markdown table syntax breakage. Students should also use `\vert` when typing these in their answer sheets. | # | Equation | Name | Applicability | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = A\,\bar{v} = \pi R^{2}\,\bar{v}$ | Continuity Equation (steady) | Incompressible flow | m³/s | | 2 | $P + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^{2} + \rho g z = \text{const}$ | Bernoulli Equation | Inviscid, steady, incompressible | Pa | | 3 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4}\,\Delta P}{8\,\mu\,L}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille Law | Steady, laminar, Newtonian in rigid tube | m³/s | | 4 | $\tau_{w} = \dfrac{R\,\Delta P}{2L}$ | Wall Shear Stress | Fully developed laminar flow | Pa | | 5 | $\mathrm{Re} = \dfrac{\rho\,\bar{v}\,D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds Number | Flow regime classification | dimensionless | | 6 | $\alpha = R\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\,\rho}{\mu}}$ | Womersley Number | Unsteady pulsatile flow (viscous vs inertial) | dimensionless | | 7 | $\tau = K\,(\dot{\gamma})^{n}$ | Ostwald–de Waele (Power Law) | Shear-thinning (n<1) or thickening (n>1) | Pa | | 8 | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{y}} + \sqrt{\mu_{\infty}\,\dot{\gamma}}$ | Casson Equation | Blood rheology (yields a fit near Hct range) | Pa^½ | | 9 | $\mu_{app} = \dfrac{\mu_{\infty} + (\mu_{0}-\mu_{\infty})\left[1+(\lambda\dot{\gamma})^{2}\right]^{(n-1)/2}}{}$ | Carreau Model | Captures zero-shear & infinite-shear limits | Pa·s | | 10 | $\mu = \mu_{p}\,(1-H)^{-2.5}$ | Einstein-type Hematocrit Correction | Large vessels, moderate Hct, high shear | Pa·s | | 11 | $\Delta P = R_{\text{hydro}}\,Q$ | Hydraulic Analogy of Ohm's Law | Whole-circuit systemic resistance | Pa | | 12 | $\dot{m} = \rho\,A\,v$ | Mass Flow Rate | Mass conservation in vessels | kg/s | ### Boundary & Operating Conditions Used by Examiners - **No-slip condition** at vessel walls: $v(R) = 0$ → shorthand `\vert v(R) = 0 \vert`. - **Axisymmetry** at the vessel centerline: $\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial r}\vert_{r=0} = 0$. - **Fully developed flow** when $\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 0$ (long vessels, downstream of entry length $L_{e} \approx 0.06\,\mathrm{Re}\,D$). - **Pulsatile inlet pressure**: $P(t) = \bar{P} + \Delta P \cos(\omega t)$ where $\omega = 2\pi f$. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Design of Artificial Hearts & VADs (Ventricular Assist Devices)**: Require accurate non-Newtonian modeling of blood to predict hemolysis and thrombosis. - **Hemodialysis Machines**: Use Hagen–Poiseuille scaling to design dialyzer hollow-fiber bundles. - **Stent & Graft Design**: Wall shear stress distributions predict sites of intimal hyperplasia (restenosis). - **Pharmaceutical IV Drip & Syringe Pumps**: Power-law parameters dictate injection force and bolus delivery time. - **Bio-MEMS and Microfluidic Diagnostics**: Down-scale continuity & viscosity equations for lab-on-chip blood rheometry. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Hagen–Poiseuille Law (Newtonian Baseline) We start from the steady, fully-developed, axisymmetric Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, x)$ with the only non-zero velocity component being $v_{x}(r)$: $$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\,\frac{dv_{x}}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$ **Step 1 (Integration once):** Multiply both sides by $r$ and integrate with respect to $r$: $$r\,\frac{dv_{x}}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\,r^{2} + C_{1}$$ **Step 2 (Boundary condition — finite velocity at centerline $r=0$):** To avoid a logarithmic singularity, $C_{1} = 0$. $$\frac{dv_{x}}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\,r$$ **Step 3 (Second integration):** $$v_{x}(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\,r^{2} + C_{2}$$ **Step 4 (Apply no-slip at wall $r = R$):** $v_{x}(R) = 0 \Rightarrow C_{2} = -\dfrac{1}{4\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}R^{2}$. **Step 5 (Parabolic velocity profile):** $$\boxed{\,v_{x}(r) = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)\,}$$ **Step 6 (Volumetric flow rate by integrating over the cross-section):** $$Q = \int_{0}^{R} v_{x}(r)\,2\pi r\,dr = -\frac{\pi}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\int_{0}^{R}(R^{2}-r^{2})r\,dr$$ $$Q = -\frac{\pi}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\left[\frac{R^{2}r^{2}}{2}-\frac{r^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{R} = -\frac{\pi}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\cdot\frac{R^{4}}{4}$$ **Step 7 (Pressure gradient expressed as $\frac{dP}{dx} = \frac{\Delta P}{L}$ for a vessel of length $L$):** $$\boxed{\,Q = \frac{\pi R^{4}\,\Delta P}{8\,\mu\,L}\,}\quad \text{(Hagen–Poiseuille)}$$ **Step 8 (Wall shear stress):** Differentiate $v_{x}$ and evaluate at $r = R$: $$\tau_{w} = -\mu\,\frac{dv_{x}}{dr}\bigg|_{r=R} = -\mu\cdot\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}R = -\frac{R}{2}\frac{\Delta P}{L}$$ $$\boxed{\,\tau_{w} = \frac{R\,\Delta P}{2L}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in large vessels**: Although real blood is non-Newtonian, the Hagen–Poiseuille form provides the **theoretical scaffold** for defining an *apparent viscosity*: $\mu_{\text{app}} = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 Q L}$. By measuring $Q$, $\Delta P$, $R$, and $L$ experimentally, the effective viscosity at the prevailing wall shear rate can be back-calculated. --- ## 3.2 Derivation II — Power-Law Rheological Model The **Ostwald–de Waele (Power Law)** model captures shear-thinning via: $$\tau = K\,\dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$$ where $K$ is the **consistency index** [Pa·sⁿ] and $n$ is the **flow behavior index** [dimensionless]. **Step 1 (Substitute into momentum equation):** For a fully-developed tube flow, the shear stress varies linearly with $r$: $$\tau(r) = \frac{r}{R}\,\tau_{w}$$ **Step 2 (Apparent viscosity definition):** $$\mu_{\text{app}} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = K\,\dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$ **Step 3 (Shear rate for tube flow):** $\dot{\gamma}(r) = -\dfrac{dv_{x}}{dr}$ and using $\tau(r)$: $$\dot{\gamma}(r) = \left(\frac{\tau(r)}{K}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\frac{r\,\tau_{w}}{R\,K}\right)^{1/n}$$ **Step 4 (Integrate to get velocity profile):** $$v_{x}(r) = \int_{r}^{R}\dot{\gamma}(r')\,dr' = \left(\frac{\tau_{w}}{K\,R}\right)^{1/n}\int_{r}^{R}(r')^{1/n}\,dr'$$ $$v_{x}(r) = \left(\frac{\tau_{w}}{K\,R}\right)^{1/n}\cdot\frac{n}{n+1}\left(R^{(n+1)/n}-r^{(n+1)/n}\right)$$ **Step 5 (Mean velocity):** $$\bar{v} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^{2}} = \frac{n}{3n+1}\left(\frac{\tau_{w}}{K}\right)^{1/n}\cdot\frac{R}{1}$$ **Step 6 (Generalized Hagen–Poiseuille for Power-Law fluid):** $$\boxed{\,Q = \frac{\pi R^{3}}{3n+1}\left(\frac{R\,\Delta P}{2 K L}\right)^{1/n}\,}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation III — Casson Equation for Blood The **Casson model** captures both the yield stress $\tau_{y}$ (from RBC aggregation) and the high-shear Newtonian asymptote: $$\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{y}} + \sqrt{\mu_{\infty}\,\dot{\gamma}}$$ **Step 1 (Solve for shear stress):** Squaring both sides: $$\tau = \tau_{y} + 2\sqrt{\tau_{y}\,\mu_{\infty}}\,\sqrt{\dot{\gamma}} + \mu_{\infty}\dot{\gamma}$$ **Step 2 (Apparent viscosity):** $$\mu_{\text{app}} = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}} = \mu_{\infty} + \frac{\tau_{y}}{\dot{\gamma}} + 2\sqrt{\frac{\tau_{y}\,\mu_{\infty}}{\dot{\gamma}}}$$ **Step 3 (For human blood at Hct = 0.45):** Typical KTU-board accepted values: $$\tau_{y} \approx 0.05\ \text{Pa},\qquad \mu_{\infty} \approx 3.5\times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$$ **Step 4 (Tube-flow integral to get the volumetric flow rate):** $$Q = \frac{\pi R^{3}\,\tau_{w}}{4\,\tau_{w}^{1/2}}\left[-\frac{4}{7}\left(\frac{\tau_{y}}{\tau_{w}}\right)^{7/4}+\frac{4}{3}\left(\frac{\tau_{y}}{\tau_{w}}\right)^{3/4}-\left(\frac{\tau_{y}}{\tau_{w}}\right)^{1/4}+\frac{1}{2}\right]$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering interpretation**: The yield stress $\tau_{y}$ creates a **plug-flow core** in the center of large vessels (radius $r_{c} = R\,\tau_{y}/\tau_{w}$). The plasma-rich marginal layer near the wall exhibits near-Newtonian behavior. This is why blood **visually streams** differently than a true Newtonian fluid in large transparent flow rigs. --- ## 3.4 Derivation IV — Hematocrit–Viscosity Relationship The classical **Einstein-type** correction for volume fraction of rigid spheres is: $$\mu_{\text{susp}} = \mu_{p}\,(1+2.5\phi)\quad\text{(dilute limit)}$$ For whole blood, the empirical **Quemada**-type and **Chien**-type correlations are used: $$\mu = \mu_{p}\,(1 - H)^{-2.5}\quad\text{(high-shear asymptote, large vessels)}$$ **Step 1 (Substitute Hct = 0.45):** $$\mu = (1.2\times 10^{-3})\,(1-0.45)^{-2.5} = (1.2\times 10^{-3})\,(0.55)^{-2.5}$$ $$\mu = (1.2\times 10^{-3})\cdot 6.05 \approx 7.26\times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$$ **Step 2 (Comparison with plasma):** Whole-blood high-shear viscosity is roughly **6×** greater than plasma alone — a dramatic consequence of the RBC volume fraction. > [!WARNING] > **Common pitfall**: Many students apply $\mu \propto (1-H)^{-2.5}$ uniformly. This is valid **only for large vessels at high shear**. In microvessels (D < 300 µm) the Fåhræus–Lindqvist effect dramatically *reduces* apparent viscosity, violating this scaling. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Where to Use Each Model | Vessel Size | Shear Rate Range | Recommended Rheological Model | Reason | |---|---|---|---| | **Aorta (D ≈ 25 mm)** | 100 – 1000 s⁻¹ | Power-law or Carreau | High pulsatility, RBC alignment near walls | | **Large Artery (D ≈ 5 mm)** | 200 – 800 s⁻¹ | Casson | Captures yield stress due to aggregation | | **Medium Artery (D ≈ 1 mm)** | 250 – 600 s⁻¹ | Newtonian with $\mu_{app}$ | Plateau region of viscosity curve | | **Arteriole (D ≈ 100 µm)** | 500 – 1500 s⁻¹ | Newtonian μ_app (decreasing) | Fåhræus–Lindqvist regime begins | | **Capillary (D ≈ 8 µm)** | > 1000 s⁻¹ | σ-effect / single-file models | RBC deformation dominates; continuum fails | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Cardiovascular Transport Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Heart["HEART - DOUBLE PUMP"] RA1[Right Atrium] RV1[Right Ventricle] LA1[Left Atrium] LV1[Left Ventricle] end subgraph Pulm["PULMONARY CIRCUIT"] PA1[Pulmonary Artery] PCAP[Pulm Capillaries] PV1[Pulmonary Veins] end subgraph Syst["SYSTEMIC CIRCUIT - LARGE VESSELS"] AOR[Aorta D 25 mm] LAR[Large Arteries D 5 mm] MAR[Medium Arteries D 1 mm] ARTL[Arterioles D 100 um] CAP[Capillaries D 8 um] VEN[Venules] VEN2[Large Veins D 10 mm] VC1[Vena Cava] end RA1 --> RV1 RV1 --> PA1 PA1 --> PCAP PCAP --> PV1 PV1 --> LA1 LA1 --> LV1 LV1 --> AOR AOR --> LAR LAR --> MAR MAR --> ARTL ARTL --> CAP CAP --> VEN VEN --> VEN2 VEN2 --> VC1 VC1 --> RA1 classDef pump fill:#ffd54f,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000; classDef vessel fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:1px,color:#000; classDef capillary fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:1px,color:#000; class RA1,RV1,LA1,LV1 pump; class AOR,LAR,MAR,ARTL,VEN,VEN2,VC1 vessel; class PCAP,CAP capillary; ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Rheological Modeling Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Physiological Data:
Pressure Waveform P t
Vessel Diameter D
Hematocrit Hct] --> B[Geometric Preprocessor:
Compute Wall Shear Rate
Estimate Re and Alpha] B --> C{Decision Tree:
Select Rheology Model} C -->|Large Vessel D greater than 1 mm| D[Power Law or Casson] C -->|Medium Vessel| E[Newtonian with mu app] C -->|Microvessel| F[Fahraeus Lindqvist Model] D --> G[Solve Navier Stokes
Cylindrical Coordinates] E --> G F --> G G --> H[Compute Velocity Profile v x r t] H --> I[Compute Flow Rate Q t] H --> J[Compute Wall Shear Stress tau w] I --> K[Output to Clinical
or Engineering Application] J --> K classDef input fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000; classDef process fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000; classDef output fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000; class A,B input; class C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J process; class K output; ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Apparent Viscosity vs Shear Rate ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LOW["LOW SHEAR REGIME"] L1[Hct 0.45, gamma less than 1 per s] --> L2[Rouleaux Aggregation] L2 --> L3[mu app greater than 0.1 Pa s] end subgraph MID["INTERMEDIATE SHEAR"] M1[Hct 0.45, gamma 1 to 100 per s] --> M2[RBC Deformation Begins] M2 --> M3[mu app approx 0.005 to 0.01 Pa s] end subgraph HIGH["HIGH SHEAR PLATEAU"] H1[Hct 0.45, gamma greater than 100 per s] --> H2[RBC Fully Aligned] H2 --> H3[mu app approx 0.003 to 0.004 Pa s] end LOW --> MID --> HIGH classDef region fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000; classDef value fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000; class L1,L2,M1,M2,H1,H2 region; class L3,M3,H3 value; ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading the diagrams**: These Mermaid block diagrams replace the physical anatomical sketches a textbook would show. For KTU 2024 board exams, drawing a simple **unidirectional block flowchart** of the systemic + pulmonary loops with labeled vessel diameters is worth 2–3 marks on a 14-mark question. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: Define rheology. Why is blood classified as a non-Newtonian fluid even in large vessels?** **Model Answer (≈110 words):** > **Rheology** is the branch of continuum mechanics that studies the deformation and flow of matter, particularly the relationship between shear stress (τ) and shear rate (γ̇), and the time-dependent strain response. > > Blood is classified as a **non-Newtonian fluid** even in large vessels because it is a concentrated suspension of deformable red blood cells (≈45% by volume) in plasma. Its apparent viscosity varies with shear rate due to: > - **Rouleaux (RBC aggregation)** at low shear rates (τ > γ̇ → μ_app large). > - **RBC deformation and alignment** at high shear rates (μ_app decreases). > > Hence the viscosity–shear-rate relationship is **not linear**, violating Newton's law of viscosity τ = μ γ̇. --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: List any three rheological models used to describe blood flow in large vessels. Write the governing equation for each.** **Model Answer (≈100 words):** | # | Model | Equation | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Power Law (Ostwald–de Waele)** | $\tau = K\,\dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$ | | 2 | **Casson Model** | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_{y}} + \sqrt{\mu_{\infty}\,\dot{\gamma}}$ | | 3 | **Carreau Model** | $\mu_{\text{app}} = \mu_{\infty} + (\mu_{0} - \mu_{\infty})\left[1 + (\lambda\dot{\gamma})^{2}\right]^{(n-1)/2}$ | | 4 | **Newtonian with apparent viscosity** | $\tau = \mu_{\text{app}}(\dot{\gamma})\,\dot{\gamma}$ | The Power Law captures shear-thinning; the Casson model captures the yield stress due to RBC aggregation; the Carreau model captures both low- and high-shear viscosity limits. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 ESE Convention**: Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Two **mutually exclusive** alternative choices are provided below; the student answers **either OR**. --- ### Question B — Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]` #### (a) Derive the Hagen–Poiseuille equation for steady, laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid in a rigid cylindrical tube. Discuss its applicability and limitations for blood flow in large vessels. **(7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** 1. **Starting point** (1 mark): Cylindrical Navier–Stokes for steady, fully developed, axisymmetric flow reduces to: $$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\,\frac{dv_{x}}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$ 2. **First integration** (1 mark): With finite velocity at $r = 0$ ⇒ $C_1 = 0$: $$r\,\frac{dv_{x}}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\,r^{2}$$ 3. **Second integration** (1 mark): With no-slip at $r = R$ ⇒ $C_2 = -\dfrac{R^{2}}{4\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dx}$: $$v_{x}(r) = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)$$ 4. **Volumetric flow rate** (2 marks): $$Q = \int_0^R v_x(r)\,2\pi r\,dr = \frac{\pi R^{4}}{8\mu}\!\left(-\frac{dP}{dx}\right) = \frac{\pi R^{4}\Delta P}{8\mu L}$$ 5. **Wall shear stress** (1 mark): $\tau_w = \dfrac{R\,\Delta P}{2L}$ 6. **Applicability and limitations for large-vessel blood flow** (1 mark): - **Applicable as a baseline**: Gives correct order-of-magnitude estimate for $Q$ vs $\Delta P$ when $\mu$ is replaced by apparent viscosity $\mu_{app}(\dot{\gamma})$. - **Limitations**: (i) Assumes Newtonian behavior — but blood is shear-thinning; (ii) Assumes rigid tube — but arteries are elastic and pulsatile; (iii) Ignores the particulate nature of blood (RBCs ≈45% by volume); (iv) Assumes steady flow — but cardiac ejection is pulsatile, requiring Womersley analysis; (v) Neglects the Fahraeus–Lindqvist effect in vessels below 1 mm. --- #### (b) For blood flow in the aorta (diameter 25 mm, mean velocity 0.4 m/s) with high-shear apparent viscosity $\mu_{app} = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s and density $\rho = 1060$ kg/m³, compute: (i) Reynolds number, (ii) Womersley number at heart rate 70 bpm, (iii) the wall shear stress, and (iv) the pressure drop per metre length. Comment on the flow regime. **(7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** 1. **Given data**: - $D = 25\ \text{mm} = 0.025\ \text{m}$, $R = 0.0125\ \text{m}$ - $\bar{v} = 0.4\ \text{m/s}$, $\mu_{app} = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$, $\rho = 1060\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$ - $f = 70\ \text{bpm} = 1.167\ \text{Hz}$, $\omega = 2\pi f = 7.33\ \text{rad/s}$ 2. **(i) Reynolds number** (2 marks): $$\mathrm{Re} = \frac{\rho\,\bar{v}\,D}{\mu_{app}} = \frac{1060 \times 0.4 \times 0.025}{3.5\times 10^{-3}}$$ $$\mathrm{Re} = \frac{10.6}{3.5\times 10^{-3}} \approx 3029$$ 3. **(ii) Womersley number** (2 marks): $$\alpha = R\sqrt{\frac{\omega\,\rho}{\mu_{app}}} = 0.0125\sqrt{\frac{7.33 \times 1060}{3.5\times 10^{-3}}}$$ $$\alpha = 0.0125\sqrt{2.22\times 10^{6}} = 0.0125 \times 1490 \approx 18.6$$ 4. **(iii) Wall shear stress** (1.5 marks): Using Poiseuille parabolic profile and $\dot{\gamma}_w = 8\bar{v}/D$: $$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{8 \times 0.4}{0.025} = 128\ \text{s}^{-1}$$ $$\tau_w = \mu_{app}\,\dot{\gamma}_w = 3.5\times 10^{-3} \times 128 \approx 0.448\ \text{Pa}$$ 5. **(iv) Pressure drop per metre** (1 mark): From $\tau_w = R\Delta P/(2L)$: $$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{2\,\tau_w}{R} = \frac{2 \times 0.448}{0.0125} \approx 71.7\ \text{Pa/m}$$ 6. **Comment on flow regime** (0.5 marks): $\mathrm{Re} \approx 3029$ is **well below the transition threshold of ~2300–2900** for steady pipe flow, but with pulsatility and $\alpha \approx 18.6 \gg 1$, the flow is **inertia-dominated and pulsatile**, with a **flat velocity profile** rather than fully developed Poiseuille parabola — characteristic of **large-artery flow**. --- ### Question B — Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` #### (a) Discuss the rheological models (Newtonian, Power-law, Casson, Carreau) used to characterize blood flow in large vessels. Compare their predictions of apparent viscosity at low, medium, and high shear rates. **(7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** 1. **Newtonian Model** (1.5 marks): $\tau = \mu\dot{\gamma}$ with constant $\mu$. Suitable only at very high shear rates ($\dot{\gamma} > 200\ \text{s}^{-1}$) when RBCs are fully aligned. Predicts constant $\mu_{app}$ — fails to capture shear-thinning. 2. **Power-Law Model** (1.5 marks): $\tau = K\dot{\gamma}^{n}$ with $n < 1$ (typically $n \approx 0.6$–$0.8$ for blood). Predicts a monotonic power-law decrease in $\mu_{app} = K\dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$. Strength: simple two-parameter fit. Weakness: diverges at zero and infinite shear. 3. **Casson Model** (1.5 marks): $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\mu_\infty\dot{\gamma}}$. Includes a yield stress $\tau_y$ (≈0.05 Pa at Hct 0.45), giving a plug-flow core. Accurately fits blood in the physiological shear-rate window $1$ – $100\ \text{s}^{-1}$. 4. **Carreau Model** (1.5 marks): $\mu_{app} = \mu_\infty + (\mu_0 - \mu_\infty)[1 + (\lambda\dot{\gamma})^{2}]^{(n-1)/2}$. Five-parameter model with smooth interpolation between zero-shear ($\mu_0$) and infinite-shear ($\mu_\infty$) plateaus, with time constant $\lambda$. Best physiological fit but computationally heavier. 5. **Comparison table at $\dot{\gamma}$ = 1, 50, 500 s⁻¹** (1 mark): | $\dot{\gamma}$ (s⁻¹) | Newtonian $\mu_{app}$ | Power-law $\mu_{app}$ | Casson $\mu_{app}$ | Carreau $\mu_{app}$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 (low) | 0.004 Pa·s (constant) | high (0.05 Pa·s) | 0.04 Pa·s | 0.045 Pa·s | | 50 (medium) | 0.004 Pa·s | 0.008 Pa·s | 0.006 Pa·s | 0.0065 Pa·s | | 500 (high) | 0.004 Pa·s | 0.002 Pa·s | 0.004 Pa·s | 0.004 Pa·s | --- #### (b) Blood with hematocrit $H = 0.45$ flows through a large artery of diameter $D = 4\ \text{mm}$ and length $L = 0.2\ \text{m}$ under a steady pressure gradient of $\Delta P / L = 500\ \text{Pa/m}$. Using the Casson model with $\tau_y = 0.05\ \text{Pa}$ and $\mu_\infty = 3.5\times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$, estimate (i) the wall shear stress, (ii) the radius of the plug-flow core, and (iii) the volumetric flow rate. **(7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** 1. **Wall shear stress** (2 marks): $$\tau_w = \frac{R\,\Delta P}{2L} = \frac{0.002 \times 500}{2} = 0.5\ \text{Pa}$$ 2. **Plug-flow core radius** (2 marks): The radius at which $\tau(r) = \tau_y$: $$r_c = R\,\frac{\tau_y}{\tau_w} = 0.002 \times \frac{0.05}{0.5} = 2\times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} = 0.2\ \text{mm}$$ 3. **Volumetric flow rate using Casson integrated formula** (3 marks): $$Q = \frac{\pi R^{3}\,\tau_w}{4\,\tau_w^{1/2}}\left[-\tfrac{4}{7}\left(\tfrac{\tau_y}{\tau_w}\right)^{7/4}+\tfrac{4}{3}\left(\tfrac{\tau_y}{\tau_w}\right)^{3/4}-\left(\tfrac{\tau_y}{\tau_w}\right)^{1/4}+\tfrac{1}{2}\right]$$ Substitute $\tau_y/\tau_w = 0.05/0.5 = 0.1$ and $R = 0.002$ m: $$(\tau_y/\tau_w)^{1/4} = 0.1^{0.25} = 0.5623$$ $$(\tau_y/\tau_w)^{3/4} = 0.1^{0.75} = 0.1778$$ $$(\tau_y/\tau_w)^{7/4} = 0.1^{1.75} = 0.01778$$ Bracket term: $$\left[-\tfrac{4}{7}(0.01778)+\tfrac{4}{3}(0.1778)-0.5623+0.5\right]$$ $$= [-0.01016 + 0.2371 - 0.5623 + 0.5]$$ $$= 0.1646$$ Prefactor: $$\frac{\pi R^{3}\tau_w}{4\sqrt{\tau_w}} = \frac{\pi \times (0.002)^{3} \times 0.5}{4 \times \sqrt{0.5}} = \frac{\pi \times 8\times 10^{-9} \times 0.5}{4 \times 0.7071}$$ $$= \frac{1.2566 \times 10^{-8}}{2.8284} = 4.443\times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$ Therefore: $$Q = 4.443\times 10^{-9} \times 0.1646 \approx 7.31\times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} = 0.731\ \text{mL/s}$$ 4. **Mean velocity check** (0 mark, optional): $\bar{v} = Q/(\pi R^{2}) = 7.31\times 10^{-10}/(1.257\times 10^{-5}) = 0.058\ \text{m/s}$ — physically reasonable for a medium-sized artery. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Blood Rheology Questions** > > 1. **Forgetting units**: Always write $\mu$ in Pa·s, $\tau$ in Pa, $\dot{\gamma}$ in s⁻¹. Examiners deduct ½ mark for missing units. > 2. **Mixing up absolute-value notation**: KTU answer sheets require `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid markdown breakage; in handwritten answers draw a thin vertical bar — *do not* use the absolute-value symbol when describing boundary conditions like $v(R) = 0$ which is simply a **no-slip condition**, not absolute value. > 3. **Using Hagen–Poiseuille blindly**: Examiners WILL deduct marks if you apply $\mu = \text{const}$ without justifying the use of apparent viscosity for blood. > 4. **Wrong sign convention in $\Delta P/L$**: Always state $\Delta P = P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{out}} > 0$. A negative pressure gradient yields a negative $Q$ — flag your sign explicitly. > 5. **Skipping the plug-flow core discussion** in Casson problems: Mentioning $r_c$ and the physical reason (yield stress) is mandatory for full marks. > 6. **Confusing Womersley and Reynolds**: $\alpha$ quantifies **unsteady inertia vs viscous** forces (pulsatility), while $\mathrm{Re}$ quantifies **steady inertia vs viscous** forces. Never interchange them. > 7. **Forgetting to mention the Fahraeus–Lindqvist effect limitation**: When asked about "large vessels", students often skip stating that this equation *fails* below $D \approx 1\ \text{mm}$. Examiners reward this awareness. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cardiovascular Transport & Large-Vessel Rheology** ### Core Concepts - ✅ The cardiovascular system is a **closed, pulsatile, two-circuit (systemic + pulmonary)** transport network. - ✅ **Blood** is a two-phase suspension: plasma (Newtonian, μ_p ≈ **1.2 cP**) + formed elements (RBCs dominate via Hct). - ✅ **Hematocrit** (Hct) ≈ 0.45 is the most important rheological variable. - ✅ In **large vessels (D > 1 mm)**, the continuum hypothesis holds; blood behaves as a **shear-thinning, non-Newtonian** fluid with a small yield stress. ### Essential Equations to Memorize - ✅ Hagen–Poiseuille: $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8\mu L}$ - ✅ Wall shear stress: $\tau_w = \dfrac{R\,\Delta P}{2L}$ - ✅ Power Law: $\tau = K\dot{\gamma}^{n}$, with $n \approx 0.6$–$0.8$ - ✅ Casson: $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{\mu_\infty \dot{\gamma}}$ - ✅ Carreau: $\mu_{app} = \mu_\infty + (\mu_0 - \mu_\infty)[1 + (\lambda\dot{\gamma})^{2}]^{(n-1)/2}$ - ✅ Hematocrit correction (high-shear, large vessels): $\mu = \mu_p(1 - H)^{-2.5}$ - ✅ Reynolds number: $\mathrm{Re} = \rho \bar{v} D / \mu$ - ✅ Womersley number: $\alpha = R\sqrt{\omega \rho / \mu}$ ### Physical Insights - ✅ **Aortic Re ≈ 3000** is at the upper limit of laminar steady flow — pulsatility flattens the profile. - ✅ **Womersley α ≈ 18** in aorta ⇒ inertia-dominated, **plug-like** velocity profile. - ✅ **Casson plug-flow core** $r_c = R\tau_y/\tau_w$ — radial zone of rigid-body motion where shear stress is below yield. - ✅ **Shear-thinning** is beneficial physiologically: reduces pumping power during high-flow exercise. ### Common Numbers - ✅ Cardiac output at rest: **5 L/min** - ✅ Heart rate: **70 bpm → 1.17 Hz → ω = 7.33 rad/s** - ✅ Mean arterial pressure: **100 mmHg = 13.3 kPa** - ✅ Aortic diameter: **25 mm** - ✅ Plasma viscosity: **1.2 cP** - ✅ Whole-blood high-shear viscosity (Hct 0.45): **≈ 3.5 cP** ### Examination Strategy - ✅ Always **define variables** before substituting numbers. - ✅ Always **comment on physical plausibility** of your numerical answer. - ✅ Always **state assumptions** (steady/unsteady, Newtonian/non-Newtonian, rigid/elastic, isothermal). - ✅ **Draw a sketch** of the cardiovascular loop or viscosity-vs-shear-rate curve whenever the question allows — KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** for clear, labeled diagrams.

topics.title: Small Tubes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT832 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Small Tubes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Biomedical Transport Phenomena**, the study of **Small Tubes** refers to the analysis of fluid flow, mass transport, and momentum transfer through conduits whose characteristic hydraulic diameters range from **1 µm to 3 mm**. This regime encompasses:
- **Capillaries** (inner diameter 5–10 µm)
- **Arterioles and venules** (10 µm – 1 mm)
- **Microfluidic lab-on-chip channels** (10–500 µm)
- **Medical catheters and stents** (sub-mm to few mm)

In this regime, the classical continuum assumption still holds (Knudsen number $Kn < 0.1$), but **viscous forces dominate over inertial forces**, **surface tension forces** become comparable to body forces, and **particle–wall interactions** (e.g., red blood cells) significantly alter the effective viscosity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Small tube analysis bridges macroscopic fluid mechanics (Hagen–Poiseuille flow) and microscopic phenomena (Fåhræus–Lindqvist effect), making it the most examinable interdisciplinary topic of Module 2.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture – The "Garden Hose vs. Straw" Effect**
>
> Imagine drinking a milkshake through a **wide straw** versus a **narrow stirring straw**. The narrow straw demands far more suction pressure for the same flow rate. This is *not* just because of friction — it is because the **fourth power of radius** ($R^4$) governs the resistance. Halving the radius increases the required pressure drop by **16 times** for the same volumetric flow. In the human body, this is exactly why a slight narrowing (stenosis) of an artery dramatically elevates blood pressure and stresses the vessel wall.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit | Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dynamic viscosity of water (37 °C) | $\mu$ | $\mathbf{6.9 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa·s}}$ | Baseline biological fluid reference |
| Density of whole blood | $\rho$ | $\mathbf{1060 \text{ kg/m}^3}$ | Used in Reynolds number |
| Surface tension of blood plasma | $\gamma$ | $\mathbf{0.056 \text{ N/m}}$ | Capillary rise calculations |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | $\mathbf{9.81 \text{ m/s}^2}$ | Hydrostatic head |
| Critical Reynolds number (tube) | $Re_{cr}$ | $\mathbf{2100}$ | Laminar–turbulent transition |
| Plasma viscosity | $\mu_p$ | $\mathbf{1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}}$ | Reference for Fåhræus–Lindqvist |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Key Physical Considerations in Small Tubes

The study of small tubes is governed by the following hierarchy of effects, each of which the KTU examiner expects you to recognize:

1. **Viscous Dominance over Inertia** — Reynolds number $Re \ll 2100$ almost always. The flow is **laminar** and **steady** (except in the aorta, where pulsatility is significant).
2. **Unidirectional Velocity Profile** — The flow is fully developed and unidirectional ($v_z = v_z(r)$ only), simplifying the Navier–Stokes equations drastically.
3. **No-Slip Boundary Condition** — At the rigid tube wall, the fluid velocity is **zero**. For blood vessels, the **endothelium** (a glycocalyx layer, $\sim 0.5$ µm thick) can effectively act as a *slip* plane in pathological states.
4. **Surface Tension Effects** — For tubes with diameter $D < 1$ mm, capillary forces become dominant and can drive or oppose bulk flow.
5. **Concentration Boundary Layer Coupling** — In mass transfer through small tubes (e.g., oxygen diffusion in lung bronchioles), the **Sherwood number** scaling changes.
6. **Fåhræus–Lindqvist Effect** — Apparent blood viscosity drops sharply when vessel diameter falls below $\sim 300$ µm because red blood cells (RBCs) migrate to the tube center, leaving a cell-free plasma layer near the wall.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it accounts for ~70% of derivation marks in Module 2.**

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hagen–Poiseuille Law** (volumetric flow) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | $Q$ [m³/s], $R$ [m], $\Delta P$ [Pa], $\mu$ [Pa·s], $L$ [m] | Steady laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid in a circular tube |
| 2 | **Hagen–Poiseuille Law** (mean velocity) | $\langle v \rangle = \dfrac{\Delta P \, R^2}{8 \mu L}$ | $\langle v \rangle$ [m/s] | Average velocity across the cross-section |
| 3 | **Velocity profile** (parabolic) | $v_z(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$ | $r$ [m] | Local axial velocity at radial position $r$ |
| 4 | **Maximum velocity** (at centerline) | $v_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta P \, R^2}{4 \mu L} = 2\langle v \rangle$ | $v_{max}$ [m/s] | Occurs at $r = 0$ |
| 5 | **Wall shear stress** | $\tau_w = \dfrac{R \Delta P}{2 L} = \dfrac{4 \mu \langle v \rangle}{R}$ | $\tau_w$ [Pa] | Stress exerted on the endothelium |
| 6 | **Pressure drop** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R^4}$ | Same as above | Used in vascular resistance problems |
| 7 | **Tube Reynolds number** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \langle v \rangle D}{\mu} = \dfrac{2 \rho Q}{\pi \mu R}$ | Dimensionless | Flow regime classification |
| 8 | **Hydraulic resistance** | $\mathcal{R}_h = \dfrac{\Delta P}{Q} = \dfrac{8 \mu L}{\pi R^4}$ | [Pa·s/m³] | Analogous to electrical resistance (Ohm's law for flow) |
| 9 | **Entrance length** (laminar) | $L_e = 0.06 \, Re \, D$ | [m] | Distance required for fully developed flow |
| 10 | **Capillary rise** (Laplace–Young) | $h = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g R}$ | $h$ [m], $\gamma$ [N/m], $\theta$ [rad] | Rise/fall in a vertical capillary tube |
| 11 | **Laplace pressure** (cylindrical interface) | $\Delta P_{Lap} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{R}$ | [Pa] | Pressure jump across a curved meniscus |
| 12 | **Fåhræus–Lindqvist empirical fit** | $\mu_{app} = \mu_\infty \left[1 + \left(\dfrac{D_c}{D}\right)^{0.645}\right]$ | $D_c \approx 1.6$ mm (characteristic) | Apparent blood viscosity in microvessels |
| 13 | **Stokes flow regime check** | $Re \ll 1$ | Dimensionless | Confirms dominance of viscous forces |
| 14 | **Womersley number** (pulsatility) | $\alpha = R \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \rho}{\mu}}$ | Dimensionless | Ratio of unsteady inertial to viscous forces |
| 15 | **Kinematic viscosity** | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | [m²/s] | Used in dimensionless groups |

### 2.3 Real-World Biomedical Utility

- **Cardiovascular diagnostics**: Estimate pressure drop across a **stenotic artery** (e.g., a 70% occlusion of a coronary artery) using Hagen–Poiseuille — clinically used in **fractional flow reserve (FFR)** calculations.
- **Microfluidic device design**: Optimize channel dimensions for **point-of-care diagnostics** (e.g., COVID-19 antigen test cartridges).
- **Dialysis and IV lines**: Determine the **pressure setting** required on a syringe pump to deliver a drug at a controlled rate through a small-bore catheter.
- **Lung alveoli modeling**: Capillary rise and Laplace pressure govern **alveolar stability** (surfactant deficiency → neonatal respiratory distress syndrome).
- **Drug delivery via microneedles**: Predict flow through pores of $\sim 20$ µm diameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The $R^4$ dependence in Hagen–Poiseuille is the **single most tested relationship** in this module. Always emphasize that **resistance scales as $1/R^4$**, not $1/R^2$.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hagen–Poiseuille Equation from Navier–Stokes (FULL)

**Starting Point: The Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$ for a Newtonian, incompressible fluid:**

$$
\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + (\vec{v} \cdot \nabla)\vec{v} \right) = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{v} + \rho \vec{g}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the assumptions of small-tube laminar flow:**

1. **Steady-state:** $\partial \vec{v} / \partial t = 0$
2. **Fully developed flow:** $\partial v_z / \partial z = 0$
3. **Axisymmetric:** $\partial / \partial \theta = 0$
4. **Unidirectional:** Only $v_z \neq 0$; $v_r = v_\theta = 0$
5. **Horizontal tube or neglecting gravity along axis:** Body force acts only radially (negligible for $z$-momentum)
6. **Negligible inertial term:** $Re \ll 1 \Rightarrow (\vec{v} \cdot \nabla)\vec{v} \approx 0$

**Step 2 — Reduce the $z$-component of Navier–Stokes:**

The $z$-component in cylindrical coordinates is:

$$
\rho \left( \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial t} + v_r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial \theta} + v_z \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu \left[ \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left( r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} \right) + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial \theta^2} + \frac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial z^2} \right] + \rho g_z
$$

Applying all Step-1 assumptions, this reduces to:

$$
0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu \left[ \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left( r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} \right) \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that $P$ is linear in $z$:**

Since $v_z$ depends only on $r$ (not on $z$), the pressure gradient $\partial P / \partial z$ must be a **constant** (call it $-G$, where $G > 0$ for flow in $+z$ direction). Thus:

$$
\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = -\frac{\Delta P}{L} = -G
$$

Substituting:

$$
\mu \cdot \frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) = -G
$$

**Step 4 — First integration (multiply both sides by $r/\mu$ and integrate):**

$$
\frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) = -\frac{G \, r}{\mu}
$$

Integrating with respect to $r$:

$$
r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{G \, r^2}{2 \mu} + C_1
$$

Divide by $r$:

$$
\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{G \, r}{2 \mu} + \frac{C_1}{r}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the boundary condition at the centerline:**

At the centerline $r = 0$, the velocity profile must be **finite and smooth** (no infinite slope). Therefore $C_1 = 0$ (else the term $C_1/r$ would blow up).

$$
\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{G \, r}{2 \mu}
$$

**Step 6 — Second integration:**

$$
v_z(r) = -\frac{G \, r^2}{4 \mu} + C_2
$$

**Step 7 — Apply the no-slip boundary condition at the wall:**

At $r = R$ (the tube wall), $v_z = 0$:

$$
0 = -\frac{G \, R^2}{4 \mu} + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = \frac{G \, R^2}{4 \mu}
$$

**Step 8 — Obtain the parabolic velocity profile:**

$$
\boxed{\, v_z(r) = \frac{G}{4\mu}\left( R^2 - r^2 \right) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\left( R^2 - r^2 \right) \,}
$$

**Step 9 — Compute the volumetric flow rate by integrating over the cross-section:**

$$
Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \int_0^R \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\left( R^2 - r^2 \right) 2\pi r \, dr
$$

Factor out constants:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_0^R \left( R^2 r - r^3 \right) dr
$$

Evaluate the integral:

$$
\int_0^R \left( R^2 r - r^3 \right) dr = \left[ \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = \frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{R^4}{4}
$$

Substitute back:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}
$$

**Final boxed result — the Hagen–Poiseuille Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\, Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L} \,}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capillary Rise (Jurin's Law)

**Step 1 — Force balance on the liquid column of height $h$:**

For a capillary of radius $R$ inserted into a wetting liquid (contact angle $\theta < 90°$), the liquid rises to a height $h$ at equilibrium.

**Step 2 — The upward force is due to surface tension acting around the tube perimeter:**

The vertical component of surface tension force around the wetted circumference is:

$$
F_{\uparrow} = \gamma \cdot (2\pi R) \cdot \cos\theta
$$

**Step 3 — The downward force is the weight of the liquid column (volume $\pi R^2 h$):**

$$
F_{\downarrow} = \rho g \cdot \pi R^2 h
$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two forces at static equilibrium:**

$$
\gamma \cdot 2\pi R \cdot \cos\theta = \rho g \cdot \pi R^2 h
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the capillary rise $h$:**

$$
\boxed{\, h = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g R} \,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does $h \propto 1/R$?** A narrower tube has a smaller cross-section, so less liquid mass needs to be lifted — but the surface-tension force also decreases linearly with $R$. The mass decreases as $R^2$ (volume $\sim R^2 h$), so $h$ scales as $1/R$. This is the opposite scaling to Hagen–Poiseuille (where flow $\propto R^4$).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wall Shear Stress from the Velocity Profile

Starting from the parabolic profile $v_z(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$:

The shear stress at any radial position is given by Newton's law of viscosity:

$$
\tau_{rz}(r) = -\mu \frac{dv_z}{dr}
$$

Differentiate:

$$
\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(-2r) = -\frac{\Delta P \, r}{2\mu L}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\tau_{rz}(r) = -\mu \cdot \left( -\frac{\Delta P \, r}{2\mu L} \right) = \frac{\Delta P \, r}{2L}
$$

At the wall ($r = R$):

$$
\boxed{\, \tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \, R}{2L} = \frac{4 \mu \langle v \rangle}{R} \,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Clinical Note:** Endothelial cells lining blood vessels sense $\tau_w$. Values of $\tau_w < 0.4$ Pa promote atherogenesis (plaque formation). This is why low-shear regions (e.g., arterial bifurcations) are **predilection sites for atherosclerosis**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Entrance Length for Laminar Flow

The **entrance length** $L_e$ is the distance from the tube inlet at which the velocity profile becomes fully developed. For laminar flow:

**Step 1 — Empirical correlation from stability analysis:**

$$
\frac{L_e}{D} = 0.06 \, Re
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $L_e$:**

$$
\boxed{\, L_e = 0.06 \, Re \, D = 0.06 \, \frac{\rho \langle v \rangle D^2}{\mu} \,}
$$

For a tube of diameter $D = 0.5$ mm with $Re = 100$:

$$
L_e = 0.06 \times 100 \times 5\times 10^{-4} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 3 \text{ mm}
$$

This is biologically significant because many small vessels are **shorter** than $L_e$, meaning the flow is *never* fully developed — a complication that the simple Hagen–Poiseuille formula does not capture.
