# 🎓 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

### **Q1. Define a Tissue Engineered Construct. List its three essential components.**
**(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A Tissue Engineered Construct (TEC) is a *three-dimensional, in vitro* or *in vivo* hybrid system in which living cells are seeded within a biodegradable, porous scaffold and exposed to biochemical and biophysical cues to regenerate damaged tissue.

The three essential components (the **"Tissue Engineering Triad"**) are:

1. **Cells** — the biological building blocks (e.g., chondrocytes, MSCs, hepatocytes).
2. **Scaffold** — a 3-D porous biomaterial matrix that provides structural support and a substrate for adhesion.
3. **Signals** — biochemical (growth factors like BMP-2, VEGF) and biophysical (mechanical, electrical) cues that direct cell behaviour.

---

### **Q2. Why does the centre of a thick tissue engineered scaffold undergo necrosis?**
**(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The centre of a thick scaffold undergoes necrosis because of **insufficient mass transport of oxygen and nutrients**.

* Cells consume $O_2$ and glucose at a constant rate $q_s$.
* These substrates reach the construct only by **diffusion** through the porous scaffold.
* As distance from the surface increases, the **diffusion path lengthens** and concentration falls parabolically: $C_s(x) = C_s^\infty - \frac{q_s}{2D_{\text{eff}}}\left[\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2 - x^2\right]$.
* When the centreline concentration drops below the **critical viability threshold** $C_s^{\text{crit}}$, cells die by hypoxia, producing **central necrosis**.

The **maximum thickness** before this occurs is the critical thickness $L_c = \sqrt{8 D_{\text{eff}} (C_s^\infty - C_s^{\text{crit}})/q_s}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retrieved from KTU Question Bank]**

---

### ✍️ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the steady-state oxygen concentration profile inside a **planar tissue engineered scaffold** of half-thickness $h$, with zero-order cellular consumption rate $q_{O_2}$ and effective diffusivity $D_{\text{eff}}$. State the boundary conditions used.
**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A planar cartilage construct has half-thickness $h = 0.3 \, mm$, $D_{\text{eff}} = 1.8 \times 10^{-9} \, m^2/s$, $q_{O_2} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \, kg/(m^3 \cdot s)$, $C_s^\infty = 0.25 \, kg/m^3$, and $C_s^{\text{crit}} = 0.05 \, kg/m^3$. Calculate **(i)** the oxygen concentration at the centreline, and **(ii)** the critical half-thickness $h_c$ above which necrosis will occur.
**(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Set up a 1-D steady-state mass balance on a differential element of thickness $dx$ at position $x$ from the centreline. In = Out + Consumed:

$$J_s(x) - J_s(x+dx) = q_s \, dx \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dJ_s}{dx} = -q_s$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply Fick's First Law in the porous scaffold:

$$J_s = -D_{\text{eff}} \frac{dC_s}{dx} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dJ_s}{dx} = -D_{\text{eff}} \frac{d^2 C_s}{dx^2}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Equate to get the governing ODE:

$$\frac{d^2 C_s}{dx^2} = \frac{q_s}{D_{\text{eff}}}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** State the boundary conditions:

> * Symmetry at centre: $\left.\dfrac{dC_s}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = 0$
> * Surface concentration: $C_s(h) = C_s^\infty$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Integrate once:

$$\frac{dC_s}{dx} = \frac{q_s}{D_{\text{eff}}} x \quad (\text{since } C_1 = 0)$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Integrate again and apply the second BC:

$$C_s(x) = \frac{q_s}{2 D_{\text{eff}}} x^2 + C_s^\infty - \frac{q_s h^2}{2 D_{\text{eff}}}$$

$$C_s(x) = C_s^\infty - \frac{q_s}{2 D_{\text{eff}}} \left(h^2 - x^2\right)$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Box the final profile and physically interpret: concentration is **maximum at the surface** and **minimum at the centre**, decreasing parabolically.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)**

**(i) Centreline concentration (3 marks):** Substitute $x = 0$:

$$C_s(0) = C_s^\infty - \frac{q_s h^2}{2 D_{\text{eff}}}$$

$$= 0.25 - \frac{(4 \times 10^{-4})(0.3 \times 10^{-3})^2}{2 \times (1.8 \times 10^{-9})}$$

$$= 0.25 - \frac{(4 \times 10^{-4})(9 \times 10^{-8})}{3.6 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$= 0.25 - \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-11}}{3.6 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.25 - 0.01 = 0.24 \, kg/m^3$$

$\boxed{C_s(0) = 0.24 \, kg/m^3}$

**(ii) Critical half-thickness (4 marks):** Set $C_s(0) = C_s^{\text{crit}}$:

$$h_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 D_{\text{eff}} (C_s^\infty - C_s^{\text{crit}})}{q_s}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (1.8 \times 10^{-9}) \times (0.25 - 0.05)}{4 \times 10^{-4}}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.20}{4 \times 10^{-4}}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{7.2 \times 10^{-10}}{4 \times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{h_c \approx 1.34 \times 10^{-3} \, m = 1.34 \, mm}$$

Since actual $h = 0.3 \, mm < h_c = 1.34 \, mm$, **the construct is safe from central necrosis**.

---

### ✍️ **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Tissue Engineering Triad** and describe how mass transport limitations arise in each component. List two strategies to overcome these limitations.
**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **spherical hepatocyte spheroid** of radius $R$ is cultured in a stirred bioreactor. Derive the steady-state concentration profile $C_s(r)$ assuming zero-order oxygen consumption. If $R = 200 \, \mu m$, $D_{\text{eff}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-9} \, m^2/s$, $q_{O_2} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \, kg/(m^3 \cdot s)$, and $C_s^\infty = 0.30 \, kg/m^3$, determine whether the centre of the spheroid remains viable. Use $C_s^{\text{crit}} = 0.04 \, kg/m^3$.
**(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Triad & Transport (7 Marks)**

| Component | Transport Limitation | Mitigation Strategy |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Scaffold** | Low effective diffusivity ($\varepsilon/\tau$ small) | Increase porosity, reduce tortuosity, use nanofibres |
| **Cells** | High consumption rate $q_s$ near core | Use lower-density seeding, perfusion, hypoxic preconditioning |
| **Signals (growth factors)** | Slow diffusive release | Controlled-release microspheres, heparin-binding domains |

**Two broad strategies** (2 marks each):

1. **Scaffold Architecture Optimisation:** Use highly porous, interconnected pore networks; incorporate **channel architectures** (3-D printed) to mimic capillaries; use **electrospun nanofibres** with aligned pores.
2. **Bioreactor Perfusion:** Apply **forced convection** through the scaffold (interstitial flow) to overcome the diffusion limit. Quantified by **Péclet number** $Pe = vL/D_{\text{eff}}$; at $Pe > 1$, convection dominates and cells throughout the construct receive adequate supply.

(Diagrams to be drawn: **(a)** Triangular triad diagram with cells/scaffold/signals at vertices and the TEC in the centre; **(b)** schematic of concentration gradient from bulk to centre.)

**Part (b) — Spherical Spheroid (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Governing ODE in spherical coordinates:

$$\frac{1}{r^2} \frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2 \frac{dC_s}{dr}\right) = \frac{q_s}{D_{\text{eff}}}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Multiply by $r^2$ and integrate once:

$$r^2 \frac{dC_s}{dr} = \frac{q_s}{3 D_{\text{eff}}} r^3 + C_1$$

No singularity at $r=0$ implies $C_1 = 0$:

$$\frac{dC_s}{dr} = \frac{q_s}{3 D_{\text{eff}}} r$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Integrate again:

$$C_s(r) = \frac{q_s}{6 D_{\text{eff}}} r^2 + C_2$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply $C_s(R) = C_s^\infty$:

$$C_2 = C_s^\infty - \frac{q_s R^2}{6 D_{\text{eff}}}$$

Final profile:

$$C_s(r) = C_s^\infty - \frac{q_s}{6 D_{\text{eff}}} \left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

**Step 5 (3 marks):** Centreline concentration at $r = 0$:

$$C_s(0) = C_s^\infty - \frac{q_s R^2}{6 D_{\text{eff}}}$$

$$= 0.30 - \frac{(5 \times 10^{-4})(200 \times 10^{-6})^2}{6 \times (1.2 \times 10^{-9})}$$

$$= 0.30 - \frac{(5 \times 10^{-4})(4 \times 10^{-8})}{7.2 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$= 0.30 - \frac{2 \times 10^{-11}}{7.2 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.30 - 2.78 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{C_s(0) \approx 0.297 \, kg/m^3}$$

Since $C_s(0) = 0.297 > C_s^{\text{crit}} = 0.04$, **the spheroid centre is viable**. ✅

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (Per KTU Valuation Key):**
>
> 1. **Missing symmetry boundary condition** — forgetting $\frac{dC_s}{dx}\big|_{x=0} = 0$ in slab problems costs **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Coordinate system mismatch** — choosing $x=0$ at the surface vs. centreline changes the formula constant between **2 and 8**. **Always declare your origin** explicitly in the first line of the answer.
> 3. **Units conversion** — failing to convert $\mu m$ to $m$ or $mm$ to $m$ in numericals results in answers that are off by **$10^6$ or $10^3$**. KTU examiners **specifically check** for consistent SI units.
> 4. **Sign error in Fick's law** — writing $\frac{d^2C_s}{dx^2} = -\frac{q_s}{D_{\text{eff}}}$ instead of the correct positive sign loses **1 mark** and propagates errors.
> 5. **No physical interpretation** — the **final boxed equation is not enough**; the KTU 2024 scheme requires a 1–2 sentence physical interpretation of the result for full marks.
> 6. **Ignoring porosity and tortuosity** — using $D_{\text{free}}$ instead of $D_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\varepsilon}{\tau} D_{\text{free}}$ is a **favourite examiner trap**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist 📝

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision — Tissue Engineered Constructs (Module 3)**
>
> * 🧬 **Definition:** TEC = Cells + Scaffold + Signals (the "Tissue Engineering Triad").
> * 🧱 **Scaffold** = biodegradable porous matrix; key parameters $\varepsilon$, $\tau$, $D_{\text{eff}}$.
> * 💨 **Effective Diffusivity:** $D_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{\tau} D_{\text{free}}$.
> * 📉 **Governing ODE (slab, 1-D, zero-order):** $\dfrac{d^2 C_s}{dx^2} = \dfrac{q_s}{D_{\text{eff}}}$.
> * 📐 **Critical Thickness (slab):** $L_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 D_{\text{eff}} \Delta C}{q_s}}$.
> * 🎯 **Critical Radius (cylinder):** $R_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 D_{\text{eff}} \Delta C}{q_s}}$.
> * 🔵 **Critical Radius (sphere):** $R_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 D_{\text{eff}} \Delta C}{q_s}}$.
> * 🧮 **Thiele Modulus:** $\phi^2 = \dfrac{k L^2}{D_{\text{eff}}}$ (for first-order kinetics).
> * ⚖️ **Effectiveness Factor:** $\eta = \dfrac{\tanh \phi}{\phi}$.
> * 💧 **Péclet Number:** $Pe = \dfrac{v L}{D_{\text{eff}}}$ — quantifies perfusion benefit in bioreactors.
> * ⚠️ **Central Necrosis Limit:** typical avascular tissue thickness ~ **100–200 $\mu m$** (static culture).
> * 🏭 **Engineering Solutions:** porous scaffolds, perfusion bioreactors, vascularised channels, controlled growth factor release.
> * 📊 **Typical Constants to Memorise:** $D_{O_2,\text{tissue}} \approx 10^{-9} \, m^2/s$, $q_{O_2} \approx 5 \times 10^{-4} \, kg/(m^3 \cdot s)$, $C_s^{\text{crit}} \approx 0.04 \, kg/m^3$.
> * 🎓 **Exam Mantra:** *State coordinates → Write BCs → Derive ODE → Solve → Box → Interpret physically.*

topics.title: Oxygen Transport in Perfusion Bioreactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT832 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Oxygen Transport in Perfusion Bioreactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oxygen Transport in Perfusion Bioreactors** is the coupled **convective–diffusive–consumptive** mass transfer of dissolved molecular oxygen (O₂) from a perfusing culture medium, through a porous tissue-engineered scaffold, to the embedded cell population, governed by the species continuity equation in porous media.

Mathematically, the phenomenon is described by the **Convection–Diffusion–Reaction (CDR) equation** applied to a porous, cell-seeded scaffold:

$$\varepsilon \frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\mathbf{v} \, C) = \nabla \cdot (D_{\text{eff}} \, \nabla C) - R(C)$$

where:
- $C$ = dissolved O₂ concentration in the perfusate (mol/m³ or mol/L)
- $\mathbf{v}$ = superficial (interstitial) velocity vector of the perfusate (m/s)
- $\varepsilon$ = scaffold porosity (dimensionless, 0 < ε < 1)
- $D_{\text{eff}}$ = **effective diffusion coefficient** of O₂ in the scaffold (m²/s)
- $R(C)$ = volumetric O₂ consumption rate of cells (mol/(m³·s))

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "City Water Supply" Analogy

Imagine a long, sponge-filled pipe through which river water is pumped. Houses (cells) are embedded inside the sponge and constantly consume dissolved air. The amount of air that reaches the houses at the far end of the pipe depends on three things working together:

| Real-World Element | Bioreactor Equivalent | Engineering Question |
|---|---|---|
| Water flow through the pipe | Convective transport of perfusate | Is the flow fast enough? |
| Air seeping from water into the sponge | Diffusive transport in porous matrix | Is the sponge too dense? |
| Houses breathing oxygen | Cellular O₂ consumption (metabolism) | Are there too many cells? |
| Houses at the far end suffocating | Necrotic core in tissue construct | Will the construct stay viable? |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in tissue engineering:** In static culture, diffusion alone limits viable tissue thickness to **~200 µm** (the O₂ diffusion length in aqueous gels). Perfusion bioreactors overcome this by **forcing fresh, oxygenated medium through the scaffold**, much like a city pumping fresh water through a long distribution pipe. Understanding the balance between flow, diffusion, and consumption is the central engineering problem.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Reference Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key O₂ Property Data Used Throughout This Module** (at 37 °C, 1 atm):
> - Henry's law constant for O₂ in water: $H_{O_2} \approx 769.2 \;\text{L·atm/mol}$  (or $k_H \approx 1.3 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{mol/(L·Pa)}$)
> - Free diffusivity of O₂ in water: $D_{0,O_2} \approx 3.0 \times 10^{-9} \;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
> - Dissolved O₂ in air-saturated DMEM at 37 °C: $C^* \approx 0.20 \;\text{mmol/L} = 200 \;\mu\text{mol/L}$
> - Critical O₂ tension (below which hypoxia ensues): $C_{\text{crit}} \approx 0.05 \;\text{mmol/L} = 50 \;\mu\text{mol/L}$
> - Typical hepatocyte consumption: $V_{\max} \approx 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{mol/(m}^3\text{·s)}$

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Coupled Transport Mechanisms

### 2.1.1 Convective Transport (Advection)
Driven by the bulk motion of the perfusate through the scaffold pores. The volumetric flow $Q$ produces a superficial velocity $v = Q/A_{\text{cross}}$ that physically carries dissolved O₂ along the flow path. The convective flux is $\mathbf{N}_{\text{conv}} = \mathbf{v} C$.

### 2.1.2 Diffusive Transport
Driven by concentration gradients. In a porous scaffold, diffusion is hindered by:
- **Tortuosity** ($\tau \ge 1$) — the longer, winding path molecules must take.
- **Porosity** ($\varepsilon < 1$) — the reduced cross-section available for transport.

The most widely used correlation is the **Bruggeman equation**:

$$D_{\text{eff}} = D_0 \, \varepsilon^{3/2} / \tau \approx D_0 \, \varepsilon^{1.5}$$

The diffusive flux obeys **Fick's first law**: $\mathbf{N}_{\text{diff}} = -D_{\text{eff}} \nabla C$.

### 2.1.3 Reactive Consumption (Sink Term)
Cells consume O₂ according to **Michaelis–Menten kinetics** (saturation-limited), which captures the biochemistry of cytochrome c oxidase:

$$R(C) = \rho_{\text{cell}} \, q_{O_2} = \frac{V_{\max} \, C}{K_m + C}$$

where:
- $V_{\max}$ = maximum specific O₂ uptake rate (mol O₂ per m³ scaffold per s)
- $K_m$ = Michaelis constant (≈ 1–10 µmol/L for most mammalian cells)
- $\rho_{\text{cell}}$ = cell density per scaffold volume

> [!NOTE]
> **Two useful limiting cases of Michaelis–Menten kinetics:**
> 1. **Zero-order** ($C \gg K_m$): $R(C) \approx V_{\max}$ — consumption is independent of O₂ availability (a flat consumption plateau).
> 2. **First-order** ($C \ll K_m$): $R(C) \approx (V_{\max}/K_m) C$ — consumption is linear in concentration (a proportional drain).

## 2.2 The Dimensionless Groups That Govern Behavior

The relative importance of convection, diffusion, and reaction is captured by three dimensionless numbers. **Mastering these is essential for KTU problems.**

| Group | Definition | Physical Meaning | Typical Range in Perfusion |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Péclet number** | $Pe = v L / D_{\text{eff}}$ | Convection ÷ Diffusion | $10^{-1}$ to $10^2$ |
| **Damköhler number** | $Da = V_{\max} L / (C_0 v)$ | Reaction rate ÷ Convective supply | $10^{-2}$ to $10^1$ |
| **Thiele modulus** | $\phi = L \sqrt{V_{\max} / (D_{\text{eff}} C_0)}$ | Reaction rate ÷ Diffusive supply | $10^{-1}$ to $10^1$ |
| **Sherwood number** | $Sh = k_L L / D_{\text{eff}}$ | Convective mass transfer ÷ Diffusion | 1 to 10 |
| **Reynolds number** | $Re = \rho v d_p / \mu$ | Inertia ÷ Viscous forces (pore-scale) | $10^{-3}$ to $10^0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation rules for the exam:**
> - $Pe \gg 1$: Convection dominates → concentration profile is **axial** (1-D along flow).
> - $Pe \ll 1$: Diffusion dominates → profile is **radial** (2-D, classical diffusion limitation).
> - $Da \ge 1$: Reaction outruns supply → **complete consumption** and possible necrotic core.
> - $Da \ll 1$: Reaction is slow → concentration remains close to inlet value.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All key equations, boundary conditions, and units for Oxygen Transport in Perfusion Bioreactors — memorize this table.**

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General CDR equation in porous scaffold | $\varepsilon \partial_t C + \nabla\!\cdot\!(\mathbf{v} C) = \nabla\!\cdot\!(D_{\text{eff}} \nabla C) - R(C)$ | $C$ [mol/m³], $\mathbf{v}$ [m/s], $D_{\text{eff}}$ [m²/s] |
| 2 | Bruggeman effective diffusivity | $D_{\text{eff}} = D_0 \, \varepsilon^{1.5}$ | $D_0 \approx 3 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s in water |
| 3 | Superficial velocity | $v = Q / A_c$ | $Q$ [m³/s], $A_c$ [m²] |
| 4 | Michaelis–Menten consumption | $R(C) = V_{\max} C / (K_m + C)$ | $V_{\max}$ [mol/(m³·s)], $K_m$ [mol/m³] |
| 5 | Zero-order rate (high $C$) | $R = V_{\max}$ | — |
| 6 | First-order rate (low $C$) | $R = (V_{\max}/K_m) \, C$ | — |
| 7 | Péclet number | $Pe = v L / D_{\text{eff}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Damköhler number | $Da = V_{\max} L / (C_0 v)$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Thiele modulus | $\phi = L \sqrt{V_{\max}/(D_{\text{eff}} C_0)}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Henry's law for O₂ | $C = k_H \, p_{O_2}$ | $k_H \approx 1.3 \times 10^{-6}$ mol/(L·Pa) |
| 11 | Inlet boundary condition | $C(0) = C_0$ | $C_0$ = feed concentration |
| 12 | Outlet BC (sealed end) | $\partial_x C(L) = 0$ | No axial flux at exit |
| 13 | Wall BC (impermeable) | $\partial_n C \vert_{\text{wall}} = 0$ | $\partial_n$ = normal derivative |
| 14 | Critical penetration depth | $L_{\text{crit}} = v(C_0 - C_{\text{crit}})/V_{\max}$ | For high-Pe limit |
| 15 | Krogh cylinder minimum C | $C_{\min} = C_0 - (R_0 r_t^2 / 4D)[1 - (r_c/r_t)^2 + 2 \ln(r_t/r_c)]$ | $r_c$ = capillary radius, $r_t$ = tissue radius |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The framework above directly enables:
- **Design of bioreactor flow rates**: choosing $Q$ to maintain $C > C_{\text{crit}}$ everywhere.
- **Scaffold architecture selection**: tuning $\varepsilon$ and $\tau$ to balance mechanical strength vs. transport.
- **Cell seeding density limits**: predicting the maximum $\rho_{\text{cell}}$ that avoids hypoxia.
- **Scale-up of tissue constructs**: extending from 1-mm lab chips to clinically relevant cm-scale grafts.
- **Drug and growth-factor delivery modeling**: the same CDR equation governs nutrient, cytokine, and waste transport.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are written in full, with no skipped algebraic steps, so they can be reproduced verbatim in a KTU examination answer.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — 1-D Steady-State Oxygen Profile Along a Perfusion Scaffold (Zero-Order Consumption)

**Problem statement:** Oxygenated medium flows with superficial velocity $v$ through a porous cylindrical scaffold of length $L$ and cross-section $A_c$. The scaffold is uniformly seeded with cells that consume O₂ at a constant rate $V_{\max}$ (zero-order assumption, valid for $C \gg K_m$). Derive the steady-state axial concentration profile $C(x)$.

### Step 1 — State the governing equation

We begin with the general CDR equation. Under the assumptions of:
- Steady state ($\partial_t C = 0$),
- One-dimensional variation along the flow ($x$),
- Constant superficial velocity $v$ and constant $D_{\text{eff}}$,
- Zero-order consumption $R(C) = V_{\max}$,

the equation collapses from a vector form to a single ODE:

$$v \frac{dC}{dx} = D_{\text{eff}} \frac{d^2 C}{dx^2} - V_{\max}$$

### Step 2 — Identify the boundary conditions

**Inlet (x = 0):** The perfusate entering the scaffold carries the dissolved O₂ concentration of the feed reservoir.

$$C(0) = C_0$$

**Outlet (x = L):** Assuming the scaffold terminates in a sealed or symmetric manner, the axial diffusive flux must vanish.

$$\left.\frac{dC}{dx}\right|_{x=L} = 0$$

### Step 3 — Rewrite the ODE in standard form

Rearranging, isolate the highest derivative:

$$\frac{d^2 C}{dx^2} - \frac{v}{D_{\text{eff}}} \frac{dC}{dx} = \frac{V_{\max}}{D_{\text{eff}}}$$

Define the constant $\alpha = v / D_{\text{eff}}$ to obtain:

$$\frac{d^2 C}{dx^2} - \alpha \frac{dC}{dx} = \frac{V_{\max}}{D_{\text{eff}}}$$

### Step 4 — Solve the homogeneous equation

The homogeneous part $C'' - \alpha C' = 0$ has the characteristic equation:

$$r^2 - \alpha r = r(r - \alpha) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; r_1 = 0, \;\; r_2 = \alpha = v / D_{\text{eff}}$$

Therefore the homogeneous solution is:

$$C_h(x) = A + B \, e^{\alpha x} = A + B \, e^{v x / D_{\text{eff}}}$$

### Step 5 — Find a particular solution

Since the right-hand side is a constant and $r = 0$ is already a root of the characteristic equation, we try a particular solution of the form $C_p = \beta x$. Substituting:

$$0 - \alpha \beta = \frac{V_{\max}}{D_{\text{eff}}} \;\Rightarrow\; \beta = -\frac{V_{\max}}{\alpha D_{\text{eff}}} = -\frac{V_{\max}}{v}$$

Thus $C_p(x) = -\dfrac{V_{\max}}{v} x$.

### Step 6 — Write the general solution

$$C(x) = A + B \, e^{v x / D_{\text{eff}}} - \frac{V_{\max}}{v} x$$

### Step 7 — Apply the outlet BC to find B

Differentiate: $\dfrac{dC}{dx} = B \dfrac{v}{D_{\text{eff}}} e^{v x / D_{\text{eff}}} - \dfrac{V_{\max}}{v}$.

At $x = L$, $\dfrac{dC}{dx} = 0$:

$$B \frac{v}{D_{\text{eff}}} e^{v L / D_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{V_{\max}}{v}$$

$$B = \frac{V_{\max} D_{\text{eff}}}{v^2} \, e^{-v L / D_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{V_{\max} L^2}{D_{\text{eff}} \, (v L / D_{\text{eff}})^2} \, e^{-v L / D_{\text{eff}}}$$

In terms of the Péclet number $Pe = v L / D_{\text{eff}}$:

$$B = \frac{V_{\max} L}{v \, Pe} \, e^{-Pe} = \frac{V_{\max} L^2}{D_{\text{eff}} \, Pe^2} \, e^{-Pe}$$

### Step 8 — Apply the inlet BC to find A

At $x = 0$: $C(0) = A + B = C_0$, so:

$$A = C_0 - B = C_0 - \frac{V_{\max} L^2}{D_{\text{eff}} \, Pe^2} \, e^{-Pe}$$

### Step 9 — Assemble the final axial profile

$$\boxed{\;C(x) = C_0 + \frac{V_{\max} L^2}{D_{\text{eff}} \, Pe^2} \, e^{-Pe} \left( e^{v x / D_{\text{eff}}} - 1 \right) - \frac{V_{\max}}{v} x\;}$$

Or, equivalently in nondimensional form $\xi = x/L$, $c = C/C_0$:

$$c(\xi) = 1 + \frac{Da}{Pe}\left( e^{-Pe(1-\xi)} - 1 \right) - Da \cdot \xi$$

where $Da = V_{\max} L / (C_0 v)$.

### Step 10 — Extract the design-limiting concentration (outlet)

The minimum O₂ concentration occurs at the outlet $x = L$ (the farthest point from the oxygen source). Substitute $x = L$:

$$C_{\min} = C(L) = C_0 - \frac{V_{\max} L}{v}\left[1 - \frac{1}{Pe}\left(1 - e^{-Pe}\right)\right]$$

For the engineering limit $Pe \gg 1$ (convection-dominated), the bracket tends to 1, giving the simple design rule:

$$C_{\min} \approx C_0 - \frac{V_{\max} L}{v}$$

### Step 11 — Compute the minimum required flow rate

To keep $C_{\min} \ge C_{\text{crit}}$ (the threshold for cell viability), rearrange:

$$v_{\min} = \frac{V_{\max} L}{C_0 - C_{\text{crit}}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad Q_{\min} = \frac{V_{\max} L \, A_c}{C_0 - C_{\text{crit}}}$$

This is the single most important design equation in the module — it is **frequently asked in KTU exams.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Radial Oxygen Profile in a Krogh-Type Tissue Cylinder (Diffusion-Limited)

**Problem statement:** A capillary of radius $r_c$ supplies O₂ at concentration $C_0$ to a surrounding cylindrical tissue region of outer radius $r_t$ (the "Krogh cylinder"). The cells consume O₂ at a constant zero-order rate $R_0$. Derive the steady-state radial profile $C(r)$.

### Step 1 — Set up the radial diffusion equation

With no flow in the tissue and steady state, the CDR equation reduces to a 1-D radial diffusion–reaction ODE:

$$D \left[\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r \frac{dC}{dr}\right)\right] = R_0$$

Or, expanding:

$$\frac{d^2 C}{dr^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{dC}{dr} = \frac{R_0}{D}$$

### Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions

**At the capillary wall ($r = r_c$):** The O₂ concentration equals the capillary blood value $C_0$.

$$C(r_c) = C_0$$

**At the outer tissue boundary ($r = r_t$):** By symmetry between adjacent Krogh cylinders, no O₂ crosses this surface.

$$\left.\frac{dC}{dr}\right|_{r = r_t} = 0$$

### Step 3 — First integration

Let $u = r \, dC/dr$. Then:

$$\frac{du}{dr} = \frac{R_0 r}{D}$$

Integrating:

$$u(r) = \frac{R_0 r^2}{2D} + K_1 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{dC}{dr} = \frac{R_0 r}{2D} + \frac{K_1}{r}$$

### Step 4 — Second integration

$$C(r) = \frac{R_0 r^2}{4D} + K_1 \ln r + K_2$$

### Step 5 — Apply the outer BC to find $K_1$

At $r = r_t$, $dC/dr = 0$:

$$0 = \frac{R_0 r_t}{2D} + \frac{K_1}{r_t} \;\Rightarrow\; K_1 = -\frac{R_0 r_t^2}{2D}$$

### Step 6 — Apply the capillary BC to find $K_2$

At $r = r_c$, $C = C_0$:

$$C_0 = \frac{R_0 r_c^2}{4D} - \frac{R_0 r_t^2}{2D} \ln r_c + K_2$$

$$K_2 = C_0 - \frac{R_0 r_c^2}{4D} + \frac{R_0 r_t^2}{2D} \ln r_c$$

### Step 7 — Assemble the full radial profile

$$\boxed{\;C(r) = C_0 + \frac{R_0}{4D}\left(r^2 - r_c^2\right) + \frac{R_0 r_t^2}{2D}\ln\!\left(\frac{r_c}{r}\right)\;}$$

### Step 8 — Find the minimum concentration

The minimum is at the outer boundary $r = r_t$ (the tissue "edge" farthest from the capillary). Substituting:

$$C_{\min} = C_0 - \frac{R_0}{4D}\left[r_t^2 - r_c^2 + 2 r_t^2 \ln\!\left(\frac{r_t}{r_c}\right)\right]$$

### Step 9 — Compute the maximum viable tissue radius

Setting $C_{\min} = C_{\text{crit}}$ and solving for $r_t$:

$$r_{t,\max} \; \text{satisfies} \; r_{t,\max}^2 - r_c^2 + 2 r_{t,\max}^2 \ln\!\left(\frac{r_{t,\max}}{r_c}\right) = \frac{4 D \, (C_0 - C_{\text{crit}})}{R_0}$$

This implicit equation has no closed-form solution for $r_{t,\max}$ and is solved numerically. The right-hand side represents the **diffusive oxygen transport capacity** of the tissue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order-of-magnitude result for muscle tissue** (at rest, $R_0 \approx 3 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/(m³·s), $D \approx 2 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s, $C_0 - C_{\text{crit}} \approx 0.15$ mmol/L): $r_{t,\max} \approx 100$ µm, **matching the well-known Krogh cylinder value of ~200 µm diameter** for mammalian muscle. This is the fundamental reason vascularization is required for thick tissues.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Non-Dimensionalization and Identification of the Critical Bioreactor Length

Combining the convective transport rate $v C_0 / L$ with the consumption rate $V_{\max}$ gives the **Damköhler number**, the central design index:

$$Da = \frac{V_{\max} L}{C_0 v}$$

The condition for **avoiding a necrotic core** is $C_{\min} \ge C_{\text{crit}}$. Using the high-Péclet approximation from §3.1:

$$C_0 - \frac{V_{\max} L}{v} \ge C_{\text{crit}} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{V_{\max} L}{C_0 v} \le 1 - \frac{C_{\text{crit}}}{C_0}$$

$$\boxed{\;Da_{\max} = 1 - \frac{C_{\text{crit}}}{C_0}\;}$$

**Example:** With $C_0 = 0.20$ mmol/L and $C_{\text{crit}} = 0.05$ mmol/L, the critical Damköhler number is $Da_{\max} = 0.75$. Operating the bioreactor above this value guarantees that the outlet O₂ will fall into the hypoxic regime and necrotic regions will form.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Regime | Conditions | Governing Equation | Solution Behavior | Design Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Convection-dominated (high $Pe$) | $Pe > 10$, $Da < 1$ | $v \, dC/dx = -V_{\max}$ | $C(x) = C_0 - V_{\max} x / v$ | Linear drop; minimum flow rule applies |
| Diffusion-dominated (low $Pe$) | $Pe < 0.1$ | $D_{\text{eff}} d^2C/dx^2 = V_{\max}$ | Parabolic profile, symmetric | Radial transport limits construct thickness |
| Reaction-limited | $Da \ll 1$ | $C \approx C_0$ | Nearly flat profile | Cell density is too low to be exploited |
| Supply-limited (necrotic) | $Da \ge Da_{\max}$ | $C(L) < C_{\text{crit}}$ | Hypoxic outlet | Increase $v$ or decrease $L$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Perfusion Bioreactor Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture of a perfusion bioreactor system**, showing the oxygen transport pathways and the mass-transfer interactions that occur in the scaffold.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    reservoirA[OXYGENATOR RESERVOIR\nC = C0 saturated] --> pumpA[PERISTALTIC PUMP\nQ, v = Q/Ac]
    pumpA --> inletA[INLET MANIFOLD\nC = C0]
    inletA --> scaffoldA[POROUS SCAFFOLD\nepsilon, tau, L]
    scaffoldA --> cellsA[CELL POPULATION\nRho_cell, Vmax, Km]
    cellsA -. CONSUMES O2 .-> scaffoldA
    scaffoldA --> outletA[OUTLET MANIFOLD\nC = C_L]
    outletA --> sampleA[OFFLINE ANALYZER\nC_L, pO2]
    sampleA --> returnA[RETURN LINE]
    returnA --> reservoirA
    diffuserA[AXIAL DIFFUSION\nD_eff grad C] -.-> scaffoldA
    advectionA[AXIAL ADVECTION\nv C] -.-> scaffoldA
    radialA[RADIAL DIFFUSION\nin scaffold wall] -.-> scaffoldA
    controlsA[CONTROL LOOP\nadjusts Q] -.-> pumpA
    sampleA -. feedback .-> controlsA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram for exam purposes:** The solid arrows represent the **bulk flow path** of the perfusate. The dotted arrows represent the **three transport mechanisms** acting inside the scaffold: axial convection, axial diffusion, and radial diffusion. The cell population is the **sink**, drawing oxygen from the flowing medium. The control loop modifies the pump flow rate based on outlet O₂ measurements to maintain $C \ge C_{\text{crit}}$ throughout the construct.

## 4.2 Mass-Transfer Resistance Network (Electrical Analogy)

Mass transfer through a perfusion scaffold can be visualized as a **resistance network**, analogous to electrical resistors in series. This is a very common KTU exam visualization.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bulkA[BULK PERFUSATE\nC = C_bulk] --> R1A[EXTERNAL FILM\nR_film = 1 / k_L A]
    R1A --> R2A[INTERPARTICLE DIFFUSION\nR_int = delta / D_eff A]
    R2A --> R3A[INTRAPARTICLE DIFFUSION\nR_pore = L_p / D_pore A]
    R3A --> cellA[CELLULAR CONSUMPTION\nR_react = 1 / k_c A]
    cellA --> CcellA[AT CELL SURFACE\nC = C_cell]
    R1A ---|main mass transfer pathway| cellA
```

**Analytical meaning of the resistances:**

| Resistance | Symbol | Physical Origin | Engineering Lever |
|---|---|---|---|
| External film | $R_{\text{film}} = 1 / (k_L A)$ | Boundary layer at scaffold surface | Increase flow velocity $v$ |
| Interparticle | $R_{\text{int}} = \delta / (D_{\text{eff}} A)$ | Macropore diffusion | Increase porosity $\varepsilon$ |
| Intraparticle | $R_{\text{pore}} = L_p / (D_{\text{pore}} A)$ | Micropore diffusion in scaffold struts | Reduce strut thickness |
| Reaction | $R_{\text{react}} = 1 / (k_c A)$ | Cellular uptake kinetics | Lower $V_{\max}$ (cell density) |

The **rate-limiting step** is the one with the largest resistance. In a well-designed perfusion bioreactor, $R_{\text{film}} \ll R_{\text{int}} \approx R_{\text{react}}$, meaning that **convective supply and cellular demand are balanced**.

## 4.3 Oxygen Concentration Profile Along Scaffold Length (Schematic Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    plotA["C vs x PLOT"] --> axisA[Axis: x from 0 to L, C from 0 to C0]
    axisA --> curveA[Profile: C0 at x=0\nlinear drop to C_L at x=L\nfor high Pe]
    axisA --> critlineA[Horizontal dashed line at C_crit]
    curveA -.-> intersectA{intersection with C_crit}
    intersectA --> safeA[SAFE ZONE: C greater than C_crit everywhere]
    intersectA --> hypoxicA[HYPOXIC ZONE: C below C_crit near outlet\nnecrotic core forms]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define the Péclet number for mass transfer in a perfusion bioreactor. Explain its physical significance and indicate what value range corresponds to a "convection-dominated" regime.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The Péclet number for mass transfer in a perfusion bioreactor is defined as the ratio of convective to diffusive transport rates:
> $$Pe = \frac{v L}{D_{\text{eff}}}$$
> where $v$ is the superficial velocity of the perfusate, $L$ is a characteristic length (scaffold thickness or diameter), and $D_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective O₂ diffusivity in the porous scaffold.
>
> **Physical significance:** $Pe$ quantifies the relative importance of bulk convective transport versus molecular diffusion in carrying oxygen to the cells. When $Pe \gg 1$ (typically $Pe > 10$), the medium's bulk flow dominates and the O₂ profile is predominantly axial and one-dimensional. When $Pe \ll 1$ (typically $Pe < 0.1$), diffusion dominates and the profile is essentially radial — this is the regime where classical diffusion-limited growth (Krogh cylinder) applies. In the intermediate range $0.1 < Pe < 10$, both mechanisms are equally important and the full 2-D CDR equation must be solved.
>
> **Convection-dominated regime:** $Pe > 10$.

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Explain Michaelis–Menten kinetics for cellular oxygen consumption. Under what condition does it reduce to zero-order kinetics?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Cellular oxygen uptake is a **saturation-limited, enzyme-mediated process** (predominantly via cytochrome c oxidase in mitochondria). It is described by Michaelis–Menten kinetics:
> $$R(C) = \frac{V_{\max} \, C}{K_m + C}$$
> where $V_{\max}$ is the maximum specific oxygen consumption rate (mol O₂ per m³ scaffold per second) and $K_m$ is the Michaelis constant (mol/m³) representing the O₂ concentration at which the consumption rate is half of $V_{\max}$. The plot of $R$ versus $C$ is a rectangular hyperbola that saturates at $V_{\max}$ for large $C$ and is linear in $C$ for small $C$.
>
> **Zero-order reduction:** When the dissolved O₂ concentration is much greater than the Michaelis constant, i.e. $C \gg K_m$ (in practice $C > 10 K_m$), the denominator $K_m + C \approx C$ and the $C$ in numerator and denominator cancel:
> $$R(C) \approx V_{\max}$$
> The consumption rate becomes **constant and independent of $C$**, i.e. zero-order in O₂. This approximation is widely used in tissue engineering design calculations because the inlet O₂ concentration in air-saturated perfusate (~0.2 mmol/L) is typically 20–200 times the cellular $K_m$ (~1–10 µmol/L).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Predicted]

**A 14-mark question on axial oxygen transport in a perfusion bioreactor.**

**(a) [7 marks]** A cylindrical perfusion bioreactor of length $L = 5$ mm and cross-sectional area $A_c = 1 \text{ cm}^2$ is seeded uniformly with hepatocytes at a density such that the zero-order O₂ consumption rate is $V_{\max} = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/(m³·s). The inlet perfusate is air-saturated DMEM at 37 °C with $C_0 = 0.20$ mmol/L. The superficial velocity is $v = 100$ µm/s, and the effective O₂ diffusivity in the scaffold is $D_{\text{eff}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s. Determine whether the outlet O₂ concentration falls below the critical value $C_{\text{crit}} = 0.05$ mmol/L. Comment on the design.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the steady-state 1-D convection–diffusion–reaction equation for oxygen transport along the scaffold length under zero-order consumption, stating all assumptions and boundary conditions clearly. Show that, in the convection-dominated limit ($Pe \gg 1$), the minimum O₂ concentration occurs at the outlet and is given by $C_{\min} = C_0 - V_{\max} L / v$. Use this to obtain a formula for the **minimum superficial velocity** required to maintain cell viability.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Compute the Péclet number.**

$$Pe = \frac{v L}{D_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{(100 \times 10^{-6})(5 \times 10^{-3})}{1.5 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-7}}{1.5 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 333$$

This is **much greater than 1**, so the convection-dominated limit is valid.

**Step 2 — Apply the high-Péclet design equation.**

$$C_{\min} = C_0 - \frac{V_{\max} L}{v} = 0.20 \times 10^{-3} - \frac{(4 \times 10^{-4})(5 \times 10^{-3})}{100 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\frac{V_{\max} L}{v} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.02 \text{ mol/m}^3 = 0.02 \text{ mmol/L}$$

$$C_{\min} = 0.20 - 0.02 = 0.18 \text{ mmol/L}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the critical value.**

Since $C_{\min} = 0.18$ mmol/L $\gg C_{\text{crit}} = 0.05$ mmol/L, the **outlet O₂ is well above the critical threshold** and the construct is fully viable.

**Step 4 — Design comment.** The bioreactor is operating with a large safety margin. The flow rate could be reduced substantially (down to $v_{\min}$ below) to save medium, or the construct length could be increased. Compute the minimum velocity:

$$v_{\min} = \frac{V_{\max} L}{C_0 - C_{\text{crit}}} = \frac{(4 \times 10^{-4})(5 \times 10^{-3})}{0.20 \times 10^{-3} - 0.05 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.0133 \text{ mm/s} = 13.3 \; \mu\text{m/s}$$

So the current $v = 100$ µm/s is approximately **7.5 times larger than the minimum required**. The designer could lower the flow rate by an order of magnitude without compromising viability.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — State assumptions.**
1. Steady state: $\partial C / \partial t = 0$.
2. One-dimensional variation along $x$ (axial).
3. Constant physical properties: $v = $ const, $D_{\text{eff}} = $ const, $\varepsilon = $ const.
4. Zero-order cellular consumption: $R(C) = V_{\max}$ (justified because $C_0 \gg K_m$).
5. The fluid is incompressible, Newtonian, and the scaffold is homogeneous and isotropic.

**Step 2 — Write the CDR equation in 1-D.**

The general porous-media CDR equation reduces to:

$$v \frac{dC}{dx} = D_{\text{eff}} \frac{d^2 C}{dx^2} - V_{\max}$$

**Step 3 — State boundary conditions.**
- Inlet: $C(0) = C_0$ (Dirichlet, feed concentration).
- Outlet: $\left. \dfrac{dC}{dx} \right|_{x=L} = 0$ (Neumann, sealed or symmetric exit, no diffusive flux).

**Step 4 — Take the convection-dominated limit.**

When $Pe \gg 1$, axial diffusion is negligible compared to convection. Drop the $D_{\text{eff}} d^2C/dx^2$ term:

$$v \frac{dC}{dx} = -V_{\max}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate.**

$$\frac{dC}{dx} = -\frac{V_{\max}}{v} \;\Rightarrow\; C(x) = C_0 - \frac{V_{\max}}{v} x$$

**Step 6 — Identify $C_{\min}$.**

Since $C(x)$ is linear and monotonically decreasing, the minimum is at $x = L$:

$$C_{\min} = C(L) = C_0 - \frac{V_{\max} L}{v}$$

**Step 7 — Set $C_{\min} = C_{\text{crit}}$ to find $v_{\min}$.**

$$C_{\text{crit}} = C_0 - \frac{V_{\max} L}{v_{\min}} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{V_{\max} L}{v_{\min}} = C_0 - C_{\text{crit}}$$

$$\boxed{\;v_{\min} = \frac{V_{\max} L}{C_0 - C_{\text{crit}}}\;}$$

This is the **fundamental design equation** for the minimum perfusate flow velocity that guarantees no necrotic core in the scaffold.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Predicted] (Alternative Choice)

**A 14-mark question on diffusion-limited oxygen transport and the Krogh cylinder model.**

**(a) [7 marks]** Define the Damköhler number and Thiele modulus in the context of oxygen transport in a perfusion bioreactor. Show explicitly that the Damköhler number can be written as the product of the square of the Thiele modulus and the inverse of the Péclet number: $Da = \phi^2 / Pe$. Discuss the practical implications when $Da$ exceeds 1.

**(b) [7 marks]** Consider a tissue cylinder of outer radius $r_t$ surrounding a single capillary of radius $r_c = 10$ µm. The capillary delivers O₂ at $C_0 = 0.20$ mmol/L. Cells in the tissue consume O₂ at a zero-order rate $R_0 = 3 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/(m³·s), and the O₂ diffusivity in tissue is $D = 2 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s. Compute the minimum O₂ concentration at the outer tissue boundary when $r_t = 80$ µm. What does the result imply for the maximum thickness of a viable, avascular tissue?

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Define Damköhler number.**

The Damköhler number is the dimensionless ratio of the cellular O₂ consumption rate to the convective O₂ supply rate:

$$Da = \frac{\text{reaction rate}}{\text{convective supply rate}} = \frac{V_{\max} L}{C_0 v}$$

**Step 2 — Define Thiele modulus.**

The Thiele modulus is the dimensionless ratio of the cellular O₂ consumption rate to the diffusive O₂ supply rate:

$$\phi = L \sqrt{\frac{V_{\max}}{D_{\text{eff}} C_0}}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the relationship.**

Compute $\phi^2$:

$$\phi^2 = L^2 \cdot \frac{V_{\max}}{D_{\text{eff}} C_0} = \frac{L}{D_{\text{eff}}} \cdot \frac{V_{\max} L}{C_0}$$

Now form the ratio $\phi^2 / Pe$:

$$\frac{\phi^2}{Pe} = \frac{\phi^2}{v L / D_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{L}{D_{\text{eff}}} \cdot \frac{V_{\max} L}{C_0} \cdot \frac{D_{\text{eff}}}{v L} = \frac{V_{\max} L}{C_0 v} = Da$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;Da = \frac{\phi^2}{Pe}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Implications when $Da > 1$.**

When $Da > 1$, the cells are consuming O₂ faster than the perfusate can deliver it. The outlet concentration drops below $C_{\text{crit}}$ and a **necrotic core** forms in the scaffold. The bioreactor must be redesigned by:
- increasing the flow rate $v$ (reduces $Da$),
- decreasing the scaffold length $L$ (reduces $Da$),
- decreasing the cell density (reduces $V_{\max}$ and thus $Da$), or
- increasing the inlet O₂ tension (e.g., using pure O₂ instead of air, raising $C_0$).

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Apply the Krogh-cylinder minimum formula derived in §3.2.**

$$C_{\min} = C_0 - \frac{R_0}{4D}\left[r_t^2 - r_c^2 + 2 r_t^2 \ln\!\left(\frac{r_t}{r_c}\right)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

- $C_0 = 0.20 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/m³ = 0.20 mmol/L
- $R_0 = 3 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/(m³·s)
- $D = 2 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s
- $r_c = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ m = $10^{-5}$ m
- $r_t = 80 \times 10^{-6}$ m = $8 \times 10^{-5}$ m

Compute the geometric factor first:

$$r_t^2 - r_c^2 = (8 \times 10^{-5})^2 - (10^{-5})^2 = 6.4 \times 10^{-9} - 1.0 \times 10^{-10} = 6.3 \times 10^{-9} \;\text{m}^2$$

$$2 r_t^2 \ln(r_t/r_c) = 2 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-9} \times \ln(8) = 1.28 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.079 = 2.66 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{m}^2$$

Sum: $6.3 \times 10^{-9} + 2.66 \times 10^{-8} = 3.29 \times 10^{-8}$ m².

**Step 3 — Compute the depletion term.**

$$\frac{R_0}{4D} \times 3.29 \times 10^{-8} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-4}}{8 \times 10^{-9}} \times 3.29 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$= 3.75 \times 10^{4} \times 3.29 \times 10^{-8} = 1.234 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{mol/m}^3 = 0.1234 \;\text{mmol/L}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_{\min}$.**

$$C_{\min} = 0.20 - 0.1234 = 0.0766 \;\text{mmol/L}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

$C_{\min} = 0.077$ mmol/L is **just above** the typical critical value of 0.05 mmol/L, so the tissue is marginally viable at $r_t = 80$ µm. The maximum viable tissue radius is therefore on the order of ~80–100 µm, which corresponds to a **maximum avascular tissue thickness of about 160–200 µm**. Beyond this thickness, a necrotic core will form and the tissue will require vascularization (or an artificial perfusion channel) to remain viable.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in this question type — read carefully before writing your exam:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units.** O₂ concentrations are often given in mmol/L but the formula for $V_{\max} L / v$ requires mol/m³. A factor of 1000 error is the **#1 cause of numerical mistakes** in this topic. Always check: $1$ mmol/L $= 1$ mol/m³.
>
> 2. **Mixing Péclet and Damköhler definitions.** Péclet is **convection ÷ diffusion**; Damköhler is **reaction ÷ convection**. Examiners specifically test whether you remember which numerator goes with which denominator.
>
> 3. **Applying the wrong BC at the outlet.** A common error is to use $C(L) = 0$ (the "well-mixed sink" assumption) instead of $dC/dx(L) = 0$ (the "sealed symmetric exit" assumption). The latter is correct for a typical perfusion scaffold terminating in a symmetric manifold.
>
> 4. **Dropping the diffusive term too early.** The $D_{\text{eff}} d^2 C / dx^2$ term can only be dropped when $Pe \gg 1$ (i.e., $Pe > 10$). For low-velocity perfusion, you must solve the full second-order ODE and you cannot use the linear profile.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to verify the zero-order assumption.** Zero-order consumption $R = V_{\max}$ is only valid when $C \gg K_m$. At the outlet where $C$ may be near $C_{\text{crit}}$, the actual consumption may be lower (first-order regime), so the **true outlet concentration is slightly higher** than predicted. This is a "favourable" error (your design is conservative) but mentioning it shows deep understanding and earns full marks.
>
> 6. **Not stating boundary conditions explicitly.** A derivation without clearly written BCs is considered incomplete by KTU examiners. Always write both BCs and label them (e.g., "Dirichlet at inlet," "Neumann at outlet").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for Oxygen Transport in Perfusion Bioreactors (Module 3):**
>
> - **Master equation:** $\varepsilon \partial_t C + \nabla \cdot (\mathbf{v} C) = \nabla \cdot (D_{\text{eff}} \nabla C) - R(C)$
> - **Effective diffusivity:** $D_{\text{eff}} = D_0 \varepsilon^{1.5}$ (Bruggeman)
> - **Michaelis–Menten consumption:** $R(C) = V_{\max} C / (K_m + C)$; reduces to **zero-order** when $C \gg K_m$
> - **Péclet number:** $Pe = v L / D_{\text{eff}}$ — high $Pe$ means convection-dominated axial flow
> - **Damköhler number:** $Da = V_{\max} L / (C_0 v)$ — must be $< Da_{\max} = 1 - C_{\text{crit}}/C_0$ for viability
> - **Thiele modulus:** $\phi = L \sqrt{V_{\max}/(D_{\text{eff}} C_0)}$ — high $\phi$ means reaction-dominated (diffusion-limited)
> - **Identity to remember:** $Da = \phi^2 / Pe$
> - **Henry's law:** $C = k_H p_{O_2}$ with $k_H \approx 1.3 \times 10^{-6}$ mol/(L·Pa) at 37 °C
> - **Boundary conditions for axial model:** $C(0) = C_0$ (inlet, Dirichlet) and $dC/dx(L) = 0$ (outlet, Neumann)
> - **High-Péclet oxygen profile:** $C(x) = C_0 - (V_{\max}/v) x$ (linear)
> - **Minimum flow velocity:** $v_{\min} = V_{\max} L / (C_0 - C_{\text{crit}})$
> - **Krogh cylinder radial profile:** $C(r) = C_0 + (R_0/4D)(r^2 - r_c^2) + (R_0 r_t^2 / 2D) \ln(r_c / r)$
> - **Krogh minimum (at $r = r_t$):** $C_{\min} = C_0 - (R_0/4D)[r_t^2 - r_c^2 + 2 r_t^2 \ln(r_t/r_c)]$
> - **Maximum avascular tissue thickness:** ~200 µm (diffusion-limited, classical Krogh result)
> - **Engineering levers to avoid hypoxia:** increase $v$, decrease $L$, decrease cell density (lower $V_{\max}$), increase $C_0$ (oxygenate with pure O₂), or increase $\varepsilon$ (improve $D_{\text{eff}}$)
> - **Three transport mechanisms:** convection (bulk flow), diffusion (gradient-driven), reaction (cellular consumption) — all three appear in the CDR equation
> - **Resistance network analogy:** total mass-transfer resistance is the sum of film, interparticle, intraparticle, and reaction resistances; the **largest** one is rate-limiting

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oxygen Transport in the Krogh Tissue Cylinder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT832 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Oxygen Transport in the Krogh Tissue Cylinder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Krogh Tissue Cylinder Model** is a classical, one-dimensional steady-state mathematical model of oxygen delivery from a single blood capillary to the surrounding biological tissue. Proposed by the Danish physiologist **Schack August Steenberg Krogh (1919)** — winner of the **Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine (1920)** — the model idealizes the capillary-tissue geometry as a concentric cylinder system: an inner cylinder of radius $r_c$ (the capillary, supplied with oxygenated blood) surrounded by an outer concentric cylinder of radius $r_t$ (the tissue cylinder) that consumes oxygen uniformly at a volumetric rate $\dot{q}$ (mL O₂ · mL tissue⁻¹ · s⁻¹). Oxygen is transported purely by **molecular diffusion** in the radial direction, governed by **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion** combined with a uniform consumption (sink) term.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The Krogh cylinder is the foundational transport model used to compute tissue oxygen tension profiles, evaluate oxygen delivery to organs (brain, muscle, tumor), and analyze pathological conditions such as **hypoxia** and **ischemia**. Mastery of the model derivation is a high-weightage 14-mark topic in KTU ESE.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **cylindrical water-soaked sponge (the tissue)** with a **thin garden hose running through its center (the capillary)**. Water (oxygen) continuously seeps out radially from the hose into the sponge. The sponge cells drink the water at a constant rate (oxygen consumption). If you sliced the sponge cross-sectionally, you would see a **"drying gradient"** — wet near the hose, dry at the outer edge. The Krogh model mathematically describes this exact "wetting front" as a **radial oxygen partial pressure profile**, $P(r)$, that falls from a maximum at the capillary wall to a minimum (possibly zero — the **anoxic point**) at the outer tissue boundary.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Key Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oxygen diffusion coefficient in tissue | $D$ | $\mathbf{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}$ | cm²/s |
| Solubility of O₂ in tissue | $\alpha$ | $\mathbf{3.1 \times 10^{-5}}$ | mL O₂ · (mL tissue · mmHg)⁻¹ |
| Capillary radius | $r_c$ | $2.5 - 5$ | μm |
| Krogh (tissue) radius | $r_t$ | $20 - 100$ | μm |
| Arterial O₂ partial pressure | $P_{O_2, art}$ | $\mathbf{95}$ | mmHg |
| Venous O₂ partial pressure | $P_{O_2, ven}$ | $40$ | mmHg |
| Uniform O₂ consumption rate | $\dot{q}$ | $1 - 10 \times 10^{-4}$ | mL O₂ · (mL tissue · s)⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> The product $\alpha D$ is called the **Krogh diffusion constant** $K$, and it characterizes the effective oxygen transport capacity of a specific tissue.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric and Physical Assumptions of the Krogh Model

The derivation rests on **eight explicit assumptions** — these are mandatory in KTU answers and frequently tested as 3-mark questions:

1. **Steady-state** oxygen distribution: $\partial C / \partial t = 0$.
2. **Single isolated capillary** surrounded by a uniform cylindrical tissue region.
3. **Symmetry in angular and axial directions**: concentration depends only on the radial coordinate $r$ (i.e., $C = C(r)$).
4. **Oxygen is consumed uniformly** throughout the tissue at a constant rate $\dot{q}$ (zero-order kinetics).
5. **No oxygen consumption inside the capillary lumen** — the capillary is only a source/sink boundary.
6. **Constant physical properties**: $D$ and $\alpha$ are independent of position and concentration.
7. **No axial diffusion** in the tissue (axial transport is dominated by convection in the blood).
8. **The capillary wall offers no resistance** to oxygen diffusion.

### 2.2 Derivation Logic: The Diffusion-Consumption Equation

Starting from Fick's second law for a non-stationary, consuming medium in cylindrical coordinates:

$$J_r = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial r}$$

A mass balance on a cylindrical shell of thickness $dr$ and unit length gives the **steady-state diffusion-consumption equation**:

$$D \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left( r \frac{\partial C}{\partial r} \right) \right] - \dot{q} = 0$$

### 2.3 Boundary Conditions

- **At the capillary wall** ($r = r_c$): The tissue oxygen partial pressure equals the capillary wall partial pressure, $P = P_c$.
- **At the Krogh tissue radius** ($r = r_t$): No-flux symmetry boundary, $\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial r} = 0$. This reflects the assumption that each capillary supplies its own cylinder, and adjacent cylinders meet with no net flux.

### 2.4 Final Krogh-Erlang Solution

The classical solution — known as the **Krogh-Erlang equation** — for the radial oxygen tension profile is:

$$P(r) = P_c - \frac{\dot{q}}{4 \alpha D} \left( r_t^2 \ln \frac{r}{r_c} - \frac{r^2 - r_c^2}{2} \right)$$

Or equivalently, expressing in terms of concentration:

$$C(r) = C_c - \frac{\dot{q}}{4 D} \left( r_t^2 \ln \frac{r}{r_c} - \frac{r^2 - r_c^2}{2} \right)$$

### 2.5 Critical Radius (Anoxic Condition)

The **critical Krogh radius** $r_{t,crit}$ is the value at which the oxygen tension just drops to zero at the venous end of the capillary, defining the **maximum tissue volume that a single capillary can oxygenate**. Setting $P(r_t) = 0$ and solving for $r_t$:

$$r_{t,crit}^2 \, \ln \left( \frac{r_{t,crit}}{r_c} \right) = \frac{r_c^2}{2} + \frac{2 \alpha D P_c}{\dot{q}}$$

> [!TIP]
> If the actual $r_t > r_{t,crit}$, an anoxic core forms and tissue necrosis ensues — the physiological basis of **tumor hypoxia** and **stroke damage**.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D \nabla^2 C - \dot{q} = 0$ | Steady-state diffusion + consumption | Derivation starting point |
| 2 | $P(r) = P_c - \dfrac{\dot{q}}{4\alpha D}\left(r_t^2 \ln \dfrac{r}{r_c} - \dfrac{r^2 - r_c^2}{2}\right)$ | Radial O₂ tension profile | Numerical plotting, 14-mark problem |
| 3 | $r_{t,crit}^2 \ln\left(\dfrac{r_{t,crit}}{r_c}\right) = \dfrac{r_c^2}{2} + \dfrac{2 \alpha D P_c}{\dot{q}}$ | Critical tissue radius | Find $r_{t,crit}$ or $\dot{q}$ |
| 4 | $J_r(r_c) = \dfrac{\dot{q}}{2} \left( \dfrac{r_t^2}{r_c} - r_c \right)$ | O₂ flux at capillary wall | Calculating O₂ delivery rate |
| 5 | $\dot{V}_{O_2} = \pi L \dot{q} (r_t^2 - r_c^2)$ | Total O₂ consumption per unit length | Cardiac output, tissue demand |
| 6 | $K = \alpha D$ | Krogh diffusion constant | Tissue-specific comparison |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid common markdown-pipe failures:** When writing expressions like $\vert P_c \vert$ in tables, always use the LaTeX vertical bar commands `\vert` or `\mid`, e.g., $\vert P_c \vert$, never the bare ASCII pipe character `\vert`. This prevents table-rendering corruption in KTU PDF/HTML exports.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cancer radiotherapy planning**: Tumor hypoxia reduces radiosensitivity. The Krogh model predicts hypoxic zones to optimize dose fractionation.
- **Tissue engineering scaffolds**: Designing vascularized bioreactors for organoids requires Krogh-radius analysis to prevent necrotic cores.
- **Cardiac and cerebral ischemia diagnosis**: $r_{t,crit}$ values are used to estimate the penumbra (viable but hypoxic tissue) in stroke imaging.
- **Artificial oxygen carriers (e.g., hemoglobin-based):** Used as a benchmark to evaluate synthetic blood substitutes against physiological performance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Radial Oxygen Profile

**Step 1 — Governing differential equation in cylindrical coordinates.**

For an isotropic, homogeneous medium with a uniform volumetric sink $\dot{q}$, Fick's second law in cylindrical coordinates (with no $\theta$ or $z$ dependence) is:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left( r \frac{\partial C}{\partial r} \right) \right] - \dot{q}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-state assumption** $\partial C / \partial t = 0$:

$$D \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left( r \frac{\partial C}{\partial r} \right) \right] - \dot{q} = 0$$

Dividing throughout by $D$:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dC}{dr} \right) = \frac{\dot{q}}{D}$$

**Step 3 — First integration (multiply both sides by $r$ and integrate with respect to $r$):**

$$\frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dC}{dr} \right) = \frac{\dot{q}}{D} \, r$$

Integrating:

$$r \frac{dC}{dr} = \frac{\dot{q}}{2 D} r^2 + A$$

where $A$ is the constant of integration. Therefore:

$$\frac{dC}{dr} = \frac{\dot{q}}{2 D} r + \frac{A}{r}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the second boundary condition at $r = r_t$** (no-flux symmetry):

$$\left. \frac{dC}{dr} \right|_{r = r_t} = 0$$

Substituting:

$$0 = \frac{\dot{q}}{2 D} r_t + \frac{A}{r_t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = -\frac{\dot{q}}{2 D} r_t^2$$

So the radial flux gradient becomes:

$$\frac{dC}{dr} = \frac{\dot{q}}{2 D} \left( r - \frac{r_t^2}{r} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Second integration (integrate $dC/dr$ with respect to $r$):**

$$C(r) = \frac{\dot{q}}{2 D} \left( \frac{r^2}{2} - r_t^2 \ln r \right) + B$$

where $B$ is the second integration constant.

**Step 6 — Apply the first boundary condition at $r = r_c$** where $C = C_c$ (or in pressure form, $P = P_c$):

$$C_c = \frac{\dot{q}}{2 D} \left( \frac{r_c^2}{2} - r_t^2 \ln r_c \right) + B$$

Solving for $B$:

$$B = C_c - \frac{\dot{q}}{2 D} \left( \frac{r_c^2}{2} - r_t^2 \ln r_c \right)$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $B$ back into the general solution** and simplify:

$$C(r) = C_c + \frac{\dot{q}}{2 D} \left( \frac{r^2 - r_c^2}{2} - r_t^2 \ln \frac{r}{r_c} \right)$$

Rearranging into the canonical Krogh form:

$$C(r) = C_c - \frac{\dot{q}}{4 D} \left( r_t^2 \ln \frac{r}{r_c} - \frac{r^2 - r_c^2}{2} \right)$$

**Step 8 — Convert concentration to partial pressure** using Henry's law: $C = \alpha P$, which gives $P = C / \alpha$. Dividing the entire equation by $\alpha$:

$$P(r) = P_c - \frac{\dot{q}}{4 \alpha D} \left( r_t^2 \ln \frac{r}{r_c} - \frac{r^2 - r_c^2}{2} \right)$$

This is the **complete Krogh-Erlang radial oxygen profile**, valid in the tissue annulus $r_c \leq r \leq r_t$.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Boundary Condition Application

| Boundary | Location | Mathematical Statement | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r = r_c$ | $C(r_c) = C_c$ | Capillary wall sets tissue concentration |
| 2 | $r = r_t$ | $\dfrac{dC}{dr} \vert_{r_t} = 0$ | No-flux symmetry at tissue-cylinder edge |
| 3 | $r \to 0$ | Finite $C$ (capillary interior) | No singularity inside the blood vessel |
| 4 | $r = r_{t,crit}$ | $C(r_t) = 0$ | Anoxic core formation condition |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Sub-Part)

> **Problem:** Oxygen diffuses from a capillary of radius $r_c = 5 \,\mu\text{m}$ into a surrounding tissue cylinder of outer radius $r_t = 50 \,\mu\text{m}$. The oxygen consumption rate is $\dot{q} = 3.0 \times 10^{-4}$ mL O₂ · (mL tissue · s)⁻¹, the tissue oxygen solubility is $\alpha = 3.0 \times 10^{-5}$ mL O₂ · (mL · mmHg)⁻¹, and the diffusion coefficient is $D = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ cm²/s. If the capillary wall O₂ partial pressure is $P_c = 90$ mmHg, compute:
> (a) The oxygen partial pressure at $r = 25 \,\mu\text{m}$.
> (b) Whether an anoxic core forms at $r = r_t$.

**Solution (a):**

First, compute the lumped coefficient:

$$\frac{\dot{q}}{4 \alpha D} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times (3.0 \times 10^{-5}) \times (1.5 \times 10^{-5})}$$

$$= \frac{3.0 \times 10^{-4}}{1.8 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.667 \times 10^{5} \; \frac{\text{mmHg}}{\text{cm}^2}$$

Convert all radii to cm: $r_c = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ cm, $r_t = 50 \times 10^{-4}$ cm, $r = 25 \times 10^{-4}$ cm.

Compute the bracketed term:

$$r_t^2 \ln \frac{r}{r_c} - \frac{r^2 - r_c^2}{2}$$

$$= (50 \times 10^{-4})^2 \ln \left( \frac{25 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} \right) - \frac{(25 \times 10^{-4})^2 - (5 \times 10^{-4})^2}{2}$$

$$= 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \ln(5) - \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$= (2.5 \times 10^{-4})(1.6094) - 3.0 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$= 4.024 \times 10^{-4} - 3.0 \times 10^{-6} = 3.994 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{cm}^2$$

Therefore:

$$P(25 \,\mu\text{m}) = 90 - (1.667 \times 10^{5})(3.994 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$= 90 - 66.57 = 23.4 \; \text{mmHg}$$

**Solution (b):**

Evaluate $P$ at $r = r_t = 50 \,\mu\text{m}$. The bracketed term becomes:

$$r_t^2 \ln \frac{r_t}{r_c} - \frac{r_t^2 - r_c^2}{2}$$

$$= r_t^2 \left( \ln \frac{r_t}{r_c} - \frac{1}{2} \right) + \frac{r_c^2}{2}$$

Numerically: $r_t^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ cm², $\ln(10) = 2.3026$, so:

$$= 2.5 \times 10^{-4} (2.3026 - 0.5) + 1.25 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$= 2.5 \times 10^{-4} (1.8026) + 1.25 \times 10^{-7} = 4.508 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{cm}^2$$

Then:

$$P(r_t) = 90 - (1.667 \times 10^{5})(4.508 \times 10^{-4}) = 90 - 75.14 = 14.86 \; \text{mmHg}$$

Since $P(r_t) > 0$, **no anoxic core forms** at the outer tissue boundary.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following diagram maps the **information flow of the Krogh model**, from physiological inputs to engineering outputs. It uses purely alphanumeric node identifiers and clean uppercase labels (no inline formatting) to ensure clean Mermaid compilation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["INPUT CAPILLARY GEOMETRY"] --> node2["DEFINE KROGH CYLINDER DOMAIN"]
    node2 --> node3["CAPILLARY RADIUS RC"]
    node2 --> node4["TISSUE RADIUS RT"]
    node2 --> node5["LENGTH L"]
    node6["TISSUE PROPERTIES"] --> node7["DIFFUSION COEFFICIENT D"]
    node6 --> node8["SOLUBILITY ALPHA"]
    node6 --> node9["CONSUMPTION RATE Q"]
    node3 --> node10["ASSEMBLE GOVERNING PDE"]
    node4 --> node10
    node5 --> node10
    node7 --> node10
    node8 --> node10
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11["STEADY STATE DIFFUSION CONSUMPTION EQUATION"]
    node11 --> node12["APPLY BOUNDARY CONDITION AT RC PC KNOWN"]
    node11 --> node13["APPLY NO FLUX AT RT DPR DR ZERO"]
    node12 --> node14["INTEGRATE TWICE TO OBTAIN C R"]
    node13 --> node14
    node14 --> node15["KROGH ERLANG RADIAL PROFILE"]
    node15 --> node16["OUTPUT PO2 VS R PROFILE"]
    node15 --> node17["OUTPUT CRITICAL RADIUS RT CRIT"]
    node15 --> node18["OUTPUT O2 FLUX AT RC"]
    node16 --> node19["CLINICAL DECISION SUPPORT"]
    node17 --> node19
    node18 --> node19
    node19 --> node20["HYPOXIA OR ANOXIA ASSESSMENT"]
    node20 --> node21["RADIOTHERAPY PLANNING"]
    node20 --> node22["TISSUE ENGINEERING DESIGN"]
    node20 --> node23["STROKE PENUMBRA MAPPING"]
```

### 4.2 Description of the Architecture Flow

| Stage | Module | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Geometry block | Defines $r_c$, $r_t$, $L$ from histology or imaging |
| Property block | Tissue parameters | Supplies $D$, $\alpha$, $\dot{q}$ from physiological tables |
| Mathematical engine | PDE assembly | Constructs the steady-state diffusion-consumption equation |
| Solver | Integration chain | Two-fold integration with two BCs yields the analytical solution |
| Output | Profile generator | Produces $P(r)$, $r_{t,crit}$, and the wall flux $J_r$ |
| Application layer | Decision support | Translates transport outputs to clinical/engineering action |

> [!NOTE]
> For a physical cross-sectional depiction of the Krogh cylinder (concentric rings with oxygen tension contours), a hand-drawn sketch in the exam booklet — capillary at center, isobaric $P$ curves forming concentric rings falling outward — is the KTU-expected diagram. The Mermaid block above is the schematic companion for logical understanding.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the assumptions made in deriving the Krogh tissue cylinder model for oxygen transport.**

**Model Answer:**

1. Steady-state oxygen distribution ($\partial C/\partial t = 0$).
2. Single capillary surrounded by a uniform cylindrical tissue region.
3. Axial and angular symmetry; concentration varies only with radial position $r$.
4. Uniform volumetric oxygen consumption rate $\dot{q}$ in the tissue.
5. No oxygen consumption inside the capillary lumen.
6. Constant $D$ and $\alpha$, independent of position and concentration.
7. No axial diffusion in tissue; oxygen transport is purely radial.
8. Capillary wall offers no resistance to oxygen diffusion.

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Krogh diffusion constant? How is it used to compare tissues?**

**Model Answer:**

The Krogh diffusion constant is the product $K = \alpha D$, where $\alpha$ is the solubility of oxygen in tissue and $D$ is the diffusion coefficient of oxygen in the tissue.

$$K = \alpha D \;\; [\text{cm}^2 \cdot \text{mmHg} / \text{s}]$$

It quantifies the ability of a tissue to deliver oxygen by diffusion per unit partial pressure gradient. A higher $K$ value indicates a more efficient oxygen-transporting tissue. Typical values: skeletal muscle $\approx 5 \times 10^{-10}$ cm² mmHg / s, brain tissue $\approx 4 \times 10^{-10}$ cm² mmHg / s.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Starting from Fick's second law of diffusion in cylindrical coordinates, derive the steady-state equation for oxygen transport in tissue surrounding a capillary, including the uniform consumption term. State all boundary conditions clearly. **7 Marks**

**(b)** Solve the governing differential equation to obtain the radial oxygen partial pressure profile $P(r)$ in the Krogh cylinder, and derive the expression for the **critical tissue radius** $r_{t,crit}$ at which an anoxic core first appears. **7 Marks**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Governing equation derivation:**

Fick's second law in cylindrical coordinates:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \frac{\partial C}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2} \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial \theta^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2} \right] - \dot{q}$$

By symmetry (no $\theta$, $z$ dependence) and steady-state ($\partial C/\partial t = 0$):

$$D \cdot \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dC}{dr} \right) = \dot{q}$$

**Boundary conditions:**

- At $r = r_c$: $C = C_c$ (or $P = P_c$).
- At $r = r_t$: $\dfrac{dC}{dr} = 0$ (no-flux symmetry).

**(b) Solution for $P(r)$ and $r_{t,crit}$:**

First integration (multiply by $r$, integrate):

$$r \frac{dC}{dr} = \frac{\dot{q}}{2D} r^2 + A$$

Applying $\dfrac{dC}{dr} \vert_{r_t} = 0$ gives $A = -\dfrac{\dot{q}}{2D} r_t^2$. Second integration:

$$C(r) = \frac{\dot{q}}{2D}\left(\frac{r^2}{2} - r_t^2 \ln r\right) + B$$

Applying $C(r_c) = C_c$ gives $B = C_c - \dfrac{\dot{q}}{2D}\left(\dfrac{r_c^2}{2} - r_t^2 \ln r_c\right)$. Therefore:

$$C(r) = C_c + \frac{\dot{q}}{2D}\left(\frac{r^2 - r_c^2}{2} - r_t^2 \ln \frac{r}{r_c}\right)$$

Converting $C = \alpha P$:

$$P(r) = P_c - \frac{\dot{q}}{4\alpha D}\left(r_t^2 \ln \frac{r}{r_c} - \frac{r^2 - r_c^2}{2}\right)$$

**Critical radius:** Setting $P(r_t) = 0$:

$$\frac{\dot{q}}{4\alpha D}\left(r_{t,crit}^2 \ln \frac{r_{t,crit}}{r_c} - \frac{r_{t,crit}^2 - r_c^2}{2}\right) = P_c$$

Rearranging:

$$r_{t,crit}^2 \ln \left( \frac{r_{t,crit}}{r_c} \right) = \frac{r_c^2}{2} + \frac{2 \alpha D P_c}{\dot{q}}$$

---

#### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** For the Krogh tissue cylinder model, derive the expression for the **oxygen flux at the capillary wall** $J_r(r_c)$ and the **total oxygen consumption rate per unit capillary length** $\dot{V}_{O_2}/L$. **7 Marks**

**(b)** In a tumor, the capillary radius is $r_c = 4 \,\mu\text{m}$, the Krogh tissue radius is $r_t = 60 \,\mu\text{m}$, $D = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ cm²/s, $\alpha = 3.5 \times 10^{-5}$ mL O₂/(mL·mmHg), and the tissue oxygen consumption is $\dot{q} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ mL O₂/(mL·s). If the arterial $P_{O_2} = 80$ mmHg at the inlet and $P_{O_2} = 30$ mmHg at the venous end, determine the **maximum tissue cylinder length** over which the tissue remains fully oxygenated (i.e., no anoxic core forms at the venous end). **7 Marks**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Wall flux and total consumption:**

From the radial gradient obtained in the derivation:

$$\frac{dC}{dr} = \frac{\dot{q}}{2D}\left(r - \frac{r_t^2}{r}\right)$$

Therefore, the oxygen flux at $r = r_c$:

$$J_r(r_c) = -D \frac{dC}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r_c} = -\frac{\dot{q}}{2}\left(r_c - \frac{r_t^2}{r_c}\right) = \frac{\dot{q}}{2}\left(\frac{r_t^2}{r_c} - r_c\right)$$

The total oxygen consumption per unit capillary length (integrated over the tissue annulus, since $\dot{q}$ is volumetric):

$$\frac{\dot{V}_{O_2}}{L} = \int_{r_c}^{r_t} \dot{q} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \pi \dot{q} (r_t^2 - r_c^2)$$

**(b) Critical tissue cylinder length:**

For tumor oxygenation, the relevant condition is at the **venous end** where $P_c = 30$ mmHg (lowest). Substituting into the critical radius equation:

$$r_t^2 \ln \frac{r_t}{r_c} = \frac{r_c^2}{2} + \frac{2 \alpha D P_c}{\dot{q}}$$

Compute the right-hand side term-by-term:

$$r_c^2 / 2 = (4 \times 10^{-4})^2 / 2 = 8.0 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{cm}^2$$

$$\frac{2 \alpha D P_c}{\dot{q}} = \frac{2 \times (3.5 \times 10^{-5}) \times (2.0 \times 10^{-5}) \times 30}{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$= \frac{4.2 \times 10^{-8}}{5.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 8.4 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{cm}^2$$

So the RHS $\approx 8.4 \times 10^{-5}$ cm². The LHS with $r_t = 60 \,\mu\text{m}$:

$$r_t^2 \ln \frac{r_t}{r_c} = (60 \times 10^{-4})^2 \ln \frac{60}{4} = 3.6 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \ln(15) = 3.6 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.708 = 9.75 \times 10^{-4}$$

Since $9.75 \times 10^{-4} > 8.4 \times 10^{-5}$, the actual $r_t$ **already exceeds** $r_{t,crit}$ at the venous end. The tissue will become anoxic for any non-zero $L$, indicating the need for **angiogenic intervention** or a reduction in inter-capillary distance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losses:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the no-flux BC at $r_t$** during the first integration — yields the wrong integration constant $A$ and a non-physical divergent solution. **(-3 marks typical)**
> 2. **Using $\dot{q}$ and $D$ in inconsistent units** (e.g., mixing cm and μm without conversion). **(-2 marks)**
> 3. **Confusing oxygen flux $J_r$ with concentration gradient** — remember $J = -D \nabla C$, not the gradient itself. **(-2 marks)**
> 4. **Omitting the $\alpha$ conversion** when writing the pressure form $P(r) = C(r)/\alpha$. **(-1 mark)**
> 5. **Not specifying the validity domain** of the solution ($r_c \le r \le r_t$). **(-1 mark)**

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Model purpose:** Predicts radial oxygen tension $P(r)$ in tissue around a single capillary.
- **Governing equation:** $D \cdot \dfrac{1}{r} \dfrac{d}{dr}\left(r \dfrac{dC}{dr}\right) - \dot{q} = 0$ (steady-state, uniform consumption).
- **Final profile (Krogh-Erlang):** $P(r) = P_c - \dfrac{\dot{q}}{4\alpha D}\left(r_t^2 \ln \dfrac{r}{r_c} - \dfrac{r^2 - r_c^2}{2}\right)$.
- **Boundary conditions:** $C(r_c) = C_c$ and $\left. \dfrac{dC}{dr} \right|_{r_t} = 0$.
- **Critical radius:** $r_{t,crit}^2 \ln\left(\dfrac{r_{t,crit}}{r_c}\right) = \dfrac{r_c^2}{2} + \dfrac{2\alpha D P_c}{\dot{q}}$.
- **Wall oxygen flux:** $J_r(r_c) = \dfrac{\dot{q}}{2}\left(\dfrac{r_t^2}{r_c} - r_c\right)$.
- **Volumetric consumption rate per length:** $\dot{V}_{O_2}/L = \pi \dot{q}(r_t^2 - r_c^2)$.
- **Krogh diffusion constant:** $K = \alpha D$ — a tissue-specific property.
- **Typical values:** $D \sim 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ cm²/s, $\alpha \sim 3.0 \times 10^{-5}$ mL O₂/(mL·mmHg), $r_c \sim 5 \,\mu\text{m}$, $r_t \sim 50 \,\mu\text{m}$.
- **Anoxic core condition:** If actual $r_t > r_{t,crit}$, tissue necrosis/hypoxia occurs — central to tumor biology.
- **Assumption checklist (for exam answers):** steady-state, cylindrical symmetry, uniform $\dot{q}$, constant $D$ and $\alpha$, negligible capillary wall resistance.
- **Clinical relevance:** Tumor radiotherapy planning, stroke penumbra imaging, tissue-engineered scaffold design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transport in Kidneys: Review of Physiological Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT832 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transport in Kidneys: Review of Physiological Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Renal Transport Phenomena** refers to the integrated mass, momentum, and solute transport processes that occur within the nephron — the functional unit of the kidney — to regulate the composition and volume of body fluids. The physiological process of urine formation is governed by three coupled transport operations:

1. **Glomerular Ultrafiltration** – pressure-driven bulk flow of plasma across the glomerular membrane.
2. **Tubular Reabsorption** – selective transport of water and solutes from the tubular lumen back into peritubular capillaries.
3. **Tubular Secretion** – transport of solutes from peritubular capillaries into the tubular lumen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The kidney is the most quantitatively important mass-transfer organ in the body — processing approximately **180 L/day** of filtrate, of which ~**99%** is reabsorbed, leaving only ~**1.5–2 L/day** as final urine.

Mathematically, the overall mass balance for any solute *i* in the kidney is expressed as:

$$E_i = F_i - R_i + S_i$$

where $E_i$ is the urinary excretion rate, $F_i$ is the filtration rate, $R_i$ is the reabsorption rate, and $S_i$ is the secretion rate (all in mg/min or mmol/min).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Treatment Plant" Model

Imagine the kidney as a **sophisticated water-recycling plant** with a million parallel filter units (nephrons). Each filter works in three sequential stages:

- **Stage 1 — Ultrafiltration Plant (Glomerulus):** Raw water (blood plasma) is forced under high pressure through a fine mesh. Almost everything passes through, except large debris (blood cells and large proteins).
- **Stage 2 — Recovery Line (Tubular Reabsorption):** The filtrate flows through a long, winding recovery channel. Useful materials (glucose, amino acids, Na⁺, water) are selectively pumped *back* into the main supply line, while waste products are left behind.
- **Stage 3 — Polishing Section (Tubular Secretion):** A second crew actively dumps additional waste products (drugs, H⁺, K⁺, ammonia) from the side stream *into* the filtrate to ensure thorough cleaning.
- **Final Output:** The concentrated waste stream is the **urine**.

This analogy captures the **selective, active, and passive** nature of renal transport and clarifies why a seemingly "wasteful" 180 L/day filtration is in fact an evolutionary masterpiece of **high-throughput, high-selectivity** mass transfer.

---

## 1.3 The Nephron — Functional Unit of the Kidney

Each human kidney contains approximately **1 million nephrons**. A nephron is anatomically divided into:

| Segment | Primary Function | Transport Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Glomerulus** (Bowman's capsule) | Bulk ultrafiltration of plasma | Pressure-driven convection |
| **Proximal Convoluted Tubule (PCT)** | Bulk reabsorption of Na⁺, glucose, amino acids, HCO₃⁻, water (~65%) | Active (Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase) + passive osmosis |
| **Loop of Henle** | Establishes medullary osmotic gradient | Countercurrent multiplication |
| **Distal Convoluted Tubule (DCT)** | Fine-tuning of Na⁺, Ca²⁺, regulated by aldosterone | Active transport |
| **Collecting Duct** | Final water reabsorption (ADH) and concentration | Passive (osmosis) + active (urea) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant to Remember:** Normal **GFR ≈ 125 mL/min** (≈ 180 L/day). This figure appears in nearly every KTU numerical on renal transport.

---

## 1.4 Renal Blood Flow (RBF) and Plasma Flow (RPF)

The kidneys receive ~**20–25% of cardiac output** despite being only ~0.5% of body mass. This enormous perfusion is essential because filtration is the first step.

$$RBF = \frac{RPF}{1 - Hct}$$

where **Hct** is the hematocrit (~0.45). Typical value: $RPF \approx 650$ mL/min, $RBF \approx 1200$ mL/min.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Glomerular Ultrafiltration — The Starling Equation for the Kidney

The glomerular filtration rate (GFR) is the cornerstone parameter. It is governed by the **net filtration pressure** acting across the glomerular capillary wall, in direct analogy to Starling's law for capillary exchange.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Hydrostatic pressure in glomerular capillary ($P_{GC}$):** ~**45 mmHg** (high, due to afferent/efferent arteriolar resistance).
2. **Hydrostatic pressure in Bowman's space ($P_{BS}$):** ~**15 mmHg** (opposes filtration).
3. **Oncotic pressure of plasma proteins in glomerular capillary ($\pi_{GC}$):** ~**25–30 mmHg** (opposes filtration; rises along the capillary as fluid leaves).
4. **Oncotic pressure in Bowman's space ($\pi_{BS}$):** ~**0 mmHg** (proteins are too large to filter; usually neglected).

The net filtration pressure is:

$$\text{NFP} = (P_{GC} - P_{BS}) - (\pi_{GC} - \pi_{BS})$$

GFR is then:

$$\boxed{GFR = K_f \cdot \text{NFP} = K_f \cdot \big[(P_{GC} - P_{BS}) - \pi_{GC}\big]}$$

where $K_f$ is the **ultrafiltration coefficient** (product of hydraulic conductivity and surface area), typically ~**12.5 mL/min/mmHg**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** $K_f$ is conceptually identical to the permeability × area (PA) product in membrane separation processes. The glomerular membrane behaves like a **size- and charge-selective ultrafilter**, with an effective pore radius of ~**7–8 nm**.

---

## 2.2 Filtration Fraction (FF)

The fraction of renal plasma flow that becomes filtrate:

$$FF = \frac{GFR}{RPF} \approx \frac{125}{650} \approx 0.19 \text{ (or 19\%)}$$

A higher FF means more concentrated plasma leaves the glomerulus, raising $\pi_{GC}$ downstream.

---

## 2.3 Tubular Reabsorption — Transport Mechanisms

Reabsorption can occur via:

- **Active transport** (against electrochemical gradient, requires ATP, e.g., Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase, SGLT for glucose).
- **Secondary active transport** (co-transport driven by Na⁺ gradient, e.g., glucose, amino acids).
- **Passive transport** (down gradient, e.g., water via aquaporins, Cl⁻, urea).
- **Convection (solvent drag)** — solutes carried with water.

The **transport maximum ($T_m$)** concept applies to carrier-mediated systems exhibiting saturation kinetics (e.g., glucose, $T_m \approx 375$ mg/min). When filtered load exceeds $T_m$, the excess is excreted (glucosuria in diabetes).

$$\text{Filtration Load}_i = GFR \times P_i$$
