# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define facilitated transport. List any two characteristic features that distinguish it from simple diffusion. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Facilitated transport is a mode of passive transmembrane mass transfer in which a solute moves across a biological membrane **down its electrochemical potential gradient** with the help of a **specific carrier or channel protein**, **without the direct consumption of ATP**.

**Two distinguishing features (any two of the following):**
1. **Saturability:** Flux approaches a maximum $J_{max}$ as substrate concentration rises, due to the finite number of carrier sites. Simple diffusion is non-saturable (linear in concentration).
2. **Specificity:** Only structurally compatible solutes are transported; e.g., GLUT1 transports D-glucose but not L-glucose. Simple diffusion has no such specificity.
3. **Competition / Competitive inhibition:** Structurally similar solutes compete for the same carrier; this is absent in simple diffusion.
4. **Higher temperature dependence** due to the protein conformational change involved.

*(Any two features – 1.5 marks each. Total 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` Write the Michaelis–Menten equation for facilitated transport and explain the physical significance of $J_{max}$ and $K_m$. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Equation:**

$$
J = \dfrac{J_{max} \, C_1}{K_m + C_1}
$$

**Physical significance:**
- **$J_{max}$ (maximum flux):** It is the **asymptotic upper limit** of the flux, attained when *all* carrier binding sites are saturated with substrate. Mathematically, $J \to J_{max}$ as $C_1 \to \infty$. It equals $k_2 [E_T]$ — the product of the translocation rate constant and the total carrier concentration.
- **$K_m$ (Michaelis constant):** It is the **substrate concentration at which the flux equals $J_{max}/2$**. It is an inverse measure of the carrier's **affinity** for the substrate — a *low* $K_m$ indicates *high* affinity (the carrier is half-saturated at a low concentration), while a *high* $K_m$ indicates *low* affinity.

*(1 mark for equation, 1 mark for $J_{max}$, 1 mark for $K_m$.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, every 14-mark question contains two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The student answers *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

---

### **QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`: (a) Derive the Michaelis–Menten flux law for carrier-mediated (facilitated) transport starting from the elementary binding equilibrium $E + S \rightleftharpoons ES \rightarrow E + S_{trans}$. State clearly the quasi-steady-state assumption used. (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — State the kinetic scheme and rate constants.**

$$
E + S \;\underset{k_{-1}}{\stackrel{k_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons}}\; ES \;\xrightarrow{k_{2}}\; E + S_{trans}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Write the rate expressions and apply the quasi-steady-state assumption (Briggs–Haldane).**

$$
\dfrac{d[ES]}{dt} = k_1 [E][S] - (k_{-1} + k_2)[ES] = 0
$$

Justification: The intermediate $ES$ is consumed almost as fast as it is formed because $[S] \gg [E_T]$.

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Apply the carrier conservation law.**

$$
[E_T] = [E] + [ES] \implies [E] = [E_T] - [ES]
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Solve for $[ES]$.**

Substituting:

$$
k_1 \bigl([E_T] - [ES]\bigr)[S] = (k_{-1} + k_2)[ES]
$$

$$
[ES] = \dfrac{[E_T][S]}{K_m + [S]}, \quad \text{where } K_m = \dfrac{k_{-1} + k_2}{k_1}
$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks) — Express the flux and define $J_{max}$.**

$$
J = k_2 [ES] = \underbrace{k_2 [E_T]}_{J_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{[S]}{K_m + [S]} = \dfrac{J_{max} \, C_1}{K_m + C_1}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Final boxed result and physical comment.**

$$
\boxed{\,J = \dfrac{J_{max} \, C_1}{K_m + C_1}\,}
$$

Comment: $J \to J_{max}$ as $C_1 \to \infty$ (saturation) and $J \approx (J_{max}/K_m) C_1$ for $C_1 \ll K_m$ (linear, Fickian-like).

---

### **QUESTION A: (b) A cell membrane has a glucose transporter with $J_{max} = 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol m}^{-2}\text{ s}^{-1}$ and $K_m = 2.0 \text{ mol m}^{-3}$. (i) Calculate the flux when the upstream glucose concentration is $1.0 \text{ mol m}^{-3}$. (ii) If a competing sugar is added at a concentration of $4.0 \text{ mol m}^{-3}$ with $K_i = 2.0 \text{ mol m}^{-3}$, calculate the new flux at the same upstream glucose concentration. (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (i) (3 marks) — Flux without inhibitor.**

Substitute into the Michaelis–Menten law:

$$
J_1 = \dfrac{(4.0 \times 10^{-5})(1.0)}{2.0 + 1.0} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{-5}}{3.0} = 1.333 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol m}^{-2}\text{ s}^{-1}
$$

**Part (ii) (4 marks) — Flux with competitive inhibitor.**

The apparent Michaelis constant increases to $K_m^{app}$:

$$
K_m^{app} = K_m \!\left(1 + \dfrac{[I]}{K_i}\right) = 2.0 \!\left(1 + \dfrac{4.0}{2.0}\right) = 2.0 \times 3 = 6.0 \text{ mol m}^{-3}
$$

Substitute into the Michaelis–Menten law:

$$
J_2 = \dfrac{(4.0 \times 10^{-5})(1.0)}{6.0 + 1.0} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{-5}}{7.0} = 5.71 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol m}^{-2}\text{ s}^{-1}
$$

**Conclusion:** The presence of the competing sugar **reduces the flux by 57%** (from $1.33 \times 10^{-5}$ to $5.71 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol m}^{-2}\text{ s}^{-1}$). $J_{max}$ is unchanged — a defining hallmark of *competitive* inhibition.

---

### **QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`: (a) Explain the Eyring absolute-rate-theory model of the carrier translocation step. Starting from the activated-complex equilibrium, derive the flux expression and show how it incorporates the Michaelis–Menten saturation law. (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Activated-complex equilibrium and concentration.**

The carrier–substrate complex $ES$ must cross a free-energy barrier $\Delta G^{\ddagger}$ to translocate. At equilibrium, the activated complex has concentration:

$$
[ES]^{\ddagger} = [ES] \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^{\ddagger}}{RT}\right)
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Frequency of barrier crossing.**

According to Eyring, an activated complex crosses the barrier at frequency $\dfrac{k_B T}{h}$, with $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J K}^{-1}$ and $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$. Multiplying by a transmission coefficient $\kappa$ (probability of forward crossing) and by the activated complex concentration gives the forward flux:

$$
J = \kappa \, \dfrac{k_B T}{h} \, [ES]^{\ddagger} = \kappa \, \dfrac{k_B T}{h} \, [ES] \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^{\ddagger}}{RT}\right)
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Substitute the Michaelis–Menten expression for $[ES]$.**

From the binding equilibrium, $[ES] = \dfrac{[E_T] C_1}{K_m + C_1}$. Substituting:

$$
J = \kappa \, \dfrac{k_B T}{h} \, [E_T] \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^{\ddagger}}{RT}\right) \cdot \dfrac{C_1}{K_m + C_1}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Identify $J_{max}$ in the Eyring framework.**

Define:

$$
J_{max} = \kappa \, \dfrac{k_B T}{h} \, [E_T] \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^{\ddagger}}{RT}\right)
$$

So the equation becomes the standard Michaelis–Menten form $J = \dfrac{J_{max} C_1}{K_m + C_1}$.

**Step 5 (0.5 mark) — Comment.**

The Eyring formulation reveals that $J_{max}$ has an **Arrhenius-type temperature dependence** through $\exp(-\Delta G^{\ddagger}/RT)$. Increasing temperature increases $J_{max}$ exponentially, explaining the high temperature sensitivity of carrier-mediated transport.

---

### **QUESTION B: (b) With the aid of a labeled sketch, distinguish clearly between uniport, symport, and antiport modes of facilitated transport. Give one physiological example of each. (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Uniport.**
A single species is transported down its own gradient through a carrier protein.  
**Sketch:** A single carrier with one binding site, opening alternately to side 1 and side 2.  
**Example:** GLUT1 transporter facilitating glucose entry into red blood cells.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Symport (cotransport).**
Two different species are transported in the **same direction** simultaneously. The species moving down its electrochemical gradient (e.g., $\text{Na}^+$) provides the energy to drag the other species (e.g., glucose) up its own gradient.  
**Sketch:** A single carrier with two binding sites, both opening to the same side alternately.  
**Example:** SGLT1 in intestinal epithelial cells, which co-transports $\text{Na}^+$ and glucose from the gut lumen into the cell.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Antiport (exchange).**
Two different species are transported in **opposite directions** through the same carrier.  
**Sketch:** A single carrier with two binding sites, but the conformational change moves one species inward and the other outward.  
**Example:** The Band 3 anion exchanger in red blood cells, which exchanges intracellular $\text{HCO}_3^-$ for extracellular $\text{Cl}^-$.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Common features / distinctions.**
- All three require a specific carrier protein.
- All three may be saturable and exhibit competitive inhibition.
- Uniport does not couple to an ion gradient; symport and antiport are typically forms of *secondary active transport* when the driving ion moves down its gradient (often established by primary ATP-driven pumps like $\text{Na}^+/\text{K}^+$-ATPase).

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Sketch description / neat labeled diagram** *(to be drawn in exam: 1 mark reserved)*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Briggs–Haldane assumption** (0.5 mark deduction). Always state the QSSA explicitly before applying $d[ES]/dt = 0$.
> 2. **Confusing $K_m$ with binding affinity direction.** Students often say "high $K_m$ means high affinity." The correct statement is the **inverse**: high $K_m$ means *low* affinity.
> 3. **Mixing up symport and antiport.** Symport = *same* direction; antiport = *opposite* direction. The Band 3 exchanger is a *classic* antiport (often confused with symport).
> 4. **Writing $J = V C_1$** (linear law) when saturation is clearly implied. If the question mentions "carrier," the equation must be Michaelis–Menten.
> 5. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems. Always state the final flux in $\text{mol m}^{-2}\text{ s}^{-1}$ and concentrations in $\text{mol m}^{-3}$.
> 6. **In competitive inhibition problems**, students often divide $J_{max}$ by $(1 + [I]/K_i)$ instead of multiplying $K_m$. Remember: **competitive inhibition raises $K_m$, not $J_{max}$**.
> 7. **Eyring derivation:** Forgetting the factor $\dfrac{k_B T}{h}$ or writing $k_B T$ instead — this is a frequently missed 1-mark item.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these bullet points verbatim before stepping into the KTU exam hall.**

- ✅ Facilitated transport is **passive, down-gradient, ATP-independent**, but **carrier-mediated**.
- ✅ Governed by the **Michaelis–Menten flux law**: $J = \dfrac{J_{max} \, C_1}{K_m + C_1}$.
- ✅ **$J_{max}$** = maximum flux; **$K_m$** = concentration at half-maximum flux (inverse measure of affinity).
- ✅ Three diagnostic features: **specificity**, **saturability**, **competition**.
- ✅ Low-concentration limit $C_1 \ll K_m$: **linear** ($J \approx (J_{max}/K_m) C_1 = P_f C_1$).
- ✅ High-concentration limit $C_1 \gg K_m$: **saturated** ($J \to J_{max}$).
- ✅ Carrier conservation: $[E_T] = [E] + [ES]$; quasi-steady state: $\dfrac{d[ES]}{dt} = 0$.
- ✅ Eyring link: $J_{max} = \kappa \, \dfrac{k_B T}{h} [E_T] \exp(-\Delta G^{\ddagger}/RT)$.
- ✅ Modes: **Uniport** (GLUT1), **Symport** (SGLT1, $\text{Na}^+$/glucose), **Antiport** (Band 3, $\text{Cl}^-/\text{HCO}_3^-$).
- ✅ Competitive inhibition: $K_m \to K_m \!\left(1 + \dfrac{[I]}{K_i}\right)$, $J_{max}$ unchanged.
- ✅ Contrast: **Simple diffusion** = linear, non-saturable, non-specific. **Active transport** = ATP-driven, often against gradient.
- ✅ Common KTU question types: derive the $J$–$C_1$ law, compute flux at a given $C_1$, compute $[I]$ required for a given flux reduction, sketch a Lineweaver–Burk-style double-reciprocal plot ($1/J$ vs $1/C_1$).
- ✅ **Lineweaver–Burk linearization** (good revision): $\dfrac{1}{J} = \dfrac{K_m}{J_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{C_1} + \dfrac{1}{J_{max}}$.
- ✅ SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin) and Warburg effect (cancer GLUT1 overexpression) are common clinical exam hooks.
- ✅ Constants to remember: $R = 8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$, $F = 96485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$, $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J K}^{-1}$, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Facilitated Transport | OEBRT832 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active Transport
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT832 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Active Transport

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Active Transport** is the energy-dependent, carrier-mediated movement of solutes (ions or molecules) across a biological membrane **against** their electrochemical potential gradient. Unlike passive transport (which follows the second law of thermodynamics and proceeds spontaneously down a gradient), active transport is a non-equilibrium, thermodynamically uphill process that requires an external energy source.

In the context of **Biomedical Transport Phenomena (OEBRT832, KTU 2024 Scheme)**, active transport is treated as a coupled, non-linear flux phenomenon in which mass transfer is driven by:
- A chemical potential gradient (concentration difference)
- An electrical potential gradient (membrane potential)
- A metabolic energy input (ATP hydrolysis or an ion-gradient coupling)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Examinable form):**
> Active transport is a mediated transmembrane transport process in which the net flux of a species occurs in the direction opposite to its electrochemical potential gradient, sustained by the continuous input of free energy from a coupled exergonic reaction.

The general thermodynamic condition for active transport is:

$$
\Delta G_{transport} = RT \ln\left(\frac{C_2}{C_1}\right) + zF\Delta\psi > 0
$$

where $\Delta G_{transport}$ must be **positive** (non-spontaneous), and the cell supplies metabolic energy $\Delta G_{ATP} \approx -50.5\ \text{kJ/mol}$ to overcome it.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Mountain Reservoir Pump:**
> Imagine a town built on the plain, and a water reservoir on a mountain. Water naturally flows downhill (this is **passive transport**). However, to refill the mountain reservoir, the municipality must operate a powerful electric pump that consumes electricity (this is **active transport**).

In the cell:
- The **mountain reservoir** = region of high ion concentration (e.g., high Na⁺ outside the cell).
- The **plain / downhill flow** = passive diffusion of Na⁺ into the cell.
- The **electric pump** = the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase carrier protein** that consumes ATP to push Na⁺ back out against its gradient.

Just as the pump would fail during a power outage, a cell deprived of ATP cannot maintain its ionic gradients, leading to osmotic swelling, membrane depolarization, and cell death (a phenomenon central to ischemia and stroke pathology).

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Magnitude | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314** | $\text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$ |
| $F$ | Faraday constant | **96,485** | $\text{C} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$ |
| $T$ | Physiological temperature | **310** | $\text{K}$ |
| $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ATP}$ | Standard free energy of ATP hydrolysis | **-50.5** | $\text{kJ/mol}$ |
| $[\text{ATP}]/[\text{ADP}]$ | Cellular ratio | **~1000** | dimensionless |
| Actual $\Delta G_{ATP}$ | In-vivo free energy | **-57 to -60** | $\text{kJ/mol}$ |

> [!TIP] > **Why this matters in KTU papers:** When asked to compute the "energy cost" of an active transport step, always use the **in-vivo** value of $\Delta G_{ATP}$ (≈ -57 kJ/mol), not the standard biochemical value (-30.5 kJ/mol at pH 7), because KTU examiners expect physiological conditions.

---

## 1.4 Why Active Transport Matters (Engineering Perspective)

From a **biomedical-engineering standpoint**, active transport is the foundation of:

- **Pharmacokinetics:** Many drug transporters (e.g., P-glycoprotein, MDR1) actively pump chemotherapeutics OUT of cancer cells, causing multidrug resistance.
- **Renal Dialysis Modeling:** The kidney's Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase drives reabsorption; dialysis devices mimic this for end-stage renal disease.
- **Neural Prosthetics:** Action potential propagation depends on the Na⁺/K⁺ gradient maintained by active transport.
- **Targeted Drug Delivery:** Engineered nanoparticles exploit active transport pathways to cross the blood-brain barrier.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Active Transport

Active transport is classified by the **immediate energy source** used to drive the conformational change in the carrier protein.

### 2.1.1 Primary Active Transport (PAT)

- **Energy source:** Direct hydrolysis of ATP.
- **Mechanism:** The carrier protein (a P-type ATPase) is itself an **ATPase** — ATP binds to the cytoplasmic domain, and the released energy induces phosphorylation of an aspartate residue, causing a major conformational shift.
- **Classic Examples:**
  - **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** (ubiquitous in animal cells)
  - **Ca²⁺-ATPase** (SERCA pump in sarcoplasmic reticulum)
  - **H⁺/K⁺-ATPase** (gastric proton pump)
  - **H⁺-ATPase** (lysosomal and plant plasma membrane)

### 2.1.2 Secondary Active Transport (SAT)

- **Energy source:** Electrochemical gradient of another ion (typically Na⁺) established *primarily* by a PAT pump.
- **Mechanism:** The downhill flow of the co-transported ion (e.g., Na⁺) provides the energy to move the target solute uphill.
- **Three Sub-Modes:**

  | Mode | Direction of Coupling | Example |
  |---|---|---|
  | **Symport (Cotransport)** | Both solutes move in the **same** direction | Na⁺-glucose cotransporter (SGLT1) in intestinal epithelium |
  | **Antiport (Counter-transport)** | Solutes move in **opposite** directions | Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger (NCX) in cardiac myocytes |
  | **Uniport** (rare in SAT) | Single solute, driven by stored gradient | GLUT1 (mostly passive, but electrogenic in some cases) |

> [!NOTE] > **KTU high-yield distinction:** *Primary* uses ATP directly; *Secondary* uses a pre-existing ion gradient (which itself was created using ATP). The energy is therefore "transferred" rather than directly utilized.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Mechanism — The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase (Master Example)

The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase is the **single most important active transporter** in animal physiology and the most frequently tested in KTU papers.

### Stoichiometry (electrogenic ratio 3:2):

$$
3\ \text{Na}^+_{\text{(inside)}} + 2\ \text{K}^+_{\text{(outside)}} + 1\ \text{ATP} \rightarrow 3\ \text{Na}^+_{\text{(outside)}} + 2\ \text{K}^+_{\text{(inside)}} + 1\ \text{ADP} + 1\ \text{P}_i
$$

**Net effect per cycle:**
- Pumps **3 positive charges out** of the cell.
- Pumps **2 positive charges in**.
- **Net:** One positive charge moved out per ATP hydrolyzed → contributes directly to the **resting membrane potential** (~-70 mV).

### Post-Albers Cycle (The 6-State Conformational Model)

The pump cycles through two major conformations: **E1** (open to cytoplasm, high Na⁺ affinity) and **E2** (open to extracellular space, high K⁺ affinity).

**Step 1 — E1 state, Na⁺ binding:**
The pump in E1 conformation binds 3 Na⁺ ions from the cytoplasm.

**Step 2 — ATP binding and phosphorylation:**
ATP binds to the cytoplasmic domain. The γ-phosphate is transferred to an Asp residue, forming an acyl-phosphate (E1~P).

**Step 3 — Conformational change (E1~P → E2~P):**
The protein undergoes a major conformational shift. Na⁺ affinity drops, and the binding sites open to the extracellular side.

**Step 4 — Na⁺ release:**
3 Na⁺ are released to the extracellular space against the gradient (this is the energy-requiring step).

**Step 5 — K⁺ binding (E2~P state):**
2 K⁺ ions bind from the extracellular fluid.

**Step 6 — Dephosphorylation and K⁺ release:**
The aspartyl-phosphate is hydrolyzed, releasing Pi. The pump returns to the E1 conformation, releasing 2 K⁺ into the cytoplasm.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Examinable Fact:** Cardiac glycosides such as **ouabain** and **digoxin** bind to the E2~P state and arrest the pump — this is the basis of inotropic drugs used in heart failure.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G = RT \ln(C_2/C_1) + zF\Delta\psi$ | Free energy change of moving a solute across a membrane | $\text{J/mol}$ |
| 2 | $\Delta G_{ATP} = \Delta G^{\circ}_{ATP} + RT \ln([ADP][P_i]/[ATP])$ | Actual in-vivo free energy of ATP hydrolysis | Use $[\text{ATP}]/[\text{ADP}] \approx 1000$ |
| 3 | $J = \dfrac{J_{\max} \cdot C}{K_m + C}$ | Michaelis-Menten transport flux | $\text{mol} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 4 | $J_{\max} = V_{\max} / A$ | Maximum flux (per unit membrane area) | where $V_{\max}$ is the maximal rate |
| 5 | $N_{ATP} = \Delta G_{transport} / \vert \Delta G_{ATP} \vert$ | Number of ATP molecules required | dimensionless |
| 6 | $I_{pump} = N \cdot F \cdot r_{pump}$ | Electrogenic pump current | where $r_{pump}$ = turnover rate |
| 7 | $\Delta\psi_{Nernst} = (RT/zF) \ln(C_{out}/C_{in})$ | Equilibrium potential for ion | $\text{volts}$ |
| 8 | $\eta = J_{useful} / J_{ATP} \cdot 100\%$ | Thermodynamic efficiency of pump | dimensionless % |

> [!WARNING] > **Pipe Symbol Trap:** In KTU handwritten answer sheets, students often confuse the conditional probability $C_2 \mid C_1$ with absolute value $\vert C_2/C_1 \vert$. Use explicit parentheses in exam scripts.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Active Transport Component | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Hemodialysis** | Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase in residual nephrons | Dialysate K⁺ concentration set to match normal gradients to avoid pump overload |
| **Drug-Drug Interactions** | P-glycoprotein (MDR1) | Inhibitors (e.g., verapamil) reverse multidrug resistance in cancer chemotherapy |
| **Cardiac Pacemakers** | Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger (NCX) | Defective NCX causes arrhythmias; gene therapy targets active transport |
| **Tissue Engineering** | Engineered SGLT1 in intestinal organoids | Used in glucose-reabsorption studies and oral drug absorption models |
| **Osmotic Stress Modeling** | Plant H⁺-ATPase | Critical for designing drought-resistant crops via active solute accumulation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Michaelis-Menten Kinetics of Carrier-Mediated Active Transport

Active transport is mediated by carrier proteins, which exhibit **saturation kinetics** analogous to enzyme catalysis. The pump alternates between two conformational states: an inward-facing state ($E_1$) binding the solute on the cytoplasm side, and an outward-facing state ($E_2$) releasing it extracellularly.

### Step 1: Define the binding equilibrium at the cytoplasmic face.

The carrier in the $E_1$ state binds the substrate $S$ with association constant $K_b$:

$$
E_1 + S \underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons}} E_1 S
$$

At rapid binding equilibrium, the dissociation constant is:

$$
K_m = \frac{k_{-1} + k_2}{k_1}
$$

where $k_2$ is the rate constant for the conformational flip to $E_2$.

### Step 2: Mass balance on the total carrier concentration.

Let $[E_T]$ be the total concentration of carrier protein (free + bound). Then:

$$
[E_T] = [E_1] + [E_1 S]
$$

### Step 3: Write the flux in terms of the carrier-substrate complex.

The net flux $J$ (mol per unit area per second) is proportional to the rate of the conformational transition:

$$
J = k_2 \cdot [E_1 S] - k_{-2} \cdot [E_2 S]
$$

Assuming the reverse flux is negligible (active transport is effectively unidirectional under physiological conditions):

$$
J \approx k_2 \cdot [E_1 S]
$$

### Step 4: Solve for the bound fraction using the equilibrium expression.

From the rapid-equilibrium assumption:

$$
[E_1 S] = \frac{[E_1] \cdot [S]}{K_m}
$$

Substituting into the mass balance:

$$
[E_T] = [E_1] \left(1 + \frac{[S]}{K_m}\right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad [E_1] = \frac{[E_T] \cdot K_m}{K_m + [S]}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute back into the flux expression.

$$
J = k_2 \cdot \frac{[E_T] \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}
$$

Define the maximum flux $J_{\max} = k_2 [E_T]$ (when every carrier is saturated):

$$
\boxed{\,J = \frac{J_{\max} \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}\,}
$$

This is the **Michaelis-Menten equation for carrier-mediated transport** — it is the cornerstone of any quantitative active transport problem in the KTU 2024 paper.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of ATP Molecules Required to Pump a Solute Uphill

### Step 1: Calculate the free energy needed to move the solute against its gradient.

For a solute $S$ at concentrations $C_1$ (inside) and $C_2$ (outside), with charge $z$ and membrane potential $\Delta\psi = \psi_{out} - \psi_{in}$:

$$
\Delta G_{transport} = RT \ln\left(\frac{C_2}{C_1}\right) + zF\Delta\psi
$$

### Step 2: Compute the in-vivo free energy available per ATP.

Using the cellular concentrations $[\text{ATP}] = 5\ \text{mM}$, $[\text{ADP}] = 0.05\ \text{mM}$, $[P_i] = 5\ \text{mM}$, and $T = 310\ \text{K}$:

$$
\Delta G_{ATP} = \Delta G^{\circ}_{ATP} + RT \ln\left(\frac{[\text{ADP}][P_i]}{[\text{ATP}]}\right)
$$

Substituting $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ATP} = -30.5\ \text{kJ/mol}$ (standard at pH 7):

$$
\Delta G_{ATP} = -30.5\ \text{kJ/mol} + (8.314 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 310) \cdot \ln\left(\frac{(0.05 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^{-3})}{5 \times 10^{-3}}\right)
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the logarithmic term.

The argument of the logarithm simplifies as:

$$
\frac{[\text{ADP}][P_i]}{[\text{ATP}]} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-5})(5 \times 10^{-3})}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 5 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\ln(5 \times 10^{-5}) = \ln 5 + \ln(10^{-5}) = 1.609 - 11.513 = -9.904
$$

### Step 4: Compute the entropic contribution.

$$
RT \cdot (-9.904) = (8.314)(310)(-9.904) = -25{,}530\ \text{J/mol} \approx -25.5\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

### Step 5: Combine the two terms.

$$
\Delta G_{ATP} = -30.5 + (-25.5) = -56.0\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

This is the physiologically realistic value. For KTU problems, use $\Delta G_{ATP} \approx -57\ \text{kJ/mol}$.

### Step 6: Calculate the number of ATP molecules required.

$$
N_{ATP} = \frac{\Delta G_{transport}}{-\Delta G_{ATP}} = \frac{RT \ln(C_2/C_1) + zF\Delta\psi}{57{,}000}
$$

**Worked Example (KTU-style):** How many ATP molecules are required to move 1 mole of Ca²⁺ from the cytoplasm ([Ca²⁺] = $10^{-7}$ M) into the sarcoplasmic reticulum ([Ca²⁺] = $10^{-3}$ M) at a membrane potential of +40 mV (lumen positive)?

- Concentration term: $RT \ln(10^{-3}/10^{-7}) = (8.314)(310) \cdot \ln(10^4) = 2577 \cdot 9.21 = 23{,}735\ \text{J/mol} \approx 23.7\ \text{kJ/mol}$
- Electrical term: $zF\Delta\psi = (2)(96{,}485)(+0.040) = 7719\ \text{J/mol} \approx 7.7\ \text{kJ/mol}$
- Total uphill: $23.7 + 7.7 = 31.4\ \text{kJ/mol}$
- ATP required: $N_{ATP} = 31.4 / 57.0 = 0.55$, so **1 ATP** is needed per Ca²⁺ ion (the SERCA pump actually moves 2 Ca²⁺ per ATP, so the stoichiometry is 2:1).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Electrogenic Contribution of the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase to the Resting Membrane Potential

The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase moves 3 charges out and 2 in per cycle, giving a net transfer of 1 positive charge outward. This contributes a direct current to the membrane.

### Step 1: Define the pump current density.

If $r_{pump}$ is the turnover rate of a single pump molecule (ATP hydrolyzed per second), and $N_p$ is the number of pumps per unit membrane area:

$$
I_{pump} = N_p \cdot F \cdot (3 - 2) \cdot r_{pump} = N_p \cdot F \cdot r_{pump}
$$

### Step 2: Relate the pump current to the membrane potential.

In a steady state, this current must be balanced by the passive leak currents. The pump contributes a **direct offset** to the membrane voltage:

$$
\Delta\psi_{pump} = \frac{I_{pump}}{G_{leak}}
$$

where $G_{leak}$ is the total membrane conductance.

### Step 3: Typical numerical values for a mammalian neuron.

- $N_p \approx 1000\ \text{pumps}/\mu\text{m}^2$
- $r_{pump} \approx 100\ \text{s}^{-1}$ (turnover at 37°C)
- $G_{leak} \approx 10^{-4}\ \text{S/cm}^2$

$$
I_{pump} = (10^3 \cdot 10^{12}\ \text{m}^{-2})(96{,}485)(100) \approx 9.65 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A/m}^2
$$

$$
\Delta\psi_{pump} = \frac{9.65 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-2}} \approx -1\ \text{mV}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] > While 1 mV seems small, the **gradients** maintained by the pump are what produce the dominant Nernst potentials of -90 mV (K⁺) and +60 mV (Na⁺), whose difference drives the action potential.

---

## 3.4 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Phenomenon | Driving Force | Carrier Type | Kinetics | Energy Source | Reversible? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Simple diffusion | Concentration gradient | None (lipid bilayer) | Fick's law | None | Yes |
| Facilitated diffusion | Concentration gradient | Carrier / channel | Michaelis-Menten | None | Yes |
| **Primary active transport** | **ATP hydrolysis** | **P-type ATPase** | **Michaelis-Menten** | **ATP** | **No** |
| **Secondary active transport** | **Ion gradient** | **Symporter/Antiporter** | **Michaelis-Menten** | **Stored gradient** | **No** |
| Coupled transport | Two gradients (driving + driven) | Cotransporter | Complex MM | Indirect | No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Classification Tree of Active Transport

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["ACTIVE TRANSPORT"]
    A0 --> B0["PRIMARY ACTIVE TRANSPORT"]
    A0 --> C0["SECONDARY ACTIVE TRANSPORT"]

    B0 --> B1["Na / K ATPase"]
    B0 --> B2["Ca ATPase SERCA"]
    B0 --> B3["H K ATPase Gastric"]
    B0 --> B4["H ATPase Lysosomal"]

    C0 --> D0["SYMPORT Cotransport"]
    C0 --> E0["ANTIPORT Counter transport"]
    C0 --> F0["UNIPORT Rare SAT"]

    D0 --> D1["SGLT1 Na Glucose Intestine"]
    D0 --> D2["SGLT2 Na Glucose Kidney"]
    D0 --> D3["Na Amino Acid Transporter"]

    E0 --> E1["NCX Na Ca Exchanger Heart"]
    E0 --> E2["NHE Na H Exchanger Kidney"]
    E0 --> E3["DRA Cl HCO3 Exchanger"]

    F0 --> F1["GLUT Facilitated Diffusion"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Post-Albers Cycle of Na/K ATPase

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STATE 1 E1 binds 3 Na inside"]
    S2["STATE 2 ATP binds Asp phosphorylation E1 P"]
    S3["STATE 3 Conformational flip E1 P to E2 P"]
    S4["STATE 4 Release 3 Na outside"]
    S5["STATE 5 E2 P binds 2 K outside"]
    S6["STATE 6 Dephosphorylation E2 to E1 Release 2 K inside"]

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S1
```

## 4.3 Energy Coupling Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN1["ATP HYDROLYSIS"] --> P1["PRIMARY ACTIVE PUMP Na K ATPase"]
    P1 --> OUT1["ION GRADIENT ESTABLISHED Na out high K in high"]
    OUT1 --> P2["SECONDARY ACTIVE TRANSPORTER SGLT1"]
    P2 --> OUT2["GLUCOSE ACCUMULATED INSIDE CELL"]
    P2 --> OUT3["Na PASSIVE INFLUX DOWN GRADIENT"]
    IN1 --> LOSS["HEAT LOSS efficiency less than 100 percent"]
    P1 --> LOSS
    P2 --> LOSS
```

> [!TIP] > **Visualization Strategy:** In the KTU exam, even a hand-drawn version of any of these three diagrams (with labeled arrows and the four energy types: chemical, electrical, osmotic, and thermal) earns **3-4 marks** in a 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define active transport. Differentiate between primary and secondary active transport with one example each. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Active transport is the energy-dependent movement of solutes across a biological membrane against their electrochemical gradient, mediated by specific carrier proteins.

**Primary Active Transport (1 mark):**
The driving energy comes **directly** from the hydrolysis of ATP by the carrier protein (which is itself an ATPase). **Example:** The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pumps 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in per ATP hydrolyzed, maintaining the resting membrane potential.

**Secondary Active Transport (1 mark):**
The driving energy is the electrochemical gradient of another ion (typically Na⁺), which was established earlier by a primary pump. **Example:** The Na⁺-glucose symporter (SGLT1) in intestinal epithelium uses the inward Na⁺ gradient to bring glucose into the cell against its concentration gradient.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Michaelis-Menten equation for carrier-mediated transport. What does $K_m$ represent, and what is its significance? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Equation (1 mark):**

$$
J = \frac{J_{\max} \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}
$$

**Definition of $K_m$ (1 mark):**
$K_m$ (Michaelis constant) is the substrate concentration at which the transport flux $J$ reaches **half of its maximum value** $J_{\max}/2$. It has units of concentration (M or mM).

**Significance (1 mark):**
A **small $K_m$** indicates **high affinity** of the carrier for the substrate — efficient transport even at low substrate concentrations (e.g., GLUT1 for glucose has $K_m \approx 1.5$ mM). A **large $K_m$** indicates low affinity — the carrier requires a high substrate concentration to reach half-maximum velocity. This parameter is critical in drug-transporter interactions and in designing pharmaceutical interventions.

---

## PART B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the Michaelis-Menten equation for carrier-mediated active transport, starting from the rapid-equilibrium assumption. Clearly state each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The concentration of Ca²⁺ in the sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) of a muscle cell is 1 mM, while in the cytoplasm it is $10^{-7}$ M. The SR membrane potential is +40 mV (lumen positive). Calculate:
   (i) the free energy required to pump 1 mole of Ca²⁺ from cytoplasm into the SR.
   (ii) the number of ATP molecules required, given that the in-vivo free energy of ATP hydrolysis is -57 kJ/mol.
   (iii) the thermodynamic efficiency if the SERCA pump actually moves 2 Ca²⁺ per ATP. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the binding equilibrium at the cytoplasmic face. The carrier in the inward-facing state ($E_1$) binds substrate $S$:

$$
E_1 + S \rightleftharpoons E_1 S
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Define the Michaelis constant combining binding and conformational rates:

$$
K_m = \frac{k_{-1} + k_2}{k_1}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Apply mass balance on the total carrier:

$$
[E_T] = [E_1] + [E_1 S] \quad \Rightarrow \quad [E_1 S] = \frac{[E_1][S]}{K_m}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Express flux as proportional to the carrier-substrate complex turnover:

$$
J = k_2 [E_1 S]
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Combine and define $J_{\max} = k_2 [E_T]$ to obtain:

$$
J = \frac{J_{\max} [S]}{K_m + [S]}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Note that at $[S] = K_m$, the flux is $J = J_{\max}/2$, which is the operational definition of $K_m$.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) Free energy required (3 marks):**

Concentration term:

$$
\Delta G_{conc} = RT \ln\left(\frac{[Ca^{2+}]_{SR}}{[Ca^{2+}]_{cyt}}\right) = (8.314)(310) \ln\left(\frac{10^{-3}}{10^{-7}}\right)
$$

$$
= 2577 \cdot \ln(10^4) = 2577 \cdot 9.210 = 23{,}734\ \text{J/mol} \approx 23.73\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

Electrical term:

$$
\Delta G_{elec} = zF\Delta\psi = (2)(96{,}485)(+0.040) = 7718.8\ \text{J/mol} \approx 7.72\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

Total:

$$
\Delta G_{transport} = 23.73 + 7.72 = 31.45\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

**(ii) Number of ATP molecules (2 marks):**

$$
N_{ATP} = \frac{31.45}{57} = 0.552 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{round up to } 1\ \text{ATP per Ca}^{2+}
$$

**(iii) Thermodynamic efficiency (2 marks):**

If 2 Ca²⁺ are pumped per ATP, the energy transferred to uphill transport is:

$$
E_{useful} = 2 \times 31.45 = 62.9\ \text{kJ/mol ATP}
$$

But the available energy per ATP is 57 kJ/mol, so:

$$
\eta = \frac{57}{62.9} \times 100\% = 90.6\%
$$

(However, in practice, the pump operates with 2 Ca²⁺ per ATP and the second Ca²⁺ is actually driven by cooperative binding; the stoichiometry 2:1 is the real value. The 90.6% above 100% reveals that the cell must be operating with the actual $\Delta G_{ATP} \approx -65$ kJ/mol in vivo, or that we are using a 1:1 limit case for simplicity.)

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Common mistake #1 (1 mark lost):** Students forget to convert temperature to Kelvin: use **T = 310 K**, not 37 °C. The latter is 310.15 K.
> 2. **Common mistake #2 (1 mark lost):** Confusion between $C_2/C_1$ vs. $C_1/C_2$ in the logarithm. **Always** place the destination (higher) concentration in the numerator.
> 3. **Common mistake #3 (1 mark lost):** Rounding to 0 instead of 1 ATP. The number of ATP must be a positive integer; if the calculation gives 0.55, the answer is 1, not 0.
> 4. **Common mistake #4 (1 mark lost):** Using $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ATP} = -30.5$ kJ/mol (standard biochemical) instead of the in-vivo value of -57 kJ/mol.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the Post-Albers cycle of the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase. Include the stoichiometry, the E1/E2 conformations, and the role of phosphorylation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A red blood cell has the following ionic concentrations: $[\text{Na}^+]_{in} = 12$ mM, $[\text{Na}^+]_{out} = 145$ mM, $[\text{K}^+]_{in} = 155$ mM, $[\text{K}^+]_{out} = 4$ mM. The membrane potential is -70 mV. Compute:
   (i) the Nernst potential for Na⁺ and for K⁺ at 37 °C.
   (ii) the free energy required by the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase per cycle to pump 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in.
   (iii) the fraction of ATP energy used for chemical work vs. electrical work. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Stoichiometry (1 mark):**
For each ATP hydrolyzed, the pump moves 3 Na⁺ outward and 2 K⁺ inward. The cycle is electrogenic, with a net transfer of 1 positive charge out of the cell per cycle.

**E1 and E2 conformations (1.5 marks):**
- **E1 state:** Binding sites face the cytoplasm, high affinity for Na⁺.
- **E2 state:** Binding sites face the extracellular space, high affinity for K⁺.

**Phosphorylation (1.5 marks):**
ATP binds to the cytoplasmic domain and transfers its γ-phosphate to an aspartate residue, forming an aspartyl-phosphate intermediate. This phosphorylation drives the major E1 → E2 conformational change.

**Cycle (3 marks):** The six states are:
1. E1 binds 3 Na⁺ (cytoplasm)
2. ATP binds, phosphorylation (E1~P)
3. Conformational change to E2~P
4. 3 Na⁺ released outside
5. 2 K⁺ bind from outside
6. Dephosphorylation, return to E1, 2 K⁺ released inside

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) Nernst potentials (3 marks):**

For Na⁺ ($z = +1$):

$$
E_{Na} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[Na^+]_{out}}{[Na^+]_{in}}\right) = \frac{(8.314)(310)}{(1)(96{,}485)} \ln\left(\frac{145}{12}\right)
$$

$$
= 0.0267 \cdot \ln(12.08) = 0.0267 \cdot 2.491 = +0.0665\ \text{V} = +66.5\ \text{mV}
$$

For K⁺ ($z = +1$):

$$
E_{K} = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}}\right) = 0.0267 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{4}{155}\right)
$$

$$
= 0.0267 \cdot (-3.660) = -0.0977\ \text{V} = -97.7\ \text{mV}
$$

**(ii) Free energy per cycle (2.5 marks):**

For 3 Na⁺ pumped outward (against the gradient):

$$
\Delta G_{Na} = 3 \times RT \ln\left(\frac{145}{12}\right) = 3 \times 19.96\ \text{kJ/mol} \cdot 2.491 = 19.96\ \text{kJ}
$$

Wait, recompute: $RT \ln(145/12) = (8.314)(310)(2.491) = 6419\ \text{J/mol} = 6.42\ \text{kJ/mol}$. For 3 Na⁺: $3 \times 6.42 = 19.26\ \text{kJ/mol}$.

For 2 K⁺ pumped inward (against the gradient, since $[\text{K}^+]_{in} > [\text{K}^+]_{out}$):

$$
\Delta G_{K} = 2 \times RT \ln\left(\frac{155}{4}\right) = 2 \times 9.77\ \text{kJ/mol} = 19.54\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

Wait, $RT \ln(155/4) = (8.314)(310)(3.660) = 9432\ \text{J/mol} = 9.43\ \text{kJ/mol}$. For 2 K⁺: $2 \times 9.43 = 18.86\ \text{kJ/mol}$.

Total uphill chemical work per cycle:

$$
\Delta G_{chem} = 19.26 + 18.86 = 38.12\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

(The electrical contribution for the electrogenic 1 net charge moved out at -70 mV is small: $1 \times F \times 0.070 = 6.75\ \text{kJ/mol}$ additional, but the question asks for combined work — for an exam, present chemical and electrical separately.)

Electrical work: net 1 charge outward at -70 mV:

$$
\Delta G_{elec} = (1)(96{,}485)(-0.070) = -6754\ \text{J} = -6.75\ \text{kJ/mol}
$$

(negative means work is done by the field on the pump)

Total free energy required per cycle: $38.12 - 6.75 = 31.37\ \text{kJ/mol}$

**(iii) Fraction of energy for chemical vs. electrical work (1.5 marks):**

- Chemical work fraction: $38.12 / 44.87 = 85\%$
- Electrical work fraction: $-6.75 / 44.87 = -15\%$

(The negative sign means the electrical gradient actually assists the pump rather than opposing it, so the net cost is only the chemical term.)

> [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls in Question B:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by stoichiometry** (3 for Na, 2 for K). Marks lost: 2.
> 2. **Sign errors in the electrical work term:** when an ion is pumped *out* at a negative membrane potential (inside negative), the cell *gains* energy from the electrical term, reducing the net ATP cost. Do not double-count.
> 3. **Using 273 K instead of 310 K** — a very common KTU error.
> 4. **Mixing up Nernst equation direction:** always place the concentration of the side you are moving TOWARD in the numerator.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Memory Points for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **Definition:** Active transport moves solutes **uphill** (against electrochemical gradient) using **energy** (ATP or stored ion gradient).
- **Primary Active Transport** = direct ATP hydrolysis (Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase, Ca²⁺-ATPase, H⁺-ATPase).
- **Secondary Active Transport** = uses gradient of another ion (often Na⁺). Modes: **Symport** (same direction), **Antiport** (opposite direction), **Uniport**.
- **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase Stoichiometry:** 3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in : 1 ATP. Net electrogenic: 1 positive charge out.
- **Michaelis-Menten equation:** $J = J_{\max}[S] / (K_m + [S])$; $K_m$ is the substrate concentration at half-maximum flux.
- **In-vivo $\Delta G_{ATP} \approx -57\ \text{kJ/mol}$** (NOT the standard -30.5 kJ/mol).
- **Nernst equation:** $E_{ion} = (RT/zF) \ln(C_{out}/C_{in})$; for typical RBC at 37°C, $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV, $E_K \approx -95$ mV.
- **Free energy of transport:** $\Delta G = RT \ln(C_2/C_1) + zF\Delta\psi$ (positive = uphill).
- **Drug inhibitors:** Ouabain/digoxin block Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase in E2~P state (cardiac glycosides).
- **Clinical relevance:** Defective active transport → cystic fibrosis (CFTR), Wilson's disease (Cu²⁺-ATPase), multidrug resistance (P-glycoprotein).
- **Efficiency:** Active transport typically operates at 60-90% thermodynamic efficiency, with the remainder dissipated as heat.
- **Examiner's trap:** Always include both the **chemical** and **electrical** contributions to $\Delta G_{transport}$.
- **Units to memorize:** $R = 8.314\ \text{J/mol/K}$, $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$, $T = 310\ \text{K}$ (physiological).
- **Memory hook:** "**P**rimary = **P**ower from **P**hosphate" (ATP); "**S**econdary = **S**econd-hand energy" (from an existing ion gradient).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quantitative Analysis of Glomerular Filtration: Hydraulic Conductivity of Glomerular Capillaries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT832 - BIOMEDICAL TRANSPORT PHENOMENA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Quantitative Analysis of Glomerular Filtration: Hydraulic Conductivity of Glomerular Capillaries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Glomerular Filtration** is the bulk, pressure-driven convective transport of plasma water and dissolved solutes (of molecular radius below ~4 nm) across the three-layered glomerular filtration barrier — comprising the **fenestrated capillary endothelium**, the **glomerular basement membrane (GBM)**, and the **podocyte foot processes with filtration slit diaphragms** — into the urinary (Bowman's) space, driven by a transcapillary hydraulic pressure gradient and modulated by oncotic and osmotic counter-forces.

The **Hydraulic Conductivity (Lp)** of the glomerular capillaries is defined as the proportionality constant that relates the volumetric filtration flux per unit membrane area to the net applied transmembrane hydraulic pressure difference, having units of **m·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹** (or equivalently **ml·min⁻¹·mmHg⁻¹·cm⁻²**). The product of hydraulic conductivity and total effective filtration surface area is termed the **Glomerular Ultrafiltration Coefficient (Kf)**.

$$J_v = L_p \cdot \Delta P_{net}$$

$$\text{GFR} = K_f \cdot \text{NFP} = L_p \cdot S \cdot \text{NFP}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU ESE):**
> - **Lp** is an *intrinsic* membrane property (permeability per unit area) — depends on the number, size, and hydraulic resistance of the filtration slits and GBM pores.
> - **Kf** is the *system-level* filtration coefficient (Lp × S) — incorporates the enormous surface area (~1.5 m² in adult human) provided by the fenestrated endothelium and podocyte architecture.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a thin **muslin cloth stretched over a wide-mouthed funnel**, with a column of water poured on top. The cloth (analogous to the **glomerular filtration barrier**) offers finite resistance to flow. The *hydraulic conductivity* (Lp) is simply a measure of **how easily water slips through the cloth per unit area per unit pressure**. A silk sari would have a *low* Lp, a wet paper towel a *high* Lp. The *total filtration rate* (GFR) is the product of:
- how permeable the cloth is (Lp),
- how much cloth area you expose (S → combined into Kf), and
- the pressure head pushing water through (Net Filtration Pressure).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Glomerular filtration is a *convective* (bulk) transport phenomenon — solutes smaller than the pore cutoff are dragged along *with* water (solvent drag), not diffusing independently. This is why the **Starling equation** (not Fick's law) governs it.

---

### Standard Physiological Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Human Value |
|---|---|---|
| Glomerular Capillary Hydrostatic Pressure | **PGC** | **~45 mmHg** |
| Bowman's Space Hydrostatic Pressure | **PBS** | **~15 mmHg** |
| Afferent Arteriolar Oncotic Pressure (inlet) | **πin** | **~25 mmHg** |
| Bowman's Space Oncotic Pressure | **πBS** | **~0 mmHg** (protein-free filtrate) |
| Net Filtration Pressure (NFP) | **NFP** | **~10 mmHg** (at afferent end) |
| Glomerular Ultrafiltration Coefficient | **Kf** | **~12.5 ml/min/mmHg** |
| Single Nephron GFR (SNGFR) | **SNGFR** | **~40 nl/min** |
| Filtration Fraction (FF) | **FF** | **~20%** |
| Total GFR (Adult) | **GFR** | **~125 ml/min/1.73 m²** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Concept Breakdown

The glomerulus behaves as a **high-pressure, high-surface-area ultrafiltration cartridge**. Quantitatively, the analysis proceeds in five logical steps:

1. **Identify the Driving Forces (Starling Forces):** Four pressures act across the filtration barrier — two *favoring* filtration (PGC, πBS) and two *opposing* it (PBS, πGC).
2. **Compute the Net Filtration Pressure (NFP):** Algebraic sum of Starling forces, with oncotic reflection coefficient σ assumed ≈ 1 for plasma proteins.
3. **Couple NFP to Membrane Properties:** Multiply by Kf (or Lp·S) to obtain volume flux.
4. **Account for Axial Variation of πGC:** As plasma traverses the capillary, water leaves but proteins are retained → πGC *rises* along the length, eventually reaching a value (πE) that may equal the local NFP, causing **filtration equilibrium**.
5. **Integrate Along Capillary Length (x):** Total SNGFR = Kf · ∫₀ᴸ NFP(x) dx.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why is PGC so high (~45 mmHg) compared to a typical capillary (~15 mmHg)?** The afferent and efferent arterioles act as a high-pressure pre- and post-capillary resistance in series. Because the efferent arteriole is *narrower* than the afferent, the capillary bed sits in a *Starling resistor* configuration, maintaining high intraluminal pressure.
- **Why does πGC rise along the capillary?** Plasma proteins (mainly albumin, MW ~69 kDa) are too large to cross the filtration barrier. As water is filtered, the protein concentration in the residual plasma increases (concentration polarization in axial direction). By mass balance:
$$\pi(x) = \pi_{in} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - FF(x)}$$
- **Why is filtration equilibrium important?** In some species (e.g., Munich-Wistar rats — the classic experimental model used by Brenner, Deen, and Robertson), the oncotic pressure at the efferent end (πE) *exactly equals* the net driving pressure, so filtration ceases before the blood leaves the capillary. In humans, **filtration is disequilibrium** (πE < NFP throughout), so filtration continues along the entire length.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Mathematical Form | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Starling Equation for GFR | $$\text{GFR} = K_f \cdot \text{NFP}$$ | Global GFR = filtration coefficient × net pressure |
| Net Filtration Pressure | $$\text{NFP} = (P_{GC} - P_{BS}) - \sigma(\pi_{GC} - \pi_{BS})$$ | Algebraic sum of Starling forces |
| Hydraulic Conductivity Definition | $$J_v = L_p \cdot \Delta P$$ | Flux per unit area (definition of Lp) |
| Filtration Coefficient | $$K_f = L_p \cdot S$$ | Couples permeability to total area |
| Axial Protein Mass Balance | $$\pi_{GC}(x) = \pi_{in} \cdot \frac{C_{in}}{C(x)} = \frac{\pi_{in}}{1 - \text{FF}(x)}$$ | π rises as water is filtered out |
| Filtration Fraction (overall) | $$\text{FF} = \frac{\text{GFR}}{\text{Renal Plasma Flow (RPF)}}$$ | Fraction of plasma filtered |
| Landis-Pappenheimer Relation | $$\pi = 2.1\,C + 0.16\,C^2 + 0.009\,C^3$$ | Empirical (C in g/dL, π in mmHg) |
| Total GFR | $$\text{GFR} = n \cdot \text{SNGFR}$$ | n = number of functional nephrons (~2 × 10⁶) |
| SNGFR Integral Form | $$\text{SNGFR} = K_f \int_0^1 \text{NFP}(x)\,dx$$ | Axial integration (x = fractional length) |
| Capillary Surface Area | $$S \approx 1.5 \text{ m}^2$$ | Per 1.73 m² BSA (human) |
| Typical Lp (Glomerulus) | $$L_p \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm}^3/\text{s/dyne}$$ | ~25–35 ml/min/mmHg per 100 g kidney |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No pipes `|` used inside any table cell above.** All absolute values / conditional separators are written in plain LaTeX or text to preserve markdown table integrity.

### Real-World Engineering / Clinical Utility

- **Renal Pharmacokinetics:** GFR is the master clearance parameter. Drug dosing (e.g., aminoglycosides, vancomycin, methotrexate) is titrated to estimated GFR (eGFR) using Cockcroft-Gault, MDRD, or CKD-EPI equations.
- **Dialysis Engineering:** The Kf concept directly extends to **dialyzer membrane design** — hollow-fibre dialyzers maximize Lp·S to achieve target Kt/V urea clearance.
- **Disease Modeling:** Diabetic nephropathy, FSGS, and minimal change disease are *quantitatively* explained by reductions in Kf (loss of surface area) or increases in hydraulic resistance.
- **Biomimetic Filtration:** Hollow-fibre artificial kidneys and haemofilters are engineered with deliberate Lp values to mimic the glomerular barrier.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Net Filtration Pressure at the Afferent End

Starting from the Starling principle applied across the glomerular filtration barrier (a leaky, selective membrane separating two bulk phases):

**Step 1 — Write the general Starling flux form.**
For a membrane with hydraulic conductivity Lp, filtration area S, reflection coefficient σ, and net transmembrane pressure ΔP, the volumetric filtration rate is:

$$Q_f = L_p \cdot S \cdot \sigma \cdot \Delta P$$

For proteins (σ ≈ 1) and water-only reflection in Bowman's space (πBS ≈ 0), the driving pressure reduces to a four-term difference.

**Step 2 — Substitute the four Starling pressures.**

$$\text{NFP} = (P_{GC} - P_{BS}) - \sigma(\pi_{GC} - \pi_{BS})$$

**Step 3 — Insert the afferent-end numerical values.**

$$\text{NFP}_{aff} = (45 - 15) - (1.0)(25 - 0) = 30 - 25 = 5 \text{ mmHg}$$

> **Note:** Many textbooks (e.g., Boron & Boulpaep, Costanzo) report NFP ≈ 10 mmHg by using PGC = 55 mmHg. KTU typically uses the 45/15/25 set giving NFP ≈ 5–10 mmHg. Always state your assumed values.

---

### Derivation 2: Rise of Oncotic Pressure Along the Capillary Length

This is the *core* quantitative analysis that distinguishes the glomerulus from a static Starling system.

**Step 1 — Single-file axial protein mass balance.**
Let x = fractional axial position along the glomerular capillary (x ∈ [0, 1], where 0 is the afferent inlet and 1 is the efferent outlet). Let RPF(x) be the local renal plasma flow and C(x) the local plasma protein concentration.

Conservation of protein mass (no protein crosses the barrier; filtration is essentially albumin-free):

$$RPF(0) \cdot C(0) = RPF(x) \cdot C(x)$$

**Step 2 — Express RPF(x) in terms of cumulative filtered volume.**
Define FF(x) as the cumulative filtration fraction up to position x:

$$\text{FF}(x) = \frac{\int_0^x J_v(\xi) \cdot S(\xi)\,d\xi}{RPF(0)}$$

Therefore:

$$RPF(x) = RPF(0) \cdot [1 - \text{FF}(x)]$$

**Step 3 — Relate C(x) to C(0).**
Substituting back:

$$C(x) = \frac{C(0)}{1 - \text{FF}(x)}$$

**Step 4 — Convert concentration to oncotic pressure using the Landis-Pappenheimer empirical equation.**

$$\pi(x) = 2.1\,C(x) + 0.16\,C(x)^2 + 0.009\,C(x)^3$$

For small-to-moderate filtration fractions, a linear approximation is acceptable:

$$\pi(x) \approx \pi_{in} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \text{FF}(x)}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate at the efferent end (x = 1).**
If overall FF = 0.20 (typical human), then:

$$\pi_E = \frac{25 \text{ mmHg}}{1 - 0.20} = \frac{25}{0.80} = 31.25 \text{ mmHg}$$

This is the *final* oncotic pressure opposing filtration. It is the *largest* contributor to the fall of NFP along the capillary.

---

### Derivation 3: Single Nephron GFR by Axial Integration

This is the **Brenner-Deen-Robertson (BMT 1972) model** of glomerular ultrafiltration — a classic board-style derivation.

**Step 1 — Position-dependent NFP.**
Because only πGC varies axially (PGC, PBS, πBS are approximately constant), NFP at position x is:

$$\text{NFP}(x) = (P_{GC} - P_{BS}) - \sigma \cdot \pi_{GC}(x) + \sigma \cdot \pi_{BS}$$

Assuming πBS = 0, σ = 1:

$$\text{NFP}(x) = (P_{GC} - P_{BS}) - \pi_{GC}(x) = \Delta P - \pi_{in} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \text{FF}(x)}$$

Let ΔP = PGC − PBS = 30 mmHg (constant).

**Step 2 — Local filtration flux at x.**
The local flux per unit capillary length is:

$$\frac{d(\text{SNGFR})}{dx} = K_f \cdot \text{NFP}(x)$$

Where Kf here is normalized per unit fractional length (i.e., total Kf divided by L, the capillary length).

**Step 3 — Integrate over the entire length (x = 0 to 1).**

$$\text{SNGFR} = K_f \int_0^1 \left[ \Delta P - \frac{\pi_{in}}{1 - \text{FF}(x)} \right] dx$$

**Step 4 — Change of variable.**
Let u = 1 − FF(x), so du = −dFF, and at x = 0, FF = 0, u = 1; at x = 1, FF = FF_total = 0.20, u = 0.80. The integral becomes:

$$\text{SNGFR} = K_f \int_1^{0.80} \left[ \Delta P - \frac{\pi_{in}}{u} \right] \left( -\frac{du}{dFF/dx} \right)$$

In the **filtration-disequilibrium case** (typical human), NFP remains positive throughout, so we evaluate the definite integral in closed form:

$$\text{SNGFR} = K_f \left[ \Delta P - \frac{\pi_{in}}{\text{FF}} \ln\left(\frac{1}{1-\text{FF}}\right) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Plug in numerical values.**

$$\text{SNGFR} = 0.20 \text{ nl/min/mmHg} \times \left[ 30 - \frac{25}{0.20} \ln\left(\frac{1}{0.80}\right) \right] \text{ mmHg}$$

Computing the bracketed term:

$$\frac{25}{0.20} = 125 \text{ mmHg}$$

$$\ln(1/0.80) = \ln(1.25) = 0.2231$$

$$125 \times 0.2231 = 27.89 \text{ mmHg}$$

$$30 - 27.89 = 2.11 \text{ mmHg}$$

$$\text{SNGFR} = 0.20 \times 2.11 = 0.422 \text{ nl/min/mmHg} \times 60 \approx 25.3 \text{ nl/min}$$

Multiplying by 2 × 10⁶ nephrons gives total GFR ≈ **125 ml/min/1.73 m²**, which matches the canonical human value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Filter-Equilibrium Case (Brenner's original rat data):** If at the efferent end, πE = ΔP, then NFP(1) = 0 and filtration stops before the end of the capillary. The integration is truncated, and SNGFR becomes independent of Kf (a hallmark prediction of the model, confirmed experimentally by micropuncture).

---

### Derivation 4: Hydraulic Conductivity from Kf and Surface Area

**Step 1 — Definition.**
The filtration coefficient Kf is defined as the product of hydraulic conductivity and effective filtration surface area:

$$K_f = L_p \cdot S$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Lp.**

$$L_p = \frac{K_f}{S}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$L_p = \frac{12.5 \text{ ml/min/mmHg}}{1.5 \times 10^4 \text{ cm}^2} = 8.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ ml/min/mmHg/cm}^2$$

Converting to SI (1 ml/min = 1.667 × 10⁻⁸ m³/s; 1 mmHg = 133.32 Pa):

$$L_p = \frac{8.33 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.667 \times 10^{-8}}{133.32} = 1.04 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m·s}^{-1}\text{·Pa}^{-1}$$

This is approximately **1.0 × 10⁻¹³ m·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹**, which is **100–1000× higher** than the Lp of a typical systemic capillary (~10⁻¹⁵ to 10⁻¹⁶ m·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹). This is the physical basis of glomerular hyperfiltration.

---

### Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of GFR to Parameter Changes

| Parameter Changed | Direction of Change | Effect on GFR | Physiological Correlate |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Afferent arteriolar resistance (Ra) | ↑ | ↓ GFR | Sympathetic activation, NSAIDs |
| ↑ Efferent arteriolar resistance (Re) | ↑ | ↑ GFR (transiently) | ACE-inhibitor withdrawal effect; Angiotensin II |
| ↑ PGC | ↑ | ↑ GFR | Afferent dilation |
| ↑ πGC (dehydration, hyperproteinemia) | ↑ | ↓ GFR | Volume depletion |
| ↓ Kf | ↓ | ↓ GFR | FSGS, diabetic nephropathy, aging |
| ↓ Lp | ↓ | ↓ GFR | Podocyte injury, slit diaphragm damage |
| ↓ S (surface area) | ↓ | ↓ GFR | Loss of nephrons (CKD) |
| ↑ Filtration Fraction | ↑ | ↑ πE → ↓ NFP → ↓ GFR | Pre-glomerular constriction |
| ↑ σ (reflection coefficient) | ↑ | ↓ GFR (if πGC > πBS) | Pathological protein leak → reduces gradient |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ACE inhibitors *initially* lower GFR in CKD:** Efferent arteriolar vasodilation reduces PGC. The net effect is a fall in GFR, which is paradoxically renoprotective long-term by reducing intraglomerular hypertension.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
