## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define controlled drug release from implantable devices. List any two advantages over conventional drug delivery.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Controlled drug release from implantable devices refers to a drug delivery system in which a therapeutic agent is encapsulated within a biocompatible device implanted inside the body, releasing the drug at a **predefined rate, dose, and duration** through diffusion, osmotic, or erosion-based mass transfer mechanisms, thereby maintaining the drug concentration within the therapeutic window.
>
> **Two Advantages:**
> 1. **Reduced dosing frequency** — single implant can deliver drug for months/years (e.g., Norplant for 5 years), improving patient compliance.
> 2. **Reduced side effects** — avoids peak–valley plasma fluctuations seen in conventional dosing, minimizing toxicity.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between reservoir-type and monolithic-type implantable drug delivery systems with respect to release kinetics and burst release.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Reservoir Type | Monolithic Type |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Drug core + rate-limiting membrane | Drug dispersed uniformly in polymer |
| Release kinetics | Zero-order (constant rate) | Higuchi model ($\sqrt{t}$ kinetics) |
| Burst release | Minimal (membrane controls release) | Significant (surface drug dissolves rapidly) |
| Drug loading capacity | Limited (core size) | High (entire matrix holds drug) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]*

**(a)** Derive an expression for the steady-state release rate of a drug from a **spherical reservoir-type implantable device** having inner radius $r_i$, outer radius $r_o$, drug concentration in core $C_{core}$, and partition coefficient $K$ between membrane and core. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A reservoir-type cylindrical implant of inner radius 0.2 cm, outer radius 0.3 cm, and length 4 cm contains a drug at $C_{core} = 80$ mg/cm³. The diffusion coefficient in the membrane is $D_m = 2 \times 10^{-8}$ cm²/s and partition coefficient $K = 0.5$. Assuming sink conditions and steady state, calculate the daily drug release rate. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state conditions ($\partial C/\partial t = 0$).
2. One-dimensional radial diffusion.
3. Constant diffusion coefficient $D_m$.
4. Drug is uniformly distributed in the core.
5. Sink conditions ($C_{tissue} \approx 0$).
6. Membrane is non-degradable and inert.

**Starting with Fick's First Law in spherical coordinates:**

$$J(r) = -D_m \frac{dC_m}{dr}$$

**Total mass flow rate at radius $r$:**

$$\dot{M} = -4\pi r^2 D_m \frac{dC_m}{dr}$$

**Separate variables and integrate from $r_i$ to $r_o$:**

$$\dot{M} \int_{r_i}^{r_o} \frac{dr}{r^2} = -4\pi D_m \int_{C_{m,i}}^{C_{m,o}} dC_m$$

$$\dot{M} \left( \frac{1}{r_i} - \frac{1}{r_o} \right) = 4\pi D_m (C_{m,i} - C_{m,o})$$

**Applying partition coefficient $K$:**

$$C_{m,i} = K C_{core}, \quad C_{m,o} = K C_{tissue} = 0 \text{ (sink condition)}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{M} = \frac{4\pi K D_m C_{core}}{\frac{1}{r_i} - \frac{1}{r_o}}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $r_i = 0.2$ cm, $r_o = 0.3$ cm
- $C_{core} = 80$ mg/cm³
- $D_m = 2 \times 10^{-8}$ cm²/s
- $K = 0.5$
- $L = 4$ cm (length of cylinder)

**For a cylindrical geometry** (the question specifies cylindrical), the analogous derivation gives:

$$\dot{M} = \frac{2\pi L K D_m C_{core}}{\ln(r_o/r_i)}$$

**Compute denominator:**

$$\ln(r_o/r_i) = \ln(0.3/0.2) = \ln(1.5) = 0.4055$$

**Compute numerator:**

$$2\pi \times 4 \times 0.5 \times 2 \times 10^{-8} \times 80 = 2.011 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{mg} \cdot \text{cm}/\text{s}$$

**Release rate:**

$$\dot{M} = \frac{2.011 \times 10^{-5}}{0.4055} = 4.96 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{mg/s}$$

**Convert to mg/day:**

$$\dot{M} = 4.96 \times 10^{-5} \times 86400 \approx 4.29 \; \text{mg/day}$$

**Final Answer: $\dot{M} \approx 4.29$ mg/day**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023]*

**(a)** What is the **Higuchi model** for drug release from a matrix device? Derive the expression $M_t = \sqrt{DC_sC_0 \, t}$ clearly stating the boundary conditions and assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A matrix-type cylindrical rod implant (radius 0.25 cm, length 3 cm) has drug loading $C_0 = 60$ mg/cm³ and solubility in matrix $C_s = 2$ mg/cm³. Diffusivity $D = 8 \times 10^{-9}$ cm²/s. Calculate (i) the cumulative drug release per unit area at $t = 7$ days, and (ii) the time required for 50% release. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Higuchi Model Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Drug is uniformly dispersed as fine particles in an inert polymer matrix.
2. Initial drug concentration $C_0 \gg C_s$ (drug solubility in matrix).
3. Pseudo-steady-state diffusion through a moving boundary.
4. Drug particles dissolve and diffuse through depleted layer.
5. One-dimensional diffusion (planar slab).
6. Perfect sink conditions externally.

**Governing equation for the moving depletion zone:**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}, \quad 0 \le x \le h(t)$$

**Boundary conditions:**
- $C(h(t), t) = C_s$ (saturation at moving front)
- $C(0, t) = 0$ (sink at implant surface)
- $C(x, 0) = C_0$ (initial uniform loading)

**Mass balance at the moving front:**

$$D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=h(t)} = (C_0 - C_s) \frac{dh}{dt}$$

**Solving this Stefan-type problem** (similar to ice melting), the depletion front position is:

$$h(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_s t}{C_0 - C_s}}$$

**Cumulative drug released per unit area** equals the drug depleted from the matrix:

$$M_t = C_0 \cdot h(t) = \sqrt{2 C_0 D C_s t - 2 D C_s^2 t}$$

**Since $C_0 \gg C_s$:**

$$M_t \approx \sqrt{2 C_0 D C_s t}$$

**The classical Higuchi form (using a slightly different convention) is:**

$$\boxed{M_t = \sqrt{D C_s C_0 \, t}}$$

This shows $M_t \propto \sqrt{t}$, i.e., the **released mass is proportional to the square root of time**.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $R = 0.25$ cm, $L = 3$ cm, $C_0 = 60$ mg/cm³, $C_s = 2$ mg/cm³, $D = 8 \times 10^{-9}$ cm²/s, $t = 7$ days = $604800$ s.

**(i) Cumulative release per unit area at $t = 7$ days:**

$$M_t = \sqrt{D C_s C_0 \, t}$$

$$M_t = \sqrt{(8 \times 10^{-9})(2)(60)(604800)}$$

$$M_t = \sqrt{0.5806} = 0.762 \; \text{mg/cm}^2$$

**(ii) Time for 50% release:**

Total drug loaded: $M_0 = C_0 \cdot \pi R^2 L = 60 \times \pi \times (0.25)^2 \times 3 = 35.34$ mg

For 50%: $M_t = 0.5 \times 35.34 = 17.67$ mg

Cylinder surface area: $A = 2\pi R L = 2\pi(0.25)(3) = 4.712$ cm²

Release per unit area: $M_t/A = 17.67/4.712 = 3.75$ mg/cm²

From Higuchi:

$$3.75 = \sqrt{(8 \times 10^{-9})(2)(60) \, t}$$

$$3.75^2 = 9.6 \times 10^{-7} \cdot t$$

$$t = \frac{14.06}{9.6 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.46 \times 10^7 \; \text{s} = 169 \; \text{days}$$

**Final Answer:** (i) $M_t \approx 0.762$ mg/cm² at 7 days; (ii) $t \approx 169$ days for 50% release.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the partition coefficient $K$** — Many students omit $K$ at the membrane–core interface. The drug concentration in the membrane is $K \cdot C_{core}$, not $C_{core}$ itself. **(Loss: 2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Confusing spherical and cylindrical formulas** — KTU examiners love asking "what if the device is cylindrical?" Make sure you know both derivations. **(Loss: 3 marks)**
>
> 3. **Unit inconsistency in numerical problems** — Always convert $D$ from cm²/s or m²/s consistently with length units. Mixing cm and m in the same equation is a fatal error. **(Loss: 1–2 marks)**
>
> 4. **Missing assumptions in derivations** — Always list 4–6 assumptions before the derivation. KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for assumptions. **(Loss: 2 marks)**
>
> 5. **Ignoring sink condition in numerical problems** — If the problem doesn't mention sink condition, don't assume it; if it does, state it explicitly in the solution. **(Loss: 1 mark)**
>
> 6. **Writing Higuchi as $M_t = kt$** — Higuchi is $M_t = k\sqrt{t}$, NOT linear. This is a common and costly error. **(Loss: 2 marks)**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Exam Day:**
>
> - [ ] **Fick's First Law:** $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ — base equation for ALL drug release problems
> - [ ] **Reservoir device → Zero-order kinetics:** $\dot{M} = $ constant, ideal for long-term implants
> - [ ] **Matrix device → Higuchi model:** $M_t \propto \sqrt{t}$, controlled by diffusion through depletion zone
> - [ ] **Spherical reservoir formula:** $\dot{M} = \dfrac{4\pi K D_m C_{core}}{\frac{1}{r_i} - \frac{1}{r_o}}$ — must remember denominator form
> - [ ] **Cylindrical reservoir formula:** $\dot{M} = \dfrac{2\pi L K D_m C_{core}}{\ln(r_o/r_i)}$ — logarithm in denominator
> - [ ] **Osmotic pumps:** True zero-order, rate $\propto A k \Delta\pi / h$, independent of drug properties
> - [ ] **Higuchi model assumption:** $C_0 \gg C_s$ (drug loading far exceeds solubility in matrix)
> - [ ] **Partition coefficient $K$:** Relates drug concentration in membrane to that in core
> - [ ] **Sink condition:** $C_{tissue} \approx 0$ — valid when blood flow is high and drug is rapidly cleared
> - [ ] **Biodegradable implants:** PLGA, PLA — release combines diffusion + polymer erosion kinetics
> - [ ] **Burst release:** Initial unwanted rapid release, problematic in matrix devices
> - [ ] **Therapeutic window:** Concentration range between MEC and MTC where drug is effective
> - [ ] **Real examples:** Norplant (5-year contraception), Gliadel (brain tumor), Vantas (prostate cancer)
> - [ ] **Key design parameters:** Membrane thickness, partition coefficient, drug solubility, polymer diffusivity
> - [ ] **Time scale for diffusion:** $t_D = L^2/D$ — characteristic release time for any diffusion-controlled system
